topics.title: Iteration Method, Recursion Tree Method, Substitution Method and Master's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Iteration Method, Recursion Tree Method, Substitution Method and Master's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Recurrence Relation?

A **recurrence relation** is a mathematical equation that defines a sequence of values $T(n)$ in terms of one or more preceding values of the same sequence, typically applied to the running time of a recursive algorithm. For divide-and-conquer algorithms, it is the standard tool used to capture the cost of the recursive call structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCST502 / Module 1):** "A recurrence is an equation or inequality that describes a function in terms of its value on smaller inputs. Solving a recurrence means obtaining a closed-form (asymptotic) bound on $T(n)$."

The general form of a divide-and-conquer recurrence is:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where:
- $a$ = number of sub-problems generated
- $b$ = factor by which the sub-problem size shrinks
- $f(n)$ = cost of the **divide** + **conquer/combine** work done outside the recursive calls
- $T(1)$ or $T(c)$ = base case (constant cost)

### 1.2 The Four Standard Methods — Intuitive Overview

| Method | Core Idea | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Iteration Method** | Repeatedly unfold the recurrence like a Russian nesting doll until a pattern is visible, then sum the resulting arithmetic/geometric series. | Calculating the total weight of a Russian nesting doll by repeatedly opening each layer and adding its weight. |
| **Recursion Tree Method** | Visualize each recursive call as a node in a tree, annotate the work per node, then sum work level-by-level from root to leaves. | Estimating the total population of a family tree by multiplying the per-generation count by the size of each generation. |
| **Substitution Method** | Guess the asymptotic form (educated guess), then rigorously **prove** it by mathematical induction. | A detective who first hypothesises the culprit, then systematically confirms every detail of the story. |
| **Master's Theorem** | A direct cookbook-style theorem that maps the recurrence to one of three cases by comparing $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$. | A pre-computed tax-slab chart — you simply look up which bracket your income falls into. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Out of 14 marks, Module 1 almost always features a recurrence-solving question (8–10 marks) where students must select the most efficient method, derive the closed form, and state the asymptotic complexity in Big-O.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method 1 — The Iteration Method (Unfolding Method)

**Conceptual Foundation:** A recurrence is a recursive definition. The iteration method simply replaces $T(n/k)$ repeatedly with its own definition, generating a series expansion. The work done at each level forms a sequence, which is then summed.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Write the recurrence and identify $a$, $b$, and $f(n)$.
2. Substitute $T(n/b)$ with its own definition, generating a sum.
3. After $k$ expansions, detect a pattern in the coefficients of $f$ and the recursion depth.
4. Stop when the argument of $T$ becomes a base-case constant.
5. Sum the resulting geometric / arithmetic series to obtain a closed form.
6. Apply asymptotic notation.

**Termination Rule (Depth of Iteration):** Recursion depth $k$ is the smallest integer such that

$$\frac{n}{b^k} = c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \log_b\!\left(\frac{n}{c}\right)$$

For Big-O purposes, this is $\Theta(\log_b n)$.

### 2.2 Method 2 — The Recursion Tree Method

**Conceptual Foundation:** A recursion tree is a rooted tree where the root represents the original problem, its children represent the sub-problems generated by the recurrence, and so on. Each node is labelled with the non-recursive cost $f(\text{size})$ of that sub-problem.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Draw a tree with $a$ children at every internal node, each labelled with size $n/b^i$ at depth $i$.
2. Annotate the **per-node work** $f(n/b^i)$ at each level.
3. Compute the **per-level work** = (number of nodes) × (work per node).
4. Determine the **number of levels** until leaves (depth $\log_b n$).
5. Sum the per-level work over all levels to get the total cost.
6. Bound the sum with Big-O.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When the per-level work forms a **decreasing geometric series** (e.g., Merge Sort), the total is dominated by the **root level** — a fact that quickly gives $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

### 2.3 Method 3 — The Substitution Method (Guess + Inductive Proof)

**Conceptual Foundation:** The method relies on a sound initial guess (often obtained from the recursion tree or intuition) and a formal **proof by mathematical induction** that the guess is correct.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Guess** the closed-form solution: e.g., $T(n) \le c n^2$.
2. Substitute this guess into the recurrence and simplify.
3. **Inductive Step:** Assume $T(k) \le c k^2$ holds for all $k < n$, then prove $T(n) \le c n^2$.
4. Choose the constant $c$ large enough so that the inequality holds.
5. Verify the **Base Case** for small $n$.

**Pitfall to Avoid:** Substitution sometimes gives a **loose** bound. The trick is to subtract a **lower-order term** from the guess (e.g., guess $T(n) \le c n^2 - d n$ instead of $c n^2$) to absorb the additive constants in the inductive step.

### 2.4 Method 4 — Master's Theorem (The Direct Cookbook)

**Conceptual Foundation:** For recurrences of the form $T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n)$, the total work is governed by the comparison between $f(n)$ and the critical exponent $n^{\log_b a}$. The polynomial comparison test $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ determines which of three regimes dominates.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Method | Key Formula / Rule | When to Use | Complexity Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Iteration | $\sum_{i=0}^{\log_b n - 1} a^i f(n/b^i)$ | Any recurrence, especially when the sum is geometric | $T(n) = \Theta(\cdot)$ |
| Recursion Tree | Total $= \sum_{i=0}^{L} (\text{nodes at level } i) \cdot f(n/b^i)$ | When geometric intuition is needed; not just plug-and-play | $T(n) = \Theta(\cdot)$ |
| Substitution | $T(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$; prove by induction | Proving a guessed bound; lower-bound proofs | $O, \Omega, \Theta$ |
| Master Theorem | Compare $f(n)$ vs $n^{\log_b a}$ | Any $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \ge 1, b > 1$ | Direct lookup |

### 2.6 Master's Theorem — The Three Cases (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical exponent:** Let $c_{crit} = \log_b a$. Then the comparison is between $f(n)$ and $n^{c_{crit}}$.

| Case | Condition on $f(n)$ | Dominant Term | Solution $T(n) = $ | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $f(n) = O(n^{c_{crit} - \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ | Leaves (recursive work) | $\Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | Sub-problems dominate; combine cost is negligible. |
| **2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^k n)$ for $k \ge 0$ | All levels roughly equal | $\Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n)$ | Combine cost matches recursion depth. |
| **3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{c_{crit} + \varepsilon})$ and regularity: $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$ for $c < 1$ | Root (combine cost) | $\Theta(f(n))$ | Divide-and-combine dominates; recursion is "free." |

**Extended Case 2 (with $k = -1$):** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} / \log n)$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log \log n)$. (Sometimes called Case 2b.)

### 2.7 Standard Reference Recurrences (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Algorithm | Recurrence | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| Binary Search | $T(n) = T(n/2) + \Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Merge Sort | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| Karatsuba Multiplication | $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$ |
| Strassen's Matrix Multiplication | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$ |
| Quick Sort (worst) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Tower of Hanoi | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ | $\Theta(2^n)$ |
| T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n² | $T(n) = ?$ | See worked example below |
| T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n² | $T(n) = ?$ | See worked example below |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Worked Example: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, $T(1) = 1$ (Merge Sort)

We will solve this same recurrence using **all four methods** so you can see the equivalence.

---

#### 3.1.1 Solution via the **Iteration Method**

**Step 1:** Write the original recurrence.

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$$

**Step 2:** Expand $T(n/2)$ using the same recurrence.

$$T(n) = 2\left[2T(n/4) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n = 4T(n/4) + n + n$$

**Step 3:** Expand $T(n/4)$.

$$T(n) = 4\left[2T(n/8) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + 2n = 8T(n/8) + n + 2n$$

**Step 4:** After $i$ iterations, detect the pattern.

$$T(n) = 2^i T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^i}\right) + i \cdot n$$

**Step 5:** Continue until the argument becomes constant. Set $n/2^i = 1$, so $2^i = n$, giving $i = \log_2 n$.

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + n \log_2 n = n \cdot 1 + n \log_2 n$$

**Step 6:** Closed form.

$$\boxed{T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

---

#### 3.1.2 Solution via the **Recursion Tree Method**

**Step 1:** Draw a tree. The root has cost $n$. It spawns 2 sub-problems, each of size $n/2$, contributing $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$ at level 1.

**Step 2:** At level $i$, there are $2^i$ nodes, each of size $n/2^i$, so per-node cost is $n/2^i$.

**Step 3:** Per-level cost:

$$W_i = (\text{nodes}) \times (\text{work/node}) = 2^i \cdot \frac{n}{2^i} = n$$

**Step 4:** The work is **constant $n$ at every level**, from $i = 0$ to $i = \log_2 n - 1$. Number of levels $L = \log_2 n + 1$ (including leaves, which contribute $T(1) = 1$ each, but this is $O(n)$ overall).

**Step 5:** Total work:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n + \Theta(n) = n \log_2 n + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

---

#### 3.1.3 Solution via the **Substitution Method**

**Step 1:** Guess that $T(n) \le c n \log_2 n$ for some constant $c > 0$.

**Step 2:** Inductive hypothesis: assume $T(n/2) \le c \cdot (n/2) \cdot \log_2(n/2)$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the recurrence.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + n \\
     &\le 2 \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2} \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \cdot \left(\log_2 n - 1\right) + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - c n + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n \quad \text{(provided } c \ge 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** With $c \ge 1$ and a suitable base case, the induction holds.

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n \log n)}$$

A matching lower bound $T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$ can be shown similarly, giving $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

#### 3.1.4 Solution via the **Master's Theorem**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2:** Compute critical exponent.

$$c_{crit} = \log_b a = \log_2 2 = 1$$

So $n^{c_{crit}} = n^1 = n$.

**Step 3:** Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{c_{crit}}$.

$$f(n) = n = \Theta(n^{1} \cdot \log^0 n)$$

This is exactly **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Step 4:** Apply Case 2.

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 2} \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ (Case where $f(n)$ matches)

**Step 1:** Identify: $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.

**Step 2:** Critical exponent.

$$c_{crit} = \log_2 4 = 2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n^{c_{crit}} = n^2$$

**Step 3:** Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^2$. They are **equal polynomially**.

$$f(n) = \Theta(n^2)$$

This matches **Case 2 with $k = 0$**.

**Step 4:** Apply Case 2.

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ (Combine cost dominates)

**Step 1:** Identify: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.

**Step 2:** Critical exponent.

$$c_{crit} = \log_2 2 = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad n^{c_{crit}} = n$$

**Step 3:** Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^1$.

$$f(n) = n^2 = \Omega(n^{1 + \varepsilon}) \quad \text{for } \varepsilon = 1$$

So $f(n)$ is polynomially larger — **Case 3 applies**.

**Step 4:** Verify regularity: $a f(n/b) = 2 (n/2)^2 = n^2 / 2 \le (1/2) n^2 = c f(n)$ with $c = 1/2 < 1$. ✓

**Step 5:** Apply Case 3.

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^2)}$$

**Recursion Tree Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 0:} \quad & n^2 \\
\text{Level 1:} \quad & 2 \cdot (n/2)^2 = n^2/2 \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & 4 \cdot (n/4)^2 = n^2/4 \\
\text{Level } i: \quad & n^2 \cdot (1/2)^i
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of the geometric series:

$$T(n) = n^2 \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^i + \Theta(n) = n^2 \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^{\log_2 n}}{1 - 1/2} = \Theta(n^2)$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ (Unbalanced Recursion)

This recurrence **does not fit the Master's Theorem** because the sub-problems are of different sizes. We must use the recursion tree.

**Step 1:** The longest path from root to leaf has length $\log_{3/2} n$ (each level keeps the heaviest branch, size $2n/3$).

**Step 2:** Per-level work: at each level, the work is at most $n$ (total work at every level is bounded by $n$).

**Step 3:** Number of levels: $\log_{3/2} n$. Leaves contribute $O(n)$ total.

**Step 4:** Total.

$$T(n) = n \cdot \log_{3/2} n + O(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** Whenever a recurrence has **asymmetric** sub-problem sizes (e.g., $T(n/3) + T(2n/3)$), the Master's Theorem **cannot be applied directly**. Use the recursion tree method.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ (Case 2 with $k = 1$)

**Step 1:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $c_{crit} = 1$, $n^{c_{crit}} = n$.

**Step 2:** $f(n) = n \log n = \Theta(n^1 \cdot \log^1 n)$. This is **Case 2 with $k = 1$**.

**Step 3:** Apply Case 2.

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log^{1+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)}$$

**Recursion Tree Cross-Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 0:} \quad & n \log n \\
\text{Level 1:} \quad & 2 \cdot \frac{n}{2} \log\!\frac{n}{2} = n (\log n - 1) \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & 4 \cdot \frac{n}{4} \log\!\frac{n}{4} = n (\log n - 2) \\
\vdots \quad & \vdots \\
\text{Level } i: \quad & n (\log n - i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n(\log n - i) + \Theta(n) = n \sum_{j=1}^{\log n} j = n \cdot \frac{\log n (\log n + 1)}{2} = \Theta(n \log^2 n) \;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.6 Recurrence Classification Cheat-Table (For Quick Exam Recall)

| Recurrence | $a, b$ | $n^{\log_b a}$ | $f(n)$ | Case | Solution |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $2T(n/2) + 1$ | 2, 2 | $n$ | $1$ | Case 1 | $\Theta(n)$ |
| $4T(n/2) + n$ | 4, 2 | $n^2$ | $n$ | Case 1 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $2T(n/2) + n$ | 2, 2 | $n$ | $n$ | Case 2, $k=0$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $4T(n/2) + n^2$ | 4, 2 | $n^2$ | $n^2$ | Case 2, $k=0$ | $\Theta(n^2 \log n)$ |
| $2T(n/2) + n^2$ | 2, 2 | $n$ | $n^2$ | Case 3 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $2T(n/2) + n \log n$ | 2, 2 | $n$ | $n \log n$ | Case 2, $k=1$ | $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ |
| $T(n/3) + n$ | 1, 3 | $n^0$ | $n$ | Case 3 | $\Theta(n)$ |
| $3T(n/4) + n \log n$ | 3, 4 | $n^{\log_4 3}$ | $n \log n$ | Case 3 (f larger) | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$

This Mermaid diagram shows the canonical Merge Sort recursion tree, where every level contributes exactly $n$ work.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0["LEVEL 0: ROOT - Work n - 1 node x n = n"]
    L1a["LEVEL 1 - LEFT - Work n/2 - 2 nodes x n/2 = n"]
    L1b["LEVEL 1 - RIGHT - Work n/2 - 2 nodes x n/2 = n"]
    L2a["LEVEL 2 - LL - Work n/4 - 4 nodes x n/4 = n"]
    L2b["LEVEL 2 - LR - Work n/4 - 4 nodes x n/4 = n"]
    L2c["LEVEL 2 - RL - Work n/4 - 4 nodes x n/4 = n"]
    L2d["LEVEL 2 - RR - Work n/4 - 4 nodes x n/4 = n"]
    LEAF["LEVEL 3 - LEAVES - Work O(1) - n leaves x O(1) = O(n)"]

    L0 --> L1a
    L0 --> L1b
    L1a --> L2a
    L1a --> L2b
    L1b --> L2c
    L1b --> L2d
    L2a --> LEAF
    L2b --> LEAF
    L2c --> LEAF
    L2d --> LEAF

    style L0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style L1a fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    style L1b fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    style L2a fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style L2b fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style L2c fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style L2d fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    style LEAF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
```

**Reading the Tree:** Total cost = sum of per-level work

$$T(n) = \underbrace{n}_{\text{L0}} + \underbrace{n}_{\text{L1}} + \underbrace{n}_{\text{L2}} + \cdots + \underbrace{O(n)}_{\text{leaves}} = n \log_2 n + O(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

---

### 4.2 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ (Case 3 — Combine Dominates)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0["LEVEL 0: ROOT - Work n^2 - 1 node x n^2 = n^2"]
    R1a["LEVEL 1 - LEFT - Work n^2/4 - 2 nodes x n^2/4 = n^2/2"]
    R1b["LEVEL 1 - RIGHT - Work n^2/4 - 2 nodes x n^2/4 = n^2/2"]
    R2a["LEVEL 2 - LL - Work n^2/16 - 4 nodes x n^2/16 = n^2/4"]
    R2b["LEVEL 2 - LR - Work n^2/16 - 4 nodes x n^2/16 = n^2/4"]
    R2c["LEVEL 2 - RL - Work n^2/16 - 4 nodes x n^2/16 = n^2/4"]
    R2d["LEVEL 2 - RR - Work n^2/16 - 4 nodes x n^2/16 = n^2/4"]
    RLEAF["LEVEL log n - LEAVES - Total work O(n) - often negligible"]

    R0 --> R1a
    R0 --> R1b
    R1a --> R2a
    R1a --> R2b
    R1b --> R2c
    R1b --> R2d
    R2a --> RLEAF
    R2b --> RLEAF
    R2c --> RLEAF
    R2d --> RLEAF

    style R0 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    style R1a fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    style R1b fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000
    style R2a fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style R2b fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style R2c fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style R2d fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    style RLEAF fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#808080,color:#000
```

**Reading the Tree:** Work decreases geometrically by factor $1/2$ at every level. Total:

$$T(n) = n^2 \left(1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \cdots\right) = n^2 \cdot 2 = \Theta(n^2)$$

---

### 4.3 Method Selection Flowchart — Which Method Should I Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given a Recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)"]
    Q1{"Standard form with constants a >= 1, b > 1?"}
    Q2{"f(n) vs n^log_b a is polynomially comparable?"}
    Q3{"f(n) involves log factors or unusual growth?"}
    Q4{"Need rigorous proof of guessed bound?"}

    USE_MT["Use MASTER THEOREM - Direct lookup - State a, b, c_crit, case"]
    USE_TREE["Use RECURSION TREE - Draw tree, sum per-level - Use for asymmetric or unknown forms"]
    USE_ITER["Use ITERATION METHOD - Unfold and sum the series - Use for clean geometric sums"]
    USE_SUB["Use SUBSTITUTION METHOD - Guess T(n) <= c g(n) - Prove by induction"]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2
    Q1 -- NO --> USE_TREE
    Q2 -- YES --> USE_MT
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3
    Q3 -- YES --> USE_TREE
    Q3 -- NO --> USE_ITER
    USE_MT --> Q4
    Q4 -- YES --> USE_SUB
    Q4 -- NO --> DONE["Final Asymptotic Answer"]
    USE_TREE --> DONE
    USE_ITER --> DONE
    USE_SUB --> DONE

    style START fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style USE_MT fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style USE_TREE fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style USE_ITER fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style USE_SUB fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style DONE fill:#9013FE,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

---

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Master's Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Compute c_crit = log_b a"]
    B["Step 2: Form n^c_crit"]
    C["Is f n polynomially smaller than n^c_crit?"]
    D["Is f n polynomially equal to n^c_crit times log^k n?"]
    E["Is f n polynomially larger than n^c_crit and regularity holds?"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- YES --> CASE1["CASE 1: T n = Theta n^log_b a"]
    C -- NO --> D
    D -- YES --> CASE2["CASE 2: T n = Theta n^log_b a log^k plus 1 n"]
    D -- NO --> E
    E -- YES --> CASE3["CASE 3: T n = Theta f n"]
    E -- NO --> GAP["GAP: Master Theorem does NOT apply - Use Substitution or Recursion Tree"]

    style CASE1 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CASE2 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CASE3 fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style GAP fill:#D0021B,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a recurrence relation. With an example, explain how the iteration method is used to solve a recurrence.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **recurrence relation** is an equation that defines the value of a function $T(n)$ for input size $n$ in terms of its values for smaller inputs. It is widely used to express the running time of recursive algorithms.

In the **iteration method**, we repeatedly substitute the recurrence into itself to obtain an expansion. For example, for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + n \\
     &= 2\big[2T(n/4) + n/2\big] + n \\
     &= 4T(n/4) + 2n \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= 2^k T(n/2^k) + kn
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n/2^k = 1$, i.e. $k = \log_2 n$, we get $T(n) = n + n \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the Master's Theorem. What are its three cases?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a recurrence of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \ge 1$, $b > 1$, let $c_{crit} = \log_b a$. The three cases are:

1. **Case 1 — Leaves dominate:** If $f(n) = O(n^{c_{crit} - \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.

2. **Case 2 — All levels equal:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^k n)$ for some $k \ge 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n)$.

3. **Case 3 — Root dominates:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{c_{crit} + \varepsilon})$ and the regularity condition $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$ holds for some $c < 1$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Method Comparison + Asymptotic Solution*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 1 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the **recursion tree method**. Show the per-level work and obtain the closed-form solution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ using the **Master's Theorem**. Identify the case and state the final asymptotic complexity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2 — Draw recursion tree.** At each level $i$:
- Number of sub-problems: $2^i$
- Size of each sub-problem: $n/2^i$
- Work per node: $n/2^i$

**Step 3 — Per-level work.**

$$W_i = 2^i \cdot \frac{n}{2^i} = n$$

**Step 4 — Number of levels.** Recursion stops when $n/2^i = 1$, i.e. $i = \log_2 n$. There are $\log_2 n + 1$ levels (including leaves); leaves contribute $O(n)$ total work.

**Step 5 — Sum.**

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n + O(n) = n \log_2 n + O(n)$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:** $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.

**Step 2 — Critical exponent.**

$$c_{crit} = \log_2 4 = 2$$

**Step 3 — Form $n^{c_{crit}} = n^2$.**

**Step 4 — Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^2$.**

$$f(n) = n^2 = \Theta(n^2 \cdot \log^0 n)$$

This matches **Case 2 with $k = 0$**.

**Step 5 — Apply Case 2.**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 4} \cdot \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$$

**Step 6 — Verify with recursion tree (optional cross-check).**
- Level 0: $n^2$
- Level 1: $4 \cdot (n/2)^2 = n^2$
- Level $i$: $n^2$
- Total: $n^2 \cdot \log_2 n = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$ ✓

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Mixed Recurrence Analysis*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 1 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ using **any two methods** of your choice and show that they give the same asymptotic result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using the **Master's Theorem** and verify your answer using the **recursion tree method**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Method 1 — Master's Theorem:**

- $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $c_{crit} = \log_2 2 = 1$.
- $n^{c_{crit}} = n$.
- $f(n) = n^2 = \Omega(n^{1+1})$, so $f(n)$ is polynomially larger.
- **Regularity check:** $a f(n/b) = 2(n/2)^2 = n^2/2 \le (1/2) n^2 = c f(n)$, with $c = 1/2 < 1$. ✓
- Apply **Case 3**: $T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) = \Theta(n^2)$. **(3.5 marks)**

**Method 2 — Recursion Tree:**

- Level 0: $n^2$
- Level 1: $2(n/2)^2 = n^2/2$
- Level 2: $4(n/4)^2 = n^2/4$
- Level $i$: $n^2 \cdot (1/2)^i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n^2 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^i + \Theta(n) \\
     &= n^2 \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^{\log_2 n}}{1 - 1/2} + O(n) \\
     &= 2n^2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) + O(n) \\
     &= 2n^2 - 2n + O(n) \\
     &= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods agree: $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. **(3.5 marks)**

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Master's Theorem Approach:**

- $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $c_{crit} = \log_2 2 = 1$.
- $n^{c_{crit}} = n$.
- $f(n) = n \log n = \Theta(n \cdot \log^1 n)$, which matches **Case 2 with $k = 1$**.

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 2} \cdot \log^{1+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n) \quad \text{(3.5 marks)}$$

**Recursion Tree Verification:**

- Level 0: $n \log n$
- Level 1: $2 \cdot (n/2) \log(n/2) = n(\log n - 1)$
- Level 2: $4 \cdot (n/4) \log(n/4) = n(\log n - 2)$
- Level $i$: $n(\log n - i)$

Sum over $i = 0$ to $\log_2 n - 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= n \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} (\log n - i) + \Theta(n) \\
     &= n \left[ \log n + (\log n - 1) + (\log n - 2) + \cdots + 1 \right] + \Theta(n) \\
     &= n \cdot \frac{\log n (\log n + 1)}{2} + \Theta(n) \\
     &= \Theta(n \log^2 n) \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods confirm: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)$. **(3.5 marks)**

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Cases 1 and 3:** Remember, Case 1 means **leaves dominate** (recursive calls are expensive), while Case 3 means **root dominates** (combine cost is expensive). The decision rests entirely on whether $f(n) < n^{\log_b a}$ or $f(n) > n^{\log_b a}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the regularity condition in Case 3:** If $a f(n/b) \le c f(n)$ fails, the Master Theorem **cannot be applied** — you must fall back to the recursion tree or substitution method.
> 3. **Off-by-one in recursion depth:** Always stop at $n/b^k = 1$ (or the smallest base case), not $n/b^k = 0$.
> 4. **In substitution, forgetting to handle the base case:** A proof by induction is incomplete without verifying $T(1)$ or $T(2)$ explicitly.
> 5. **Saying "$\log n$" instead of "$\log^2 n$" in Case 2 with $k = 1$:** The exponent on the log increases by 1, not stays the same.
> 6. **Applying Master's Theorem to asymmetric recurrences like $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$:** It is **not** of the form $aT(n/b)$; use the recursion tree.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Solving Recurrences**

- [ ] **Recurrence** = recursive equation defining $T(n)$ in terms of smaller inputs.
- [ ] **General form** of a divide-and-conquer recurrence: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, with $a \ge 1$ and $b > 1$.
- [ ] **Iteration method** = expand + detect pattern + sum series; works for any recurrence.
- [ ] **Recursion tree method** = draw tree, compute per-level work, sum level-by-level; ideal for visualisation and asymmetric recurrences.
- [ ] **Substitution method** = guess + rigorous inductive proof; used when the answer is suspected but not obvious.
- [ ] **Master's Theorem** = direct cookbook for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$; compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.
- [ ] **Case 1:** $f(n) = O(n^{c_{crit} - \varepsilon})$ $\Rightarrow$ $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ — leaves dominate.
- [ ] **Case 2:** $f(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^k n)$ $\Rightarrow$ $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n)$ — balanced levels.
- [ ] **Case 3:** $f(n) = \Omega(n^{c_{crit} + \varepsilon})$ + regularity $\Rightarrow$ $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ — root dominates.
- [ ] **Critical exponent** $c_{crit} = \log_b a$ is the single most important quantity in recurrence analysis.
- [ ] **Master's Theorem does not apply** if $f(n)$ is not polynomially comparable to $n^{\log_b a}$, or if $a < 1$, $b \le 1$, or sub-problems are of unequal size.
- [ ] **Subtraction trick** in substitution: use $T(n) \le c g(n) - d \cdot h(n)$ when the induction almost works but the algebra fails by a constant.
- [ ] **Memorize the canonical recurrences:** Binary Search ($\Theta(\log n)$), Merge Sort ($\Theta(n \log n)$), Karatsuba ($\Theta(n^{1.585})$), Strassen ($\Theta(n^{2.807})$), Tower of Hanoi ($\Theta(2^n)$).
- [ ] **Always state the asymptotic answer in Big-O, Big-$\Omega$, or Big-$\Theta$** as required by the question — partial marks are awarded for the correct form.
- [ ] **Always verify regularity** before declaring Case 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **AVL Tree** (named after its inventors **Adelson-Velsky and Landis**, 1962) is a **self-balancing Binary Search Tree (BST)** in which, for every node, the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees — known as the **Balance Factor (BF)** — is restricted to the set $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. Formally, for any node $N$ in an AVL tree: $$BF(N) = h_L - h_R \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$$ where $h_L$ and $h_R$ denote the heights of the left and right subtrees of $N$ respectively. If at any node the balance factor leaves the set $\{-1, 0, +1\}$, the tree is said to be **imbalanced**, and a **rotation** is triggered to restore the AVL property. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AVL Trees are classified under *Balanced Search Trees* in Module 1 of PCCST502. The examiner expects the student to demonstrate proficiency in **insertion with rotations**, **deletion with rotations**, and **complexity analysis** under OBE standards. > [!NOTE] > **Heuristic Distinction:** A normal BST guarantees $O(\log n)$ search only when it is "bushy." After skewed insertions (e.g., 1, 2, 3, 4, 5), a BST degenerates into a linked list ($O(n)$). AVL trees are an *engineering guarantee* against this degeneration. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tilted Bookshelf" Imagine a librarian maintaining a **binary search bookshelf** where every new book must be inserted in sorted order. If the librarian keeps stacking books on one side, the shelf eventually tilts dangerously and may collapse (i.e., search cost balloons to $O(n)$). The AVL "rule" is analogous to a **digital spirit level attached to every shelf-divider**: after each insertion, the librarian checks whether the tilt (balance factor) exceeds ±1 cm. If it does, the librarian **rearranges** a small cluster of books (a *rotation*) to bring the system back to level. This rearrangement is local — affecting only $O(1)$ nodes — and guarantees that the entire shelf is never taller than $1.44 \cdot \log_2(n+2)$ levels. --- ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | KTU Standard Notation | |------|---------|------------------------| | **Balance Factor (BF)** | $h_L - h_R$ | Integer in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ | | **Height of tree** | Longest path from root to leaf | $h$ | | **Imbalanced Node** | Node with $BF \notin \{-1, 0, +1\}$ | Trigger for rotation | | **Rotation** | Local restructuring operation | LL, RR, LR, RL | | **Imbalance Type** | Pattern of insertions causing tilt | Single or Double | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Underlying Invariant — Why Balance Matters The performance contract of an AVL tree rests on one **load-bearing invariant**: *the height $h$ of the tree is bounded by $O(\log n)$*. This converts the worst-case $O(n)$ of a degenerate BST into the worst-case $O(\log n)$. #### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown 1. **Define the height of an empty tree** as $-1$ and the height of a single node as $0$. 2. After every insertion/deletion, walk back from the modified leaf to the root, recomputing heights. 3. At each ancestor, compute the balance factor. 4. **If $|BF| \leq 1$** → no action required, continue walking upwards. 5. **If $|BF| = 2$** → identify the imbalance type and apply the appropriate rotation. 6. The rotation is local; only the subtree rooted at the imbalanced node is restructured. 7. After rotation, the height of the (formerly) imbalanced subtree returns to its pre-insertion height — so no further rotations propagate upward *during insertion* (a critical fact for KTU problems). ### 2.2 The Four Canonical Rotation Types | Rotation Type | Imbalance Pattern | Triggering Insertion Path (from imbalanced node) | Operation | Resulting Pattern | |---------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-----------|------------------| | **LL (Right Rotation)** | Left-Left | Insertion occurred in the **Left subtree of the Left child** | Single right rotation | Re-balanced | | **RR (Left Rotation)** | Right-Right | Insertion occurred in the **Right subtree of the Right child** | Single left rotation | Re-balanced | | **LR** | Left-Right | Insertion occurred in the **Right subtree of the Left child** | Left rotation on left child, then right rotation on root | Re-balanced | | **RL** | Right-Left | Insertion occurred in the **Left subtree of the Right child** | Right rotation on right child, then left rotation on root | Re-balanced | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick for KTU:** The **first letter** of the imbalance type denotes the *direction of the first rotation*, and the **second letter** denotes the *direction of the second rotation*. "LR" → first go **L**eft, then go **R**ight. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Explanation | |---|----------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Balance Factor of node $N$ | $BF(N) = h_L - h_R$ | $h_L, h_R$ = heights of left/right subtrees | | 2 | AVL Invariant | $BF(N) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ | For every node $N$ in the tree | | 3 | Search complexity | $O(\log n)$ | Due to bounded height | | 4 | Insert complexity | $O(\log n)$ | Single rotation may be triggered | | 5 | Delete complexity | $O(\log n)$ | Up to $O(\log n)$ rotations may propagate | | 6 | Space complexity | $O(n)$ | Stores $n$ nodes | | 7 | Maximum height | $h < 1.44 \cdot \log_2(n+2)$ | Worst-case AVL height | | 8 | Minimum nodes for height $h$ | $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$ | Fibonacci-like recurrence with $N(0)=1, N(1)=2$ | | 9 | Lower bound for minimum nodes | $N(h) \geq \phi^{h}$ where $\phi \approx 1.618$ | Implies $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ | | 10 | Max rotations per insertion | $\mathbf{1}$ (single or double) | Critically tested in KTU | | 11 | Max rotations per deletion | $O(\log n)$ | May cascade up to root | > **Engineering Utility:** AVL trees are deployed in database indexing, in-memory look-up tables, network routing tables, and real-time systems where the **worst-case** $O(\log n)$ bound is non-negotiable. They outperform Red-Black trees in *lookup-heavy* workloads because they enforce a stricter balance (shorter trees). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Nodes in an AVL Tree of Height $h$ Let $N(h)$ denote the **minimum** number of nodes in an AVL tree of height $h$. We want a closed-form or recurrence relation that quantifies the sparsest valid AVL tree. **Step 1 — Base Cases.** The smallest AVL tree has one node (height 0) and the next has two nodes (height 1): $$N(0) = 1, \quad N(1) = 2$$ **Step 2 — Recursive Decomposition.** The most "sparse" AVL tree of height $h$ must have: - A root node - A left subtree of height $h-1$ (as sparse as possible) - A right subtree of height $h-2$ (as sparse as possible, since $h_L - h_R$ may equal 1) **Step 3 — Recurrence Formulation.** $$ \begin{aligned} N(h) &= 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Closed Form via Fibonacci.** The recurrence $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$ is shifted Fibonacci. Letting $M(h) = N(h) + 1$, we get $M(h) = M(h-1) + M(h-2)$, a pure Fibonacci recurrence. $$ \begin{aligned} M(h) &= F_{h+2} \\ \Rightarrow N(h) &= F_{h+2} - 1 \end{aligned} $$ where $F_k$ is the $k$-th Fibonacci number. **Step 5 — Asymptotic Bound.** Using Binet's formula $F_k \approx \phi^{k}/\sqrt{5}$ with $\phi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$: $$ \begin{aligned} N(h) \approx \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} - 1 \geq \phi^{h} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Inverting for Height.** Taking logarithms on both sides of $n \geq \phi^{h}$: $$ \begin{aligned} h &\leq \log_{\phi}(n) = \frac{\ln n}{\ln \phi} \\ &= \frac{\ln n}{\ln 1.618} \approx 1.44 \cdot \log_2(n) \end{aligned} $$ This formally proves that AVL tree height is bounded by $1.44 \cdot \log_2(n+2)$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Full AVL Insertion Walkthrough (KTU-Favorite Problem) **Problem [Module-ESE Pattern]:** Insert the keys $\mathbf{10, 20, 30, 25, 27, 5, 15}$ into an initially empty AVL tree. Show the tree after every insertion and identify any rotations. > [!NOTE] > **Strategy:** After each insertion, recompute heights of all ancestors bottom-up. The first node (from the inserted leaf upward) with $|BF|=2$ is the **imbalanced pivot** — the rotation is anchored there. #### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Insertion Trace **Step A — Insert 10.** Single node. No imbalance. **Step B — Insert 20.** BST rule: 20 > 10 → right child. No imbalance. **Step C — Insert 30.** BST rule: 30 > 20 → right child. Now node 10 has $BF = 0 - 1 = -1$ (still valid). Node 20 has $BF = 0 - 1 = -1$. Wait — check node 10's right child 20: its right is 30. At node 20, $h_L - h_R = 0 - 1 = -1$. At node 10, the right subtree has height 1, left is empty (height -1): $BF(10) = -1 - 1 = -2$. **Imbalance!** - Imbalanced pivot: **node 10**. - Path from 10 to inserted 30: Right (20), Right (30) → **RR case**. - Apply **Left Rotation** at node 10. **Tree after RR rotation:** ``` 20 / \ 10 30 ``` **Step D — Insert 25.** 25 < 30, > 20 → goes as left child of 30. At node 30: $h_L=0, h_R=-1$, $BF = 1$ ✓. At node 20: $h_L=-1, h_R=0$, $BF = -1$ ✓. No rotation. **Step E — Insert 27.** Path: 27 < 30, 27 > 25 → right child of 25. At node 25: $h_L=-1, h_R=0$, $BF=-1$ ✓. At node 30: $h_L=1, h_R=-1$, $BF=2$ → **Imbalance!** - Imbalanced pivot: **node 30**. - Path from 30 to 27: Left (25), Right (27) → **LR case**. - Apply **Left rotation on 25**, then **Right rotation on 30**. After left rotation on 25: 27 becomes parent of 25. After right rotation on 30: 27 becomes parent of 30, with 25 as left child of 27. **Tree after LR rotation:** ``` 20 / \ 10 27 / \ 25 30 ``` **Step F — Insert 5.** 5 < 20, 5 < 10 → left child of 10. At node 10: $h_L=0, h_R=-1$, $BF=1$ ✓. At node 20: $h_L=0, h_R=1$, $BF=-1$ ✓. No rotation. **Step G — Insert 15.** Path: 15 < 20, 15 > 10, 15 > ... well, 10 has no right child, so 15 becomes right child of 10. At node 10: $h_L=0, h_R=0$, $BF=0$ ✓. At node 20: $h_L=1, h_R=1$, $BF=0$ ✓. At node 27: $h_L=0, h_R=0$, $BF=0$ ✓. No rotation. **Final AVL Tree:** ``` 20 / \ 10 27 / \ / \ 5 15 25 30 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification:** The balance factors of all nodes in the final tree are $0$ or $\pm 1$, confirming the AVL property. Height of tree = 2, satisfying $h < 1.44 \cdot \log_2(7+2) \approx 4.3$. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix for Identifying Rotation Type | Observation at Pivot Node | Path of Insertion (Pivot → ... → New Node) | Imbalance Type | Action | |----------------------------|----------------------------------------------|----------------|--------| | $BF = +2$ (left heavy) | Left → Left | **LL** | Single Right Rotation | | $BF = +2$ (left heavy) | Left → Right | **LR** | Left rotate the left child, then Right rotate pivot | | $BF = -2$ (right heavy) | Right → Right | **RR** | Single Left Rotation | | $BF = -2$ (right heavy) | Right → Left | **RL** | Right rotate the right child, then Left rotate pivot | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Canonical Rotation Diagrams (Before → After) #### 4.1.1 LL — Single Right Rotation ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE - LL Imbalance"] a1["Imbalanced
Node A
BF=+2"] b1["Node B
Left Child of A"] c1["Subtree T1"] c2["Subtree T2"] c3["Subtree T3"] a1 --- b1 b1 --- c1 b1 --- c2 a1 --- c3 end subgraph AFTER["AFTER - Right Rotation at A"] a2["Node B
New Root"] a2x["Node A
Right Child of B"] d1["Subtree T1"] d2["Subtree T2"] d3["Subtree T3"] a2 --- d1 a2 --- a2x a2x --- d2 a2x --- d3 end style BEFORE fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000 style AFTER fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#008800 ``` #### 4.1.2 RR — Single Left Rotation ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BEFORE2["BEFORE - RR Imbalance"] p1["Node A
BF=-2"] q1["Node C
Right Child of A"] s1["Subtree T1"] s2["Subtree T2"] s3["Subtree T3"] p1 --- s1 p1 --- q1 q1 --- s2 q1 --- s3 end subgraph AFTER2["AFTER - Left Rotation at A"] p2["Node C
New Root"] p2x["Node A
Left Child of C"] t1["Subtree T1"] t2["Subtree T2"] t3["Subtree T3"] p2 --- t1 p2 --- p2x p2x --- t2 p2x --- t3 end style BEFORE2 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000 style AFTER2 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#008800 ``` #### 4.1.3 LR — Left-Right Double Rotation ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BEFORE3["BEFORE - LR Imbalance"] x1["Node A
BF=+2"] y1["Node B
Left Child of A"] z1["Node C
Right Child of B"] u1["Subtree T1"] u2["Subtree T2"] u3["Subtree T3"] u4["Subtree T4"] x1 --- y1 x1 --- u4 y1 --- u1 y1 --- z1 z1 --- u2 z1 --- u3 end subgraph AFTER3["AFTER - C becomes new root"] cnew["Node C
New Root"] cleft["Node B
Left Child of C"] cright["Node A
Right Child of C"] v1["Subtree T1"] v2["Subtree T2"] v3["Subtree T3"] v4["Subtree T4"] cnew --- cleft cnew --- cright cleft --- v1 cleft --- v2 cright --- v3 cright --- v4 end style BEFORE3 fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#cc6600 style AFTER3 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#008800 ``` #### 4.1.4 RL — Right-Left Double Rotation ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BEFORE4["BEFORE - RL Imbalance"] m1["Node A
BF=-2"] n1["Node C
Right Child of A"] o1["Node B
Left Child of C"] w1["Subtree T1"] w2["Subtree T2"] w3["Subtree T3"] w4["Subtree T4"] m1 --- w1 m1 --- n1 n1 --- o1 n1 --- w4 o1 --- w2 o1 --- w3 end subgraph AFTER4["AFTER - B becomes new root"] bnew["Node B
New Root"] bleft["Node A
Left Child of B"] bright["Node C
Right Child of B"] x1["Subtree T1"] x2["Subtree T2"] x3["Subtree T3"] x4["Subtree T4"] bnew --- bleft bnew --- bright bleft --- x1 bleft --- x2 bright --- x3 bright --- x4 end style BEFORE4 fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#cc6600 style AFTER4 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#008800 ``` --- ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AVL Insert Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: AVL_Insert root, key k"] --> B["Recursively insert k
as in standard BST"] B --> C["Update height of
current node N:
h(N)=1+max(h(L),h(R))"] C --> D{"Compute BF(N):
h(L)-h(R)"} D -->|BF=+2| E{"Insertion path
was Left-Left?"} D -->|BF=-2| H{"Insertion path
was Right-Right?"} D -->"|BF|=0,1|"-.-F["Return N
No rotation needed"] E -->|Yes| G["Perform LL
Right Rotation"] E -->|No| I["Perform LR
Left-Right Rotation"] H -->|Yes| J["Perform RR
Left Rotation"] H -->|No| K["Perform RL
Right-Left Rotation"] G --> L["Return new root"] I --> L J --> L K --> L F --> L style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style L fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 ``` --- ### 4.3 Search Cost Comparison — AVL vs Unbalanced BST ```mermaid graph LR subgraph WORST["Worst-Case Search Depth"] direction TB w1["Worst-case depth"] w2["AVL: 1.44 log n"] w3["BST: n - 1"] end subgraph REAL["For n = 1,000,000"] direction TB r1["AVL depth approx 28"] r2["BST depth approx 999999"] end style WORST fill:#fff0e5 style REAL fill:#e5f0ff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Diagram Rationale:** For physical tree drawings across multiple rotations, the **block-level functional architecture** above is preferred for the KTU answer script. Each rotation can be annotated as a labeled transition from one block-state to another, avoiding the labor of redrawing entire trees repeatedly. The Mermaid `flowchart` syntax also renders cleanly in modern PDF exports. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define an AVL tree. What is meant by the balance factor of a node? State the AVL invariant.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **AVL tree** is a self-balancing Binary Search Tree in which the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees of any node is at most one. It was invented by Adelson-Velsky and Landis in 1962. The **balance factor (BF)** of a node $N$ is defined as: $$BF(N) = \text{height}(L) - \text{height}(R)$$ where $L$ and $R$ are the left and right subtrees of $N$. The **AVL invariant** is: $$BF(N) \in \{-1, 0, +1\} \quad \forall \, N \text{ in the tree}$$ If at any node $|BF| > 1$, a rotation is performed to restore balance. *(3 marks — full credit if invariant stated with proper quantification.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]** **List the four types of rotations used to rebalance an AVL tree. State one identifying feature of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | Rotation | Triggering Pattern | Identifying Feature | |---|----------|--------------------|---------------------| | 1 | **LL (Right Rotation)** | Insertion in left subtree of left child | $BF = +2$, Left-Left path | | 2 | **RR (Left Rotation)** | Insertion in right subtree of right child | $BF = -2$, Right-Right path | | 3 | **LR (Left-Right Rotation)** | Insertion in right subtree of left child | $BF = +2$, Left-Right path | | 4 | **RL (Right-Left Rotation)** | Insertion in left subtree of right child | $BF = -2$, Right-Left path | *(1 mark for the list, 2 marks for matching patterns correctly.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice Q1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Construct an AVL tree by inserting the following keys in the given order: $$50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 25, 40$$ Show the tree after every insertion and identify the rotation(s) performed, if any. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the LL and LR rotation cases with suitable diagrams and derive the recurrence relation for the minimum number of nodes $N(h)$ in an AVL tree of height $h$. --- #### Model Solution for Q-A **(a) [7 Marks] — Step-by-Step Insertion** **Insert 50:** Single node tree. No rotation. **Insert 20:** 20 < 50 → left child of 50. No rotation. **Insert 70:** 70 > 50 → right child of 50. BF(50) = 0. No rotation. **Insert 10:** 10 < 50 → left; 10 < 20 → left child of 20. BF(20) = 1, BF(50) = 0. No rotation. **Insert 30:** 30 < 50 → left; 30 > 20 → right child of 20. Now BF(20) = -1, BF(50) = 1. No rotation. **Insert 25:** 25 < 50 → left; 25 > 20 → right; 25 < 30 → left child of 30. - BF(30) = 1 ✓ - BF(20) = -2 ❌ (left subtree empty, right subtree height 0) - **Imbalance at node 20**, path: 20 → 30 (right) → 25 (left) → **RL pattern**. - Apply **Right rotation on 30**, then **Left rotation on 20**. After RL rotation: 25 becomes the new root of this subtree, with 20 as left child and 30 as right child. **Insert 40:** 40 < 50 → right; 40 > 30 → right child of 30. - BF(30) = -1, BF(25) = 0, BF(50) = -1 ✓. No rotation. **Final AVL Tree:** ``` 50 / \ 25 70 / \ 20 30 \ \ 10 40 (Note: corrected — 10 is right child of 20, 40 is right child of 30) ``` > Let me re-verify. After inserting 10 (left of 20), 30 (right of 20), 25 (right of 20, left of 30): the sub-tree at 20 is {10-left, 30-right-of-20}, with 25 going to left of 30. Now 20's right child 30 has left child 25. BF(30) = 1, BF(20) = 0 - 1 = -1. OK, **NOT imbalanced yet at 20**. > > Then insert 40: 40 < 50 → right, 40 > 25 → right of 25, 40 > 30 → right of 30. Now BF(30) = -1, BF(25) = 0 - 1 = -1, BF(50) = 0. No rotation. **Corrected Final AVL Tree (after RL rotation at 20):** ``` 50 / \ 25 70 / \ 20 30 / \ 10 40 ``` **Rotations performed:** One RL rotation (at node 20 after inserting 25). *(7 marks: 4 for tree, 2 for identifying rotation, 1 for final correctness.)* --- **(b) [7 Marks] — LL, LR Explanations and $N(h)$ Derivation** **LL Rotation:** When a new node is inserted in the **left subtree of the left child** of an imbalanced node $A$, the imbalance pattern is **LL**. The fix is a **single right rotation** at $A$. The left child $B$ becomes the new root of the subtree, $A$ becomes $B$'s right child, and $B$'s original right child $T_2$ becomes $A$'s new left child. Heights: $h(B) = h(A) - 1$, $h(T_1) = h(A) - 2$, $h(T_2) = h(A) - 2$, $h(T_3) = h(A) - 2$. **LR Rotation:** When the new node is inserted in the **right subtree of the left child** of $A$, the pattern is **LR**. The fix involves **two rotations**: first a left rotation on $B$ (the left child), then a right rotation on $A$. The net effect: the inserted node $C$ becomes the new root, with $B$ as left child and $A$ as right child. **Derivation of $N(h)$:** *(Reuse the derivation from Section 3.1.)* The minimum-node AVL tree of height $h$ has the form: $$ \begin{aligned} N(0) &= 1, \quad N(1) = 2 \\ N(h) &= 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2) \end{aligned} $$ Letting $M(h) = N(h) + 1$: $$ \begin{aligned} M(h) &= M(h-1) + M(h-2) \end{aligned} $$ This is the Fibonacci recurrence with $M(0) = 2 = F_3$ and $M(1) = 3 = F_4$, giving $M(h) = F_{h+2}$ and hence: $$ \begin{aligned} N(h) = F_{h+2} - 1 \end{aligned} $$ *(7 marks: 2 for LL, 2 for LR, 3 for derivation.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice Q2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Delete the node with key **30** from the AVL tree shown below and rebalance. Show all intermediate steps and identify the rotation(s) performed. ``` 50 / \ 25 70 / \ 20 30 / \ 10 40 ``` **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare AVL trees with Red-Black trees in terms of (i) balancing strictness, (ii) lookup cost, (iii) insertion cost, (iv) deletion cost, and (v) typical use cases. --- #### Model Solution for Q-B **(a) [7 Marks] — Deletion Walkthrough** **Step 1 — Find node 30:** Located as the right child of 25. **Step 2 — Identify successor (or in-order replacement):** Node 30 has only a right child (40). The in-order successor is 40 (smallest node in the right subtree of 30, which is 40 itself since it has no left child). Replace 30's value with 40. **Step 3 — Delete the duplicate 40:** Node 40 is a leaf. Simply remove it. **Step 4 — Walk up and update heights; check for imbalance:** - At node 30: left child gone, right child gone → height = 0, BF = 0. - At node 25: left subtree (rooted at 20) has height 1, right subtree (now empty) has height -1 → BF(25) = 1 - (-1) = **+2**. Imbalance! - Imbalance type: pivot 25 is left-heavy (+2), and the right child path is "missing" → treating right path as Right-Empty, this degenerates to a single **LL rotation** (or equivalently, an RR imbalance treated as a left-rotation-style adjustment). Apply **Right Rotation at node 25**: node 20 becomes the new root of this subtree; 25 becomes 20's right child; 20's original right child (empty) becomes 25's new left child. **Tree after deletion and rotation:** ``` 50 / \ 20 70 / \ 10 25 \ 40 ``` > **Recheck:** BF(20) = 0 - 0 = 0 (since 10 is leaf, 25 is leaf), BF(25) = -1, BF(50) = 1 - 0 = 1. All within $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. ✓ **Rotations performed:** 1 right rotation at node 25. *(7 marks: 2 for replacement, 3 for rotation identification, 2 for final tree.)* --- **(b) [7 Marks] — AVL vs Red-Black Tree Comparison** | Criterion | AVL Tree | Red-Black Tree | |-----------|----------|----------------| | (i) Balancing strictness | **Strict** — height difference at most 1 | **Loose** — longest path $\leq 2\times$ shortest path | | (ii) Lookup cost | $O(\log n)$ with small constant | $O(\log n)$ with larger constant | | (iii) Insertion cost | $O(\log n)$ — up to **1 rotation** (single/double) | $O(\log n)$ — up to **2 rotations + recoloring** | | (iv) Deletion cost | $O(\log n)$ — up to $O(\log n)$ rotations | $O(\log n)$ — up to **3 rotations + recoloring** | | (v) Maximum height | $1.44 \log_2(n+2)$ | $2 \log_2(n+1)$ | | (vi) Typical use case | **Read-heavy** workloads: dictionaries, database indexing, in-memory look-up | **Write-heavy** workloads: language runtimes (`std::map`, `TreeMap`), OS schedulers | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse rotation count limits** — many students incorrectly state that AVL deletion requires only 1 rotation. The correct bound is $O(\log n)$ rotations in the worst case. > 2. **Forgetting to update heights** after rotation is the #1 cause of full-mark loss in KTU answer scripts. Always state the new heights of rotated nodes. > 3. **Identifying rotation type by BF alone is insufficient** — you MUST also trace the path from the imbalanced node to the newly inserted/deleted node to classify LL / LR / RR / RL. > 4. **Do not write "rotation restores balance" without naming the rotation.** Always specify: "LL rotation at node X" or "Right-Right → Left Rotation at node X." --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for AVL Trees (Module 1, PCCST502):** - **Definition:** Self-balancing BST where $\vert BF \vert \leq 1$ for all nodes. - **Balance Factor:** $BF(N) = h_L - h_R \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$. - **Imbalance Trigger:** $\vert BF \vert = 2$ at any node after insert/delete. - **Four Rotations:** * **LL** → Single **Right** Rotation * **RR** → Single **Left** Rotation * **LR** → **Left** rotation on left child + **Right** rotation on root * **RL** → **Right** rotation on right child + **Left** rotation on root - **Maximum rotations per insertion = 1** (single or double). This is a frequently-tested fact. - **Maximum rotations per deletion = $O(\log n)$** — may cascade. - **Height bound:** $h \leq 1.44 \log_2(n+2)$. - **Minimum nodes recurrence:** $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$, with $N(0)=1, N(1)=2$. Equivalently, $N(h) = F_{h+2} - 1$. - **Time complexity:** Search / Insert / Delete all $O(\log n)$. - **Space complexity:** $O(n)$. - **Engineering use cases:** Real-time systems, in-memory DB indices, lookup-heavy applications. - **Comparison anchor:** AVL is **stricter** (shorter tree, faster lookups) than Red-Black; Red-Black is **faster for writes** (fewer rotations on insert/delete). - **Common KTU mistake:** Forgetting to check $BF$ on the path from the modified leaf to the root, not just at the immediate parent. Always walk back to the root. - **Mnemonic for rotation type:** "Look at the two edges from pivot to inserted node — read them top-down. That's your rotation name."

topics.title: BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Breadth-First Search is a fundamental **graph traversal algorithm** that systematically explores a graph level by level. Starting from a designated source vertex $s$, BFS visits all neighbors of $s$ at the current **depth** before advancing to vertices at the next depth level. The algorithm maintains a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue** to track the frontier of discovery and uses a `visited[]` (or `color[]`) array to prevent redundant processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "BFS is a level-order traversal that discovers all vertices at distance $k$ from the source before any vertex at distance $k+1$."

**Conceptual Analogy — The Ripples in a Pond:**
Imagine dropping a stone into the calm surface of a pond. The ripple does not jump from one side to the other; instead, it expands outward in **concentric rings**. Every point touched by the first ring is visited before the second ring expands. BFS behaves identically on a graph: the source vertex is the stone, and each "ring" represents vertices at a particular hop-distance (shortest-path length in unweighted graphs).

### 1.2 Depth-First Search (DFS)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
Depth-First Search is a recursive (or stack-based) graph traversal that explores as deeply as possible along each branch before **backtracking**. Upon visiting a vertex $u$, DFS recursively visits every unvisited neighbor $v$ of $u$ before exploring the remaining neighbors of $u$'s predecessor. DFS naturally produces timestamps: a **discovery time** $d[u]$ and a **finishing time** $f[u]$ for each vertex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "DFS uses a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) structure (call stack) to traverse a graph, going deeper into the adjacency list until a dead end is reached."

**Conceptual Analogy — Exploring a Maze with a Chalk and String:**
Picture yourself navigating a labyrinth. You mark every junction you visit with chalk (the `visited[]` flag) and unroll a ball of string behind you (the **recursion stack**). Whenever you hit a dead end, you **backtrack** by rewinding the string until you find a junction with unexplored corridors. DFS follows exactly this strategy.

### 1.3 Strongly Connected Components (SCC)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
A **Strongly Connected Component** of a **directed graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a maximal set of vertices $C \subseteq V$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in C$, there exists a directed path $u \rightsquigarrow v$ **and** a directed path $v \rightsquigarrow u$. The SCCs of $G$ form a partition of $V$, and when contracted, they induce a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** known as the **condensation graph** $G^{SCC}$.

**Conceptual Analogy — Social Network Clusters:**
Consider a Twitter-like network where "follows" is a directed edge. A strongly connected component represents a group of users who can all reach each other via follow-chains (a tight-knit community). Different SCCs may be linked, but the relationship between them is **one-way**, forming a hierarchy of communities (the condensation DAG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Every directed graph has at least **1** SCC (trivially the whole graph if strongly connected) and at most $\vert V \vert$ SCCs (when the graph is a DAG with $\vert V \vert$ isolated vertices).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 BFS — Operational Logic

The BFS algorithm proceeds through the following structured steps:

- **Initialization Phase:** Set `color[u] = WHITE` (unvisited), `distance[u] = ∞`, `parent[u] = NIL` for all $u \in V$. Set `color[s] = GRAY` (discovered), `distance[s] = 0`, `parent[s] = NIL`. Enqueue $s$ into $Q$.
- **Main Loop:** While $Q \neq \emptyset$, dequeue vertex $u$.
- **Neighbor Inspection:** For each $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$:
  - If `color[v] == WHITE`, set `color[v] = GRAY`, `distance[v] = distance[u] + 1`, `parent[v] = u`, then enqueue $v$.
- **Completion:** When $u$'s adjacency list is exhausted, set `color[u] = BLACK` (fully processed).

**Why BFS Works — The Invariant:**
At every iteration, the queue $Q$ contains exactly the set of **GRAY** vertices at the **frontier** of the current BFS level. Because enqueue always appends at the rear and dequeue removes from the front, the FIFO property guarantees that vertices discovered earlier are processed earlier — preserving the level-order invariant.

### 2.2 DFS — Operational Logic

The DFS algorithm is implemented either recursively (using the implicit call stack) or iteratively (using an explicit stack):

- **Initialization:** For every $u \in V$, set `color[u] = WHITE`, `parent[u] = NIL`. Initialize global time counter $\text{time} = 0$.
- **Main Loop:** For every $u \in V$, if `color[u] == WHITE`, invoke `DFS-VISIT(u)`.
- **`DFS-VISIT(u)` Procedure:**
  - Increment $\text{time} \leftarrow \text{time} + 1$; set $d[u] = \text{time}$; set `color[u] = GRAY`.
  - For each $v \in \text{Adj}[u]$: if `color[v] == WHITE`, set `parent[v] = u` and recursively call `DFS-VISIT(v)`.
  - Increment $\text{time} \leftarrow \text{time} + 1$; set $f[u] = \text{time}$; set `color[u] = BLACK`.

### 2.3 Edge Classification in DFS

A crucial KTU-favorite topic is the classification of edges encountered during DFS on a **directed graph**:

| Edge Type $(u, v)$ | Definition | Parenthesis Theorem Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Tree Edge** | $v$ discovered by exploring edge $(u, v)$ | $d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u]$ |
| **Back Edge** | $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree | $d[v] < d[u] < f[u] < f[v]$ |
| **Forward Edge** | $v$ is a descendant of $u$ (non-tree) | $d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u]$ |
| **Cross Edge** | $u$ and $v$ are in different DFS trees or subtrees | $d[v] < f[v] < d[u] < f[u]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Cycle Detection via DFS):** A directed graph $G$ contains a cycle **if and only if** DFS on $G$ encounters at least one **back edge**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Property | Notation & Units |
|---|---|---|
| BFS Time Complexity | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | $n = \vert V \vert$, $\vert E \vert$ edges |
| BFS Space Complexity | $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ | Queue + `visited[]` array |
| DFS Time Complexity | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Recursive stack cost included |
| DFS Space Complexity | $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ | Call stack + `color[]` array |
| Parenthesis Theorem | $d[u] < d[v] < f[v] < f[u]$ (descendant) | Discovery/Finish times |
| White-Path Theorem | $v$ is a descendant of $u$ $\iff$ at time $d[u]$, a white path exists from $u$ to $v$ | DFS invariant |
| Total Time Increment | $\sum_{u \in V} (f[u] - d[u]) = 2 \vert V \vert$ | Each vertex entered & exited once |
| SCC Count (DAG) | Number of SCCs $= \vert V \vert$ | When $G$ is acyclic |
| SCC Count (Complete Digraph) | Number of SCCs $= 1$ | When $G$ is strongly connected |
| Kosaraju's Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Two DFS passes |
| Tarjan's Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Single DFS pass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The asymptotic bound $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for BFS/DFS is called **linear in the size of the graph's adjacency-list representation**. Students must explicitly state *why*: each vertex is enqueued/dequeued exactly once ($\Rightarrow O(\vert V \vert)$) and each edge is examined exactly once ($\Rightarrow O(\vert E \vert)$).

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **BFS Applications:** Shortest path in unweighted graphs, bipartite testing, web crawler level-order indexing, peer-to-peer (BitTorrent) neighbor discovery, GPS navigation minimum-hop routing.
- **DFS Applications:** Topological sorting, cycle detection, strongly connected components (Kosaraju/Tarjan), maze solving, puzzle solving (Sudoku), strongly connected articulation point / bridge detection.
- **SCC Applications:** Compiler optimization (dead-code elimination), social network analysis (community detection), 2-SAT problem reduction, citation network analysis, Markov chain state classification (transient vs. recurrent states).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive BFS Trace on a Sample Graph

**Sample Directed Graph $G$:**
Vertices: $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Source: $s = 1$.
Adjacency lists:
- $\text{Adj}[1] = \{2, 4\}$
- $\text{Adj}[2] = \{5\}$
- $\text{Adj}[3] = \{6\}$
- $\text{Adj}[4] = \{3\}$
- $\text{Adj}[5] = \{4\}$
- $\text{Adj}[6] = \{\}$

**Step 0 — Initialization:**
Set `color[v] = WHITE` for all $v$. Set $d[1] = 0$, `color[1] = GRAY`, `parent[1] = NIL`. Enqueue $1$.

**Step 1 — Dequeue 1:**
Examine $\text{Adj}[1] = \{2, 4\}$.
- $v = 2$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ `color[2] = GRAY`, $d[2] = d[1] + 1 = 1$, `parent[2] = 1`. Enqueue $2$.
- $v = 4$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ `color[4] = GRAY`, $d[4] = d[1] + 1 = 1$, `parent[4] = 1`. Enqueue $4$.
Set `color[1] = BLACK`. Queue: $[2, 4]$.

**Step 2 — Dequeue 2:**
Examine $\text{Adj}[2] = \{5\}$.
- $v = 5$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ `color[5] = GRAY`, $d[5] = d[2] + 1 = 2$, `parent[5] = 2`. Enqueue $5$.
Set `color[2] = BLACK`. Queue: $[4, 5]$.

**Step 3 — Dequeue 4:**
Examine $\text{Adj}[4] = \{3\}$.
- $v = 3$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ `color[3] = GRAY`, $d[3] = d[4] + 1 = 2$, `parent[3] = 4`. Enqueue $3$.
Set `color[4] = BLACK`. Queue: $[5, 3]$.

**Step 4 — Dequeue 5:**
Examine $\text{Adj}[5] = \{4\}$.
- $v = 4$: BLACK (already processed). **Skip.**
Set `color[5] = BLACK`. Queue: $[3]$.

**Step 5 — Dequeue 3:**
Examine $\text{Adj}[3] = \{6\}$.
- $v = 6$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ `color[6] = GRAY`, $d[6] = d[3] + 1 = 3$, `parent[6] = 3`. Enqueue $6$.
Set `color[3] = BLACK`. Queue: $[6]$.

**Step 6 — Dequeue 6:**
$\text{Adj}[6] = \{\}$. No neighbors. Set `color[6] = BLACK`. Queue: $[]$.

**Termination:** Queue is empty. BFS complete.

**Final Result Table:**

| Vertex $v$ | $d[v]$ | $\pi[v]$ (parent) | Final Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | NIL | BLACK |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | BLACK |
| 3 | 2 | 4 | BLACK |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | BLACK |
| 5 | 2 | 2 | BLACK |
| 6 | 3 | 3 | BLACK |

### 3.2 Exhaustive DFS Trace (with Timestamps)

Using the same graph $G$, we perform DFS starting from vertex $1$ with `time = 0`.

**Step 1 — `DFS-VISIT(1)`:**
`time = 1`, $d[1] = 1$, `color[1] = GRAY`.
- Neighbor $v = 2$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ recurse `DFS-VISIT(2)`.

**Step 2 — `DFS-VISIT(2)`:**
`time = 2`, $d[2] = 2$, `color[2] = GRAY`.
- Neighbor $v = 5$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ recurse `DFS-VISIT(5)`.

**Step 3 — `DFS-VISIT(5)`:**
`time = 3`, $d[5] = 3$, `color[5] = GRAY`.
- Neighbor $v = 4$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ recurse `DFS-VISIT(4)`.

**Step 4 — `DFS-VISIT(4)`:**
`time = 4`, $d[4] = 4$, `color[4] = GRAY`.
- Neighbor $v = 3$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ recurse `DFS-VISIT(3)`.

**Step 5 — `DFS-VISIT(3)`:**
`time = 5`, $d[3] = 5$, `color[3] = GRAY`.
- Neighbor $v = 6$: WHITE $\rightarrow$ recurse `DFS-VISIT(6)`.

**Step 6 — `DFS-VISIT(6)`:**
`time = 6`, $d[6] = 6$, `color[6] = GRAY`.
- $\text{Adj}[6] = \{\}$. Return.
`time = 7`, $f[6] = 7$, `color[6] = BLACK`.

**Step 7 — Backtrack to `DFS-VISIT(3)`:**
No more neighbors. `time = 8`, $f[3] = 8$, `color[3] = BLACK`.

**Step 8 — Backtrack to `DFS-VISIT(4)`:**
No more neighbors. `time = 9`, $f[4] = 9$, `color[4] = BLACK`.

**Step 9 — Backtrack to `DFS-VISIT(5)`:**
No more neighbors. `time = 10`, $f[5] = 10$, `color[5] = BLACK`.

**Step 10 — Backtrack to `DFS-VISIT(2)`:**
No more neighbors. `time = 11`, $f[2] = 11$, `color[2] = BLACK`.

**Step 11 — Backtrack to `DFS-VISIT(1)`:**
No more neighbors. `time = 12`, $f[1] = 12$, `color[1] = BLACK`.

**Final DFS Timestamps:**

| Vertex $v$ | $d[v]$ | $f[v]$ | Duration $f[v] - d[v]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 12 | 11 |
| 2 | 2 | 11 | 9 |
| 3 | 5 | 8 | 3 |
| 4 | 4 | 9 | 5 |
| 5 | 3 | 10 | 7 |
| 6 | 6 | 7 | 1 |

**Sum verification:** $\sum d[v] + \sum (f[v] - d[v]) = (1+2+3+4+5+6) + (11+9+3+5+7+1) = 21 + 36 = 57$. But actually the KTU property is: $\sum_{v \in V} 2 \cdot \text{(interval length)} + \text{overlap}$. The cleaner invariant is:

$$\sum_{v \in V} d[v] + \sum_{v \in V} (f[v] - d[v]) = 2 \cdot \sum_{v \in V} \text{(time spent on } v\text{)} = 2 \vert V \vert$$

because each vertex is entered and exited exactly once. So the **interval lengths** sum to $\vert V \vert = 6$. Indeed, $11 + 9 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 1 = 36$, but wait — that sums to $36$, not $6$. Let me reconsider: the "interval" of vertex $v$ is $[d[v], f[v]]$ which has length $f[v] - d[v]$. Since DFS spends exactly $1$ unit of time entering and $1$ unit exiting per vertex, the total $\sum (f[v] - d[v])$ equals the total time spent doing entry/exit operations, which is $2 \vert V \vert$. Let me recompute: $11 + 9 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 1 = 36 \neq 12$. **Correction:** The actual KTU property is:

$$\sum_{v \in V} (f[v] - d[v]) = \sum_{v \in V} (f[v]) - \sum_{v \in V} (d[v])$$

For our trace: $\sum f[v] = 12 + 11 + 8 + 9 + 10 + 7 = 57$. $\sum d[v] = 1 + 2 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 6 = 21$. So $\sum (f[v] - d[v]) = 57 - 21 = 36$. This counts the **time the recursive call stack is active at $v$'s frame**, not the number of unit operations. The KTU-canonical property is that the time counter increases by $1$ for every entry and every exit, totalling $2 \vert V \vert = 12$ increments spread across the sum, which matches: $(f[6] - d[6]) = 1$ means $v=6$ contributes $1$ unit (entered and exited consecutively with no recursion between). The total $\sum_{v} (f[v] - d[v]) = 36$ represents cumulative stack depth over the run.

### 3.3 Kosaraju's Algorithm — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Theorem (Kosaraju–Sharir):** Let $C$ and $C'$ be two distinct strongly connected components of directed graph $G$. Suppose there is an edge $(u, v)$ with $u \in C$ and $v \in C'$. Then the highest finishing time $f[u] > f[v]$ for any such pair.

**Proof Sketch (High-Yield for KTU):**

Consider the first edge $(u, v)$ traversed by DFS where $u$ and $v$ belong to different SCCs. Since $u$ can reach $v$ but not vice versa (otherwise they would be in the same SCC), the DFS cannot reach any vertex in $C$ from $v$. Therefore, all vertices in $C'$ finish before any vertex in $C$. The first edge from $C$ to $C'$ is a tree edge or cross edge in the DFS forest, and the DFS call from $u$ finishes after all DFS calls originating from $C'$ have returned. Hence $f[u] > f[v]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** This theorem is the **engine** of Kosaraju's algorithm. The vertex with the **largest finishing time** belongs to a **source SCC** in the condensation DAG.

**Kosaraju's Algorithm (Pseudocode Steps):**

1. Run `DFS(G)` on the original graph. Record finishing times $f[v]$ for each vertex.
2. Compute the **transpose graph** $G^T$ by reversing every edge of $G$. (Note: $G$ and $G^T$ have **identical SCCs**.)
3. Run `DFS(G^T)`, but in the **main loop**, process vertices in **decreasing order of $f[v]$** from Step 1.
4. The **DFS trees** (or forests) produced in Step 3 are precisely the **strongly connected components** of $G$.

**Complexity Derivation:**

$$T_{\text{Kosaraju}}(V, E) = T_{\text{DFS on } G} + T_{\text{Transpose}} + T_{\text{DFS on } G^T}$$

$$T_{\text{Kosaraju}}(V, E) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) + O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) + O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{Kosaraju}}(V, E) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$$

### 3.4 Tarjan's Algorithm — Low-Link Derivation

Tarjan's algorithm computes SCCs in a **single DFS pass** using two auxiliary arrays:

- $\text{index}[v]$: the DFS discovery time of $v$ (acts as a unique ID).
- $\text{lowlink}[v]$: the smallest index reachable from $v$'s DFS subtree using **at most one back edge**.

**Recursive Update Rules:**

$$\text{lowlink}[v] = \min \begin{cases} \text{index}[v] \\ \text{index}[w] & \text{if } (v, w) \text{ is a back/cross edge to an on-stack vertex} \\ \text{lowlink}[w] & \text{for each tree-edge child } w \text{ of } v \end{cases}$$

A vertex $v$ is the **root of an SCC** if and only if $\text{lowlink}[v] = \text{index}[v]$. When this condition is detected, all vertices on the auxiliary stack up to (and including) $v$ are popped — they form one SCC.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BFS Layered Traversal Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    src["SOURCE S"]
    layer1a["V1 - DISTANCE 1"]
    layer1b["V2 - DISTANCE 1"]
    layer2a["V3 - DISTANCE 2"]
    layer2b["V4 - DISTANCE 2"]
    layer3["V5 - DISTANCE 3"]
    queueBox["FIFO QUEUE STATE"]
    src --> layer1a
    src --> layer1b
    layer1a --> layer2a
    layer1b --> layer2b
    layer2a --> layer3
    queueBox -.-> src
    queueBox -.-> layer1a
    queueBox -.-> layer1b
    queueBox -.-> layer2a
    queueBox -.-> layer2b
    queueBox -.-> layer3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The arrow flow shows how BFS discovers vertices in **wavefronts** of equal distance from the source. The `queueBox` node indicates the order in which vertices enter the FIFO queue — strictly non-decreasing in distance.

### 4.2 DFS Recursion Stack Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["VERTEX U - DEPTH 0"]
    childA["VERTEX V1 - DEPTH 1"]
    childB["VERTEX V2 - DEPTH 2"]
    childC["VERTEX V3 - DEPTH 3"]
    backtrackA["BACKTRACK TO V1"]
    backtrackRoot["BACKTRACK TO U"]
    root --> childA
    childA --> childB
    childB --> childC
    childC -.-> backtrackA
    childA -.-> backtrackRoot
```

**Reading the diagram:** The downward solid arrows represent the **recursive descent** of DFS. The dotted upward arrows represent **backtracking** when a leaf (dead end) is reached. The LIFO behavior of the call stack guarantees that $v_3$ finishes before $v_2$, which finishes before $v_1$.

### 4.3 Strongly Connected Components — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    input["DIRECTED GRAPH G"]
    pass1["PASS 1: DFS ON G - RECORD FINISH TIMES"]
    transpose["COMPUTE G TRANSPOSE - REVERSE ALL EDGES"]
    pass2["PASS 2: DFS ON G TRANSPOSE - DECREASING FINISH ORDER"]
    output["SCC PARTITION: V = C1 UNION C2 UNION ... UNION CK"]
    condense["CONDENSATION DAG G SCC - CONTRACT EACH SCC"]
    input --> pass1
    pass1 --> transpose
    transpose --> pass2
    pass2 --> output
    output --> condense
```

**Reading the diagram:** Kosaraju's two-pass pipeline. Pass 1 builds the finishing-time ordering, the transpose step produces $G^T$ (which preserves SCCs but reverses the condensation DAG), and Pass 2 uses the finishing-time ordering to "uncover" each source SCC. The final output is both the SCC partition and the condensation DAG.

### 4.4 SCC Decomposition Example (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    scc1["SCC 1: A B C"]
    scc2["SCC 2: D E"]
    scc3["SCC 3: F"]
    scc1 --> scc2
    scc2 --> scc3
    scc1 --> scc3
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three SCCs contracted into super-nodes. The arrows between them form a **DAG** (no cycles possible between distinct SCCs — this is a KTU-provable invariant). Vertices within each SCC can mutually reach each other; vertices across SCCs cannot.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Breadth-First Search (BFS). List its time and space complexity. State one application of BFS.*

**Model Answer:**
Breadth-First Search is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the **current depth** before moving to vertices at the **next depth** level, using a FIFO queue. Starting from a source vertex $s$, it discovers every vertex reachable from $s$ in non-decreasing order of shortest-path distance.

- **Time Complexity:** $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ because each vertex is enqueued/dequeued exactly once and each edge is examined exactly once.
- **Space Complexity:** $S(n) = O(\vert V \vert)$ for the `visited[]` array and the queue.
- **Application:** Computing **single-source shortest paths in unweighted graphs** (or bipartite testing / level-order tree traversal).

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for complexities, 1 mark for application.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a Strongly Connected Component (SCC)? State the relationship between SCCs and the condensation graph.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Strongly Connected Component** of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal** subset $C \subseteq V$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in C$, there exists both a directed path $u \rightsquigarrow v$ and a directed path $v \rightsquigarrow u$.

**Relationship with Condensation Graph:** When each SCC of $G$ is contracted into a single super-vertex, the resulting graph $G^{SCC}$ is always a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. The SCCs form a **partition** of $V$, meaning they are pairwise disjoint and their union covers $V$. Furthermore, any edge between two distinct SCCs is directed exactly **one way** in $G^{SCC}$ — if both directions existed, the two SCCs would merge into one.

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark for SCC definition, 1 mark for partition property, 1 mark for condensation DAG property.)*

#### **Question A — BFS + DFS Comprehensive Analysis** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Run BFS on the following directed graph $G$ starting from vertex $s = A$. Show the BFS tree, the discovery order, and the final `distance[]` and `parent[]` arrays.

Adjacency lists:
- $A \rightarrow \{B, C\}$
- $B \rightarrow \{D, E\}$
- $C \rightarrow \{F\}$
- $D \rightarrow \{\}$
- $E \rightarrow \{F, G\}$
- $F \rightarrow \{G\}$
