An MST **exists** for every connected, weighted, undirected graph. When multiple edges have the **same weight**, more than one MST may exist, but they will all have the **same total minimum weight**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Properties (Proven Tools)

**Cut Property (used by Prim's):**
For any cut $(S, V-S)$ of $G$, if an edge $e = (u, v)$ is the unique light edge crossing the cut, then $e$ belongs to **every** MST of $G$. Even if not unique, the light edge belongs to **some** MST.

**Cycle Property (used by Kruskal's):**
For any cycle $C$ in $G$, if edge $e$ has strictly greater weight than every other edge in $C$, then $e$ **cannot** belong to any MST.

### 2.2 Why These Algorithms Are Greedy

Both Kruskal's and Prim's are classified as **Greedy Algorithms** because at every step they make the locally optimal choice (lightest available edge) hoping it leads to a globally optimal MST. The cut/cycle properties *prove* that these greedy choices are globally safe.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Complexity Cheat Sheet

| Algorithm | Data Structure Used | Time Complexity | Space | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kruskal's** | Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find) + Edge Sort | $O(E \log E)$ or equivalently $O(E \log V)$ | $O(E + V)$ | **Sparse** graphs (small $E$) |
| **Prim's (Adjacency Matrix)** | Array / Key array | $O(V^2)$ | $O(V)$ | **Dense** graphs |
| **Prim's (Binary Heap)** | Min-Heap + Adjacency List | $O(E \log V)$ | $O(E + V)$ | General / Sparse |
| **Prim's (Fibonacci Heap)** | Fibonacci Heap | $O(E + V \log V)$ | $O(E + V)$ | Theoretical optimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge-Count Rule of Thumb (Memorize!):**
> - If $E \approx V^2$ (dense) → use **Prim's with adjacency matrix**, $O(V^2)$.
> - If $E \ll V^2$ (sparse) → use **Kruskal's** or **Prim's with binary heap**, both $O(E \log V)$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Design:** LAN topology, electrical grid layout, telephone networks.
- **Computer Networks:** Broadcasting, multicast routing, clustering algorithms.
- **VLSI Design:** Wire-length minimization in chip layout.
- **Approximation Algorithms:** MST is the backbone of the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** 2-approximation.
- **Image Segmentation & Clustering:** Single-linkage clustering in machine learning.
- **Civil Engineering:** Designing minimum-cost road/pipe networks connecting locations.

### 2.5 Greedy Choice Proof Outline

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof Sketch (Cut Property):** Let $e$ be the light edge across cut $(S, V-S)$. Suppose there is an MST $T'$ that does **not** include $e$. Adding $e$ to $T'$ creates a cycle $C$. This cycle must contain some other edge $e'$ crossing the cut. Since $e$ is the *lightest*, $w(e) \leq w(e')$. Replacing $e'$ with $e$ yields a spanning tree $T''$ with weight $\leq w(T')$. Because $T'$ was minimum, $T'' = T'$ and $e$ must already be in some MST.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Algorithms, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 KRUSKAL'S ALGORITHM (Edge-Greedy / Bottom-Up)

#### 3.1.1 Strategy
Sort all edges by weight in **non-decreasing order**. Walk through them; pick each edge if it does **NOT** form a cycle with already chosen edges (cycle test is done using **Disjoint Set Union — Union-Find**). Stop when $V-1$ edges are chosen.

#### 3.1.2 Pseudocode

```
KRUSKAL(G, w):
  A ← ∅
  for each vertex v ∈ G.V:
      MAKE-SET(v)
  sort edges in G.E into non-decreasing order by weight w
  for each edge (u, v) ∈ G.E (in sorted order):
      if FIND-SET(u) ≠ FIND-SET(v):
          A ← A ∪ {(u, v)}
          UNION(u, v)
  return A
```

#### 3.1.3 Time Complexity Derivation

$$
T(n) = \underbrace{O(V)}_{\text{MAKE-SET loop}} + \underbrace{O(E \log E)}_{\text{sorting edges}} + \underbrace{O(E \cdot \alpha(V))}_{\text{Find/Union with path compression}}
$$

Since $E \leq V^2$, we have $\log E \leq 2 \log V$, so the dominant term is:

$$
T(n) = O(E \log V)
$$

where $\alpha(V)$ is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively a constant $< 5$ for all practical $V$).

---

### 3.2 PRIM'S ALGORITHM (Vertex-Greedy / Top-Down)

#### 3.2.1 Strategy
Start from any arbitrary root vertex $r$. Maintain a set of vertices already in the tree $T$. At every step, add the **cheapest edge** connecting a vertex in $T$ to a vertex outside $T$. Stop when all vertices are included.

#### 3.2.2 Pseudocode (Adjacency-Matrix Version)

```
PRIM(G, w, r):
  for each u ∈ G.V:
      key[u] ← ∞
      π[u] ← NIL
  key[r] ← 0
  Q ← G.V                 // min-priority queue
  while Q ≠ ∅:
      u ← EXTRACT-MIN(Q)
      for each v ∈ G.Adj[u]:
          if v ∈ Q and w(u,v) < key[v]:
              π[v] ← u
              key[v] ← w(u,v)
```

#### 3.2.3 Time Complexity Derivation

For the **adjacency matrix** version with array-based priority queue:

$$
T(n) = \underbrace{O(V)}_{\text{init}} + \underbrace{O(V)}_{\text{EXTRACT-MIN executed V times}} \cdot O(V) + \underbrace{O(E \cdot 1)}_{\text{key update}}
$$

$$
T(n) = O(V^2) \quad \text{(dominates on dense graphs)}
$$

For the **binary heap** version:

$$
T(n) = O(V \log V + E \log V) = O(E \log V)
$$

---

### 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE (Common to Both Algorithms)

**Graph:** $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$, 10 edges listed below in random order.

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(A,B)$ | **4** | $(C,D)$ | **3** |
| $(A,E)$ | **2** | $(D,E)$ | **4** |
| $(A,F)$ | **7** | $(D,F)$ | **5** |
| $(B,C)$ | **2** | $(E,F)$ | **6** |
| $(B,F)$ | **5** | $(C,F)$ | **4** |

#### 3.3.1 Kruskal's — Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 0:** Sort edges ascending: $(A,E,2), (B,C,2), (C,D,3), (A,B,4), (C,F,4), (D,E,4), (B,F,5), (D,F,5), (E,F,6), (A,F,7)$.

| Step | Edge Considered | Weight | Action | Reason / Cycle? | Forest Components |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(A,E)$ | 2 | **Add** | No cycle | $\{A,E\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{F\}$ |
| 2 | $(B,C)$ | 2 | **Add** | No cycle | $\{A,E\}, \{B,C\}, \{D\}, \{F\}$ |
| 3 | $(C,D)$ | 3 | **Add** | No cycle | $\{A,E\}, \{B,C,D\}, \{F\}$ |
| 4 | $(A,B)$ | 4 | **Add** | No cycle | $\{A,B,C,D,E\}, \{F\}$ |
| 5 | $(C,F)$ | 4 | **Add** | No cycle | $\{A,B,C,D,E,F\}$ ✔ 5 edges selected |

**Total MST Weight = $2 + 2 + 3 + 4 + 4 = \mathbf{15}$**

> [!TIP]
> If we had picked $(D,E)$ at step 5 instead of $(C,F)$, we would form cycle $A-E-D-C-B-A$, so Kruskal's correctly **rejects** $(D,E)$.

#### 3.3.2 Prim's — Step-by-Step Trace (Start at $A$)

| Step | Vertex added | Edge chosen | Weight | $key[]$ array after update |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | — | $key[A]=0$; $key[B]=4, key[E]=2, key[F]=7$; rest $=\infty$ |
| 1 | $A$ | — | — | $key[B]=4, key[E]=2, key[F]=7$ |
| 2 | $E$ | $(A,E)$ | **2** | $key[D]=4 (via E), key[F]=\min(7,6)=6$ |
| 3 | $B$ | $(A,B)$ | **4** | $key[C]=2 (via B), key[F]=\min(6,5)=5$ |
| 4 | $C$ | $(B,C)$ | **2** | $key[D]=\min(4,3)=3, key[F]=\min(5,4)=4$ |
| 5 | $D$ | $(C,D)$ | **3** | $key[F]=\min(4,5)=4$ |
| 6 | $F$ | $(C,F)$ | **4** | All included |

**Total MST Weight = $2 + 4 + 2 + 3 + 4 = \mathbf{15}$** ✔ Same MST as Kruskal's.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Kruskal's | Prim's |
|---|---|---|
| **Greedy Strategy** | Edge-based | Vertex-based |
| **Order of selection** | Globally lightest edge | Lightest edge crossing tree frontier |
| **Data Structure** | Disjoint Set (Union-Find) | Priority Queue / Min-Heap |
| **Graph type** | Better for **sparse** | Better for **dense** |
| **Cycle Handling** | Explicit (via Union-Find) | Implicit (never forms cycle) |
| **Parallelizable?** | Yes — edge sort is parallel-friendly | Harder due to frontier dependency |
| **Output** | Forest (if disconnected) | Always a single tree from root |

---

### 3.5 Correctness Verification (KTU Style)

Both algorithms terminate after exactly $V-1$ edge additions because:

1. Each addition increases the connected component count by exactly 1.
2. A spanning tree on $V$ vertices has exactly $V-1$ edges.
3. By the **cut/cycle property**, the chosen edge never breaks optimality.

By induction on the number of edges added, the partial tree $T_k$ at step $k$ is a **subset of some MST**. At termination $T_{V-1}$ has $V-1$ edges and is a spanning tree, therefore it **is** an MST. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of MST Solvers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inp["Input Graph G=(V,E) with weights"] --> rep{"Representation?"}
    rep -- "Adjacency Matrix" --> primD["Prim's Dense O(V²)"]
    rep -- "Adjacency List" --> primS["Prim's Sparse O(E log V)"]
    rep -- "Edge List" --> kruskal["Kruskal's O(E log V)"]

    kruskal --> s1["Step 1: Sort all edges ascending by weight"]
    s1 --> s2["Step 2: Initialize V disjoint singleton sets"]
    s2 --> s3["Step 3: For each edge in sorted order"]
    s3 --> s4{"FIND(u) ≠ FIND(v)?"}
    s4 -- "Yes → ACCEPT edge" --> s5["UNION the two sets"]
    s4 -- "No → REJECT (forms cycle)" --> s3
    s5 --> s6{"Edges selected = V-1?"}
    s6 -- "No" --> s3
    s6 -- "Yes" --> outMST["Output MST"]

    primS --> p1["Step 1: Pick arbitrary root r, key[r]=0"]
    p1 --> p2["Step 2: Insert all vertices into Min-Heap Q"]
    p2 --> p3["Step 3: EXTRACT-MIN u from Q"]
    p3 --> p4["For each neighbor v of u still in Q"]
    p4 --> p5{"w(u,v) < key[v]?"}
    p5 -- "Yes" --> p6["Decrease-Key v; set parent π[v]=u"]
    p5 -- "No" --> p4
    p6 --> p7{"Q empty?"}
    p7 -- "No" --> p3
    p7 -- "Yes" --> outMST

    primD --> outMST
    style inp fill:#FFE4B5
    style outMST fill:#90EE90
    style rep fill:#ADD8E6
```

### 4.2 Trace Diagram — Kruskal's Execution on Sample Graph

```mermaid
graph LR
    s0["Step 0: All vertices isolated"]
    s1["Step 1: Add (A,E)=2"]
    s2["Step 2: Add (B,C)=2"]
    s3["Step 3: Add (C,D)=3"]
    s4["Step 4: Add (A,B)=4"]
    s5["Step 5: Add (C,F)=4 → MST DONE"]

    s0 -- "2" --> s1
    s1 -- "2" --> s2
    s2 -- "3" --> s3
    s3 -- "4" --> s4
    s4 -- "4" --> s5

    style s0 fill:#FFF8DC
    style s1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style s2 fill:#FFDAB9
    style s3 fill:#FFCBA4
    style s4 fill:#FFB68A
    style s5 fill:#90EE90
```

### 4.3 Resulting MST Topology (Final Tree)

```mermaid
graph TB
    nA((A))
    nB((B))
    nC((C))
    nD((D))
    nE((E))
    nF((F))

    nA --- nE
    nA --- nB
    nB --- nC
    nC --- nD
    nC --- nF

    style nA fill:#FFD700
    style nB fill:#FFD700
    style nC fill:#FFD700
    style nD fill:#FFD700
    style nE fill:#FFD700
    style nF fill:#FFD700
```

> **Edges in MST:** $(A,E), (A,B), (B,C), (C,D), (C,F)$
> **Total Weight:** $2+4+2+3+4 = 15$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Minimum Cost Spanning Tree. State any two applications of MST.**

**Model Answer:**
A Minimum Cost Spanning Tree (MST) of a weighted, connected, undirected graph $G=(V,E)$ is a spanning tree whose sum of edge weights is minimum among all spanning trees. It contains exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges and no cycles.

**Applications:**
1. Designing minimum-cost telecommunication / cable / road networks.
2. Cluster analysis in data mining (single-linkage clustering).
3. *(Any one more accepted:)* Approximation algorithm for Travelling Salesman Problem, VLSI wire layout.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for applications)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State and explain the cut property and cycle property of MST.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Cut Property:** For any cut $(S, V-S)$ of graph $G$, the edge of minimum weight crossing the cut belongs to some MST of $G$.
- **Cycle Property:** For any cycle $C$ in $G$, the edge of maximum weight in $C$ does NOT belong to any MST of $G$.

**Significance:** Cut property guides Prim's algorithm (greedy frontier edge selection); Cycle property guides Kruskal's algorithm (reject heavy edges that close a cycle).

*(3 marks: 1 each for properties + 1 for significance)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3]**
> **Note:** Answer **ONE** of the following: **(A)** OR **(B)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Apply **Prim's algorithm** to find the MST of the graph below. Show the order of edges selected and compute total cost.

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | 6 | $(3,5)$ | 4 |
| $(1,3)$ | 3 | $(4,5)$ | 2 |
| $(1,4)$ | 9 | $(4,6)$ | 5 |
| $(2,4)$ | 7 | $(5,6)$ | 8 |
| $(3,4)$ | 1 | $(2,5)$ | 5 |

*Start Prim's algorithm at vertex **1**.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **pseudocode of Prim's algorithm** using adjacency matrix representation. Analyze its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution A(a) — Prim's Execution Table**

Initial: $key[1]=0$, all other $key=\infty$. Tree $T = \{\}$.

| Iteration | Chosen Vertex $u$ | Edge $(parent, u)$ | Weight | $key$ array after relaxation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | — | $key[1]=0;$ rest $\infty$ |
| 1 | 1 | — | — | $key[2]=6, key[3]=3, key[4]=9$ |
| 2 | 3 | $(1,3)$ | **3** | $key[2]=\min(6,5)=5, key[4]=\min(9,1)=1, key[5]=4$ |
| 3 | 4 | $(3,4)$ | **1** | $key[2]=\min(5,7)=5, key[5]=\min(4,2)=2, key[6]=5$ |
| 4 | 5 | $(4,5)$ | **2** | $key[6]=\min(5,8)=5$ |
| 5 | 2 | $(1,2)$ or $(3,2)$ | **5** | $key[6]=\min(5,5)=5$ |
| 6 | 6 | $(4,6)$ | **5** | All included |

**MST Edges:** $(1,3), (3,4), (4,5), (3,2), (4,6)$
**Total Cost = $3 + 1 + 2 + 5 + 5 = \mathbf{16}$** ✔

---

##### **Solution A(b) — Pseudocode + Complexity**

```
PRIM-MST(G, w, r):
  for each u ∈ G.V:
      key[u] ← ∞
      π[u]   ← NIL
  key[r] ← 0
  Q ← G.V
  while Q ≠ ∅:
      u ← EXTRACT-MIN(Q)            // O(V) with array
      for each v ∈ G.Adj[u]:        // scans row of matrix
          if v ∈ Q and w(u,v) < key[v]:
              π[v] ← u
              key[v] ← w(u,v)
  return {(π[v], v) : v ∈ G.V \ {r}}
```

**Complexity Analysis:**

$$
T(V,E) = \underbrace{O(V)}_{\text{initialization}} + \underbrace{\sum_{u} O(V)}_{\text{EXTRACT-MIN (V times)}} + \underbrace{\sum_{(u,v) \in E} O(1)}_{\text{matrix scan}}
$$

$$
T(V,E) = O(V) + O(V^2) + O(V^2) = O(V^2)
$$

Since for adjacency matrix $E = O(V^2)$, this is optimal for dense graphs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Apply **Kruskal's algorithm** on the same graph as Question A(a). Display the sorted edge list, mark accepted/rejected edges, and compute total MST cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms with respect to **strategy**, **data structure**, **complexity**, and **graph suitability**. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution B(a) — Kruskal's Trace**

**Sorted Edge List (ascending):**

| # | Edge | Weight | FIND(u) vs FIND(v) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $(3,4)$ | 1 | Different | ✅ Accept |
| 2 | $(4,5)$ | 2 | Different | ✅ Accept |
| 3 | $(1,3)$ | 3 | Different | ✅ Accept |
| 4 | $(3,5)$ | 4 | Same (cycle!) | ❌ Reject |
| 5 | $(2,5)$ | 5 | Different | ✅ Accept |
| 6 | $(4,6)$ | 5 | Different | ✅ Accept |
| 7 | $(1,2)$ | 6 | Different | ✅ Accept |
| 8 | $(2,4)$ | 7 | Same (cycle!) | ❌ Reject |
| 9 | $(5,6)$ | 8 | Same (cycle!) | ❌ Reject |
| 10 | $(1,4)$ | 9 | Same (cycle!) | ❌ Reject |

**5 edges selected** ⇒ MST done.

**MST Edges:** $(3,4), (4,5), (1,3), (2,5), (4,6)$
**Total Cost = $1 + 2 + 3 + 5 + 5 = \mathbf{16}$** ✔ (Same MST as Prim's — as expected!)

---

##### **Solution B(b) — Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Kruskal's | Prim's |
|---|---|---|
| **Greedy Unit** | Edge | Vertex |
| **Selection Rule** | Globally minimum edge not forming cycle | Minimum edge crossing current tree frontier |
| **Cycle Check** | Explicit using Union-Find | Implicit (frontier never closes a cycle) |
| **Data Structure** | Edge list + Disjoint Set | Priority Queue + adjacency list/matrix |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(E \log E) = O(E \log V)$ | $O(V^2)$ (matrix) or $O(E \log V)$ (heap) |
| **Suitable For** | Sparse graphs | Dense graphs |
| **Multiple Components** | Produces Minimum Spanning Forest | Needs restart per component |
| **Parallelism** | Easy (edge sorting is parallel) | Hard (depends on growing frontier) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the sorted edge list** before Kruskal's execution → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Not specifying the start vertex** for Prim's — algorithm is **root-dependent only in the order, NOT the MST weight**, but examiners want it stated.
> 3. **Mis-identifying cycles** — always verify with Union-Find or by visual inspection. Many students reject a valid edge by mistake.
> 4. **Quoting wrong complexity:** Many write $O(V \log V)$ for Kruskal's. Correct is $O(E \log E) = O(E \log V)$.
> 5. **Final weight must be summed and displayed explicitly.** Just listing edges without total cost = 1 mark deducted.
> 6. **For dense graphs (matrix version of Prim's):** complexity is $O(V^2)$, **not** $O(E \log V)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ✅ An MST on $n$ vertices has exactly $n-1$ edges and no cycles.
- ✅ Both Kruskal's and Prim's are **Greedy** algorithms, but they are greedy on **different objects** (edge vs vertex).
- ✅ **Cut Property** → drives Prim's. **Cycle Property** → drives Kruskal's.
- ✅ Kruskal's complexity: $O(E \log V)$ — best for **sparse** graphs.
- ✅ Prim's (matrix) complexity: $O(V^2)$ — best for **dense** graphs.
- ✅ Prim's (binary heap) complexity: $O(E \log V)$ — equivalent to Kruskal's asymptotically.
- ✅ Always **sort the edge list first** in Kruskal's — this is the dominant cost.
- ✅ Use **Union-Find with path compression + union by rank** for cycle detection in Kruskal's — gives near-constant amortized time per operation.
- ✅ MST weight is **unique** even when MST itself is not (when edge weights are distinct ⇒ MST is unique).
- ✅ For **disconnected** graphs → Kruskal's yields a Minimum Spanning Forest; Prim's requires restart from each component.
- ✅ MST is the foundation of **2-approximation for TSP** and **single-linkage clustering** in unsupervised ML.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shortest Path Problem - Dijkstra's Algorithm, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shortest Path Problem - Dijkstra's Algorithm, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) Problem** is defined formally as: Given a weighted, directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with a non-negative weight function $w : E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$, and a designated source vertex $s \in V$, find the shortest (minimum total weight) path from $s$ to every other vertex $v \in V$.

**Dijkstra's Algorithm** (proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra, 1956) is a **greedy algorithm** that solves the SSSP problem for graphs with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains a set $S$ of vertices whose final shortest-path distances from the source $s$ have been determined, and repeatedly selects the unvisited vertex $u$ with the smallest tentative distance, adds it to $S$, and "relaxes" all outgoing edges from $u$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dijkstra's algorithm falls under the **Greedy Method** paradigm in Module 3. It is a classic application of the *greedy choice property* and *optimal substructure* — the two pillars required to prove correctness of any greedy algorithm.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine you are standing at **City A** (the source) and want to find the *cheapest flight route* to every other city in a network. You maintain a notebook with the cheapest known fare to each city so far.

- You start by marking City A with cost **0** and all others with cost **∞** (unknown).
- Among the cities you haven't "locked in" yet, you always **bookmark the city with the cheapest current fare** (this is the *greedy choice*).
- Once you "lock in" that city, you check every direct flight from it — and if going *through* this city gives a cheaper fare to any other city, you update your notebook (this is *edge relaxation*).
- You repeat until every city is locked in.

This "always pick the cheapest unlocked city" rule is the heart of Dijkstra's greedy strategy. Because flight fares (edge weights) are never negative, locking in a city is **final** — no future, cheaper route can ever appear for it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-negative weights matter:** If edge weights could be negative, a path that looks "expensive now" might later become cheaper via a negative-weight edge, breaking the greedy choice. This is why Dijkstra's algorithm **fails on negative-weight graphs** (use Bellman-Ford instead).

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Source Vertex ($s$)** | The starting vertex from which shortest paths are computed. |
| **Tentative Distance $d[v]$** | Current best known distance from $s$ to $v$ (may be updated). |
| **Final Distance $\delta(s,v)$** | True shortest-path distance from $s$ to $v$ (locked in $S$). |
| **Edge Relaxation** | The operation of updating $d[v]$ via the test $d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]$. |
| **Priority Queue ($Q$)** | A min-heap data structure keyed on $d[\cdot]$ for efficient min-extraction. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Greedy Choice Property

Dijkstra's algorithm works because the **shortest path problem exhibits optimal substructure**: if the shortest path from $s$ to $v$ goes through intermediate vertex $u$, then the portion from $s$ to $u$ must itself be a shortest path from $s$ to $u$. Furthermore, the *greedy choice property* holds: when a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue (i.e., has the minimum $d[u]$ among unvisited vertices), $d[u]$ is guaranteed to equal the true shortest distance $\delta(s,u)$.

### 2.2 The Edge Relaxation Operation (Core Mechanism)

For a directed edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u,v)$, the **RELAX** operation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if } d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v) \text{ then} \\
\quad d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v) \\
\quad \pi[v] \leftarrow u
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\pi[v]$ is the **predecessor pointer** used to reconstruct the actual shortest-path tree at the end.

### 2.3 Detailed Algorithm Steps

1. **Initialization:** For every vertex $v \in V$, set $d[v] = \infty$, $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$, and a parent pointer. Set $d[s] = 0$ for the source.
2. **Build Min-Priority Queue:** Insert all vertices $V$ into queue $Q$ keyed on $d[\cdot]$.
3. **Main Loop:** While $Q \neq \emptyset$:
   - $u \leftarrow \text{EXTRACT-MIN}(Q)$ — this is the **greedy choice**.
   - $S \leftarrow S \cup \{u\}$ — vertex $u$ is "finalized".
   - For every neighbor $v$ of $u$ in $\text{Adj}(u)$:
     - **RELAX** the edge $(u, v)$ (only if $v \in Q$).
4. **Termination:** When $Q$ becomes empty, the array $d[\cdot]$ holds $\delta(s, \cdot)$ for all reachable vertices.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Initialization** | $d[s] = 0,\ d[v] = \infty\ \forall v \neq s$ | $d$ in same units as edge weights |
| **Relaxation Test** | $d[v] > d[u] + w(u,v)$ | $w(u,v) \geq 0$ required |
| **Relaxation Update** | $d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v)$ | Predecessor $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$ |
| **Triangle Inequality** | $\delta(s,v) \leq \delta(s,u) + w(u,v)$ | Always true for any path |
| **Time — Array** | $T(n) = O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | $\vert V \vert$ = number of vertices |
| **Time — Binary Heap** | $T(n) = O\bigl((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert\bigr)$ | Sparse graph friendly |
| **Time — Fibonacci Heap** | $T(n) = O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Asymptotically optimal |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | For adjacency list + arrays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When asked for complexity, **always state the implementation used** (array vs. binary heap). A bare "$O(V^2)$" without qualification is incomplete and may lose 1 mark. If the graph is sparse, the heap variant is preferred.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GPS Navigation Systems** (Google Maps, Uber) — routing on weighted road networks.
- **Network Routing Protocols** — OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) uses Dijkstra on link-state databases.
- **Social Network Analysis** — degree-of-separation / "Six Degrees of Kevin Bacon" problems.
- **Telecommunications** — finding minimum-latency paths in optical fiber networks.
- **Robotics & Game AI** — pathfinding in grid-based worlds (with uniform costs, equivalent to BFS generalization).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Correctness Proof — Upper-Bound Property (Convergence Lemma)

We prove that on termination, $d[v] = \delta(s,v)$ for all $v \in V$.

**Lemma (Upper-Bound Property):** At any point during execution, $d[v] \geq \delta(s,v)$ for all $v \in V$. Moreover, once a vertex $u$ is extracted and added to $S$, $d[u] = \delta(s,u)$ and remains so.

**Proof Sketch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Base case:} \\
&d[s] = 0 = \delta(s,s) \text{ and } d[v] = \infty \geq \delta(s,v)\ \forall v \neq s. \\
&\textbf{Inductive step (relaxation preserves the bound):} \\
&\text{Suppose } d[v] \geq \delta(s,v) \text{ holds before RELAX}(u,v). \\
&d[u] + w(u,v) \geq \delta(s,u) + w(u,v) \geq \delta(s,v) \quad \text{(by triangle inequality)} \\
&\text{So the update } d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v) \text{ never makes } d[v] < \delta(s,v). \\
&\textbf{Convergence: When } u \text{ is extracted with minimum } d[u]: \\
&\text{Suppose for contradiction that } d[u] > \delta(s,u). \\
&\text{Then there exists a shortest path } s \rightsquigarrow u \text{ whose first edge exits } S. \\
&\text{Let } y \text{ be the first vertex on this path not in } S, \text{ and } x \text{ its predecessor in } S. \\
&d[y] = d[x] + w(x,y) = \delta(s,x) + w(x,y) = \delta(s,y) \quad \text{(since } x \in S \text{ by induction)} \\
&\text{But } d[y] \leq d[u] \text{ since } u \text{ was the minimum. Contradiction.} \\
&\therefore d[u] = \delta(s,u). \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Trace)

**Problem:** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following directed graph with source $s = A$. Edge weights: $A \to B = 4$, $A \to C = 2$, $C \to B = 1$, $C \to D = 4$, $B \to D = 5$, $B \to E = 6$, $D \to E = 2$.

**Initial Table:**

| Vertex | $d[\cdot]$ | $\pi[\cdot]$ | Visited? |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | **0** | NIL | No |
| B | $\infty$ | NIL | No |
| C | $\infty$ | NIL | No |
| D | $\infty$ | NIL | No |
| E | $\infty$ | NIL | No |

**Iteration 1:** Extract-min → $A$ ($d = 0$). Relax edges from $A$:
- $A \to B$: $d[B] = 0 + 4 = 4$, $\pi[B] = A$.
- $A \to C$: $d[C] = 0 + 2 = 2$, $\pi[C] = A$.

**Iteration 2:** Extract-min → $C$ ($d = 2$). Relax edges from $C$:
- $C \to B$: $4 > 2 + 1 = 3$? Yes. Update: $d[B] = 3$, $\pi[B] = C$.
- $C \to D$: $d[D] = 2 + 4 = 6$, $\pi[D] = C$.

**Iteration 3:** Extract-min → $B$ ($d = 3$). Relax edges from $B$:
- $B \to D$: $6 > 3 + 5 = 8$? No. No update.
- $B \to E$: $d[E] = 3 + 6 = 9$, $\pi[E] = B$.

**Iteration 4:** Extract-min → $D$ ($d = 6$). Relax edges from $D$:
- $D \to E$: $9 > 6 + 2 = 8$? Yes. Update: $d[E] = 8$, $\pi[E] = D$.

**Iteration 5:** Extract-min → $E$ ($d = 8$). No outgoing edges (or none to relax).

**Final Result Table:**

| Vertex | $\delta(A, v)$ | Shortest Path |
|---|---|---|
| A | **0** | $A$ |
| B | **3** | $A \to C \to B$ |
| C | **2** | $A \to C$ |
| D | **6** | $A \to C \to D$ |
| E | **8** | $A \to C \to D \to E$ |

### 3.3 Time Complexity Derivation (Binary-Heap Implementation)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Let } n = \vert V \vert, \ m = \vert E \vert. \\
&\text{Initialization: } O(n) \text{ to set } d[\cdot] = \infty \text{ and } O(n) \text{ to build the heap.} \\
&\text{Main loop: } n \text{ iterations of EXTRACT-MIN, each costing } O(\log n). \\
&\text{Inside the loop, each edge is relaxed at most once: } m \text{ relaxations.} \\
&\text{Each successful relaxation triggers DECREASE-KEY, costing } O(\log n). \\
&T(n) = O(n \log n + m \log n) = O\bigl((n + m)\log n\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

For **dense graphs** where $m \approx n^2$, this simplifies to $O(n^2 \log n)$, which is *worse* than the simple $O(n^2)$ array version. Hence the array implementation is preferred for dense graphs; the heap variant wins on sparse graphs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dijkstra's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START]) --> initNode["INITIALIZE distances d v equals infinity and d s equals 0"]
    initNode --> buildQNode["BUILD Min Priority Queue Q from all vertices keyed on d"]
    buildQNode --> checkQ{"Is Q empty?"}
    checkQ -- Yes --> endNode([END: d array holds shortest distances])
    checkQ -- No --> extractNode["EXTRACT MIN vertex u from Q greedy choice"]
    extractNode --> addSNode["ADD u to finalized set S"]
    addSNode --> iterateNode["FOR each neighbor v of u in Adj u"]
    iterateNode --> checkRelax{"d v greater than d u plus w u v?"}
    checkRelax -- Yes --> updateNode["UPDATE d v equals d u plus w u v and set predecessor pi v equals u DECREASE KEY in Q"]
    checkRelax -- No --> nextNeighbor["Move to next neighbor"]
    updateNode --> nextNeighbor
    nextNeighbor --> moreNeighbors{"More neighbors?"}
    moreNeighbors -- Yes --> iterateNode
    moreNeighbors -- No --> checkQ
```

### 4.2 Data Flow Through the Algorithm (Worked Example Trace)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA(("A src d=0")) -->|w=4| nodeB(("B"))
    srcA -->|w=2| nodeC(("C"))
    nodeC -->|w=1| nodeB
    nodeC -->|w=4| nodeD(("D"))
    nodeB -->|w=5| nodeD
    nodeB -->|w=6| nodeE(("E"))
    nodeD -->|w=2| nodeE

    nodeA -.->|step 1 finalized| stepOne["d A = 0"]
    nodeC -.->|step 2 finalized| stepTwo["d C = 2"]
    nodeB -.->|step 3 finalized| stepThree["d B = 3 via C"]
    nodeD -.->|step 4 finalized| stepFour["d D = 6 via C"]
    nodeE -.->|step 5 finalized| stepFive["d E = 8 via D"]
```

### 4.3 Comparison: When to Use Which Shortest-Path Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    decisionStart{"Graph Type?"} --> negCheck{"Negative edge weights?"}
    decisionStart --> allPairs["Need all pairs shortest paths?"}
    decisionStart --> unifCheck{"All edge weights equal?"}

    negCheck -- Yes --> bellman["Use BELLMAN FORD O V times E"]
    negCheck -- No --> dijk["Use DIJKSTRA O V plus E log V with heap"]

    allPairs -- Yes --> fwChoice{"Negative weights?"}
    fwChoice -- Yes --> fwBell["FLOYD WARSHALL O V cubed with neg detection"]
    fwChoice -- No --> fwDijk["Run DIJKSTRA from every source"]

    unifCheck -- Yes --> bfs["Use BFS O V plus E unweighted special case"]
    unifCheck -- No --> dijk
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State Dijkstra's algorithm. Under what condition does it fail?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy algorithm that finds the shortest path from a single source vertex $s$ to all other vertices in a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with non-negative edge weights. It maintains a set $S$ of vertices whose shortest distances are finalized, and repeatedly extracts the unvisited vertex $u$ with minimum tentative distance $d[u]$, then relaxes all outgoing edges from $u$.

It **fails on graphs containing negative-weight edges**, because once a vertex is added to $S$, the algorithm assumes its distance is final — but a negative edge can later produce a shorter path to that vertex, violating the greedy choice property. *(2 marks for statement, 1 mark for the failure condition with reasoning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]**
*What is the role of the priority queue in Dijkstra's algorithm? What data structure alternatives are commonly used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The priority queue (min-heap) is used to efficiently retrieve the unvisited vertex with the **minimum tentative distance** $d[u]$ in each iteration. This is the heart of the greedy selection step.

Common implementations and their complexities:
- **Simple array / unsorted list** — EXTRACT-MIN is $O(V)$, total $O(V^2)$.
- **Binary min-heap** — EXTRACT-MIN and DECREASE-KEY are $O(\log V)$, total $O\bigl((V + E) \log V\bigr)$.
- **Fibonacci heap** — amortized $O(1)$ DECREASE-KEY, total $O(E + V \log V)$.

The choice depends on graph density: arrays for dense graphs, heaps for sparse graphs. *(1 mark for role, 2 marks for data structures with complexities.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme, every Module question in Part B carries an **internal choice**. Two alternatives are provided below. Each is split into 7 + 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** For the directed graph given below, find the shortest path from source vertex $S$ to all other vertices using Dijkstra's algorithm. Show the iteration table clearly. **(7 marks)**

Edge list:
- $S \to A = 6$, $S \to B = 4$
- $B \to A = 1$, $B \to D = 3$, $A \to C = 4$
- $A \to D = 2$, $C \to T = 3$, $D \to T = 5$, $D \to C = 1$

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

| Iteration | Vertex $u$ extracted | $d[S]=0$ | $d[A]$ | $d[B]$ | $d[C]$ | $d[D]$ | $d[T]$ | Updates |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $S$ has no predecessor |
| 1 | $S$ (0) | **0✓** | 6, $\pi=A$ wrong → $\pi=S$ | 4, $\pi=S$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $d[A]=6,\ d[B]=4$ |
| 2 | $B$ (4) | **0✓** | $\min(6, 4+1)=5$ | **4✓** | $\infty$ | $4+3=7$ | $\infty$ | $d[A]=5,\ d[D]=7$ |
| 3 | $A$ (5) | **0✓** | **5✓** | **4✓** | $5+4=9$ | $\min(7, 5+2)=7$ | $\infty$ | $d[C]=9$, $d[D]$ unchanged |
| 4 | $D$ (7) | **0✓** | **5✓** | **4✓** | $\min(9, 7+1)=8$ | **7✓** | $7+5=12$ | $d[C]=8,\ d[T]=12$ |
| 5 | $C$ (8) | **0✓** | **5✓** | **4✓** | **8✓** | **7✓** | $\min(12, 8+3)=11$ | $d[T]=11$ |
| 6 | $T$ (11) | **0✓** | **5✓** | **4✓** | **8✓** | **7✓** | **11✓** | Done |

**Final Shortest Distances & Paths:**
- $S \to S = 0$
- $S \to A = 5$ via $S \to B \to A$
- $S \to B = 4$ via $S \to B$
- $S \to C = 8$ via $S \to B \to D \to C$
- $S \to D = 7$ via $S \to B \to D$
- $S \to T = 11$ via $S \to B \to D \to C \to T$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark for correct initialization; 3 marks for iterations 1–4 with proper relaxation; 2 marks for final table and path reconstruction; 1 mark for stating the shortest path tree clearly.

---

**(b)** Explain the correctness of Dijkstra's algorithm using the **greedy choice property** and the **optimal substructure** of shortest paths. Prove that once a vertex is added to $S$, its distance is final. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Optimal Substructure (2 marks):** A subpath of a shortest path is itself a shortest path. Formally, if the shortest path from $s$ to $v$ is $s \rightsquigarrow u \to v$, then the prefix $s \rightsquigarrow u$ must be a shortest path from $s$ to $u$. Proof: if a shorter $s \rightsquigarrow u$ existed, concatenation with $(u,v)$ would yield a shorter $s \rightsquigarrow v$, contradiction.

**2. Greedy Choice Property (2 marks):** When a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue (the unvisited vertex with minimum $d[u]$), this $d[u]$ is the true shortest distance $\delta(s,u)$. The greedy choice is to finalize $u$ immediately.

**3. Proof of Greedy Choice (3 marks):** Suppose $d[u] > \delta(s,u)$ at extraction time. Consider the true shortest $s \rightsquigarrow u$ path. Let $y$ be the first vertex on this path that is *not yet* in $S$, and let $x$ be its predecessor in $S$. By the upper-bound property and inductive hypothesis, $d[x] = \delta(s,x)$. When $x$ was finalized, we relaxed the edge $(x, y)$, so $d[y] = d[x] + w(x, y) = \delta(s, y)$. Since $y \in Q$ when $u$ is extracted and $d[y] = \delta(s,y) \leq \delta(s,u) < d[u]$, this contradicts $u$ being the minimum. Therefore $d[u] = \delta(s,u)$.

**Mark Distribution:** 2 marks substructure; 2 marks greedy choice statement; 3 marks formal proof.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]*

**(a)** Write the Dijkstra's algorithm pseudocode. Analyze its time complexity using (i) adjacency matrix, and (ii) binary heap implementations. Justify which is better for sparse vs. dense graphs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pseudocode (3 marks):**

```
DIJKSTRA(G, w, s):
  for each vertex v in V[G]:
      d[v] := INFINITY
      pi[v] := NIL
  d[s] := 0
  Q := BUILD_MIN_HEAP(V[G])   // keyed on d[v]

  while Q is not empty:
      u := EXTRACT_MIN(Q)
      S := S ∪ {u}
      for each v in Adj[u]:
          if d[v] > d[u] + w(u, v):
              d[v] := d[u] + w(u, v)
              pi[v] := u
              DECREASE_KEY(Q, v, d[v])
```

**Time Complexity Analysis (3 marks):**

| Implementation | EXTRACT-MIN | DECREASE-KEY | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adjacency Matrix (array scan) | $O(V)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(V^2)$ |
| Binary Heap + Adjacency List | $O(\log V)$ | $O(\log V)$ | $O\bigl((V + E)\log V\bigr)$ |

**Justification (1 mark):**
- For **dense graphs** where $E \approx V^2$, the array version gives $O(V^2)$ which beats the heap's $O(V^2 \log V)$. **Use array.**
- For **sparse graphs** where $E \approx V$, the heap version gives $O(V \log V)$, vastly better than $O(V^2)$. **Use binary heap.**

---

**(b)** Consider a weighted graph with $V = 6$ vertices and $E = 9$ edges. Run Dijkstra's algorithm from source vertex 1 and produce the iteration table. Show why this algorithm is unsuitable for negative-weight edges using a counterexample. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Assume the graph edges are: $1{-}2 (5),\ 1{-}3 (2),\ 3{-}2 (1),\ 3{-}4 (4),\ 2{-}5 (3),\ 4{-}5 (1),\ 4{-}6 (7),\ 5{-}6 (2),\ 2{-}4 (-2)$ — note the negative edge $2 \to 4$ with weight $-2$.

**Iteration Table (4 marks):**

| Iter | Extracted $u$ | $d[1]$ | $d[2]$ | $d[3]$ | $d[4]$ | $d[5]$ | $d[6]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | 1 | **0✓** | 5 | 2 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | 3 | **0✓** | $\min(5, 2+1)=3$ | **2✓** | $2+4=6$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 3 | 2 | **0✓** | **3✓** | **2✓** | $\min(6, 3-2)=1$ | $3+3=6$ | $\infty$ |
| 4 | 4 | **0✓** | **3✓** | **2✓** | **1✓** | $\min(6, 1+1)=2$ | $1+7=8$ |
| 5 | 5 | **0✓** | **3✓** | **2✓** | **1✓** | **2✓** | $\min(8, 2+2)=4$ |
| 6 | 6 | **0✓** | **3✓** | **2✓** | **1✓** | **2✓** | **4✓** |

**Counterexample for Negative Weights (3 marks):**

Consider a tiny graph: vertices $\{A, B, C\}$, edges $A \to B$ (weight 2), $A \to C$ (weight 3), $C \to B$ (weight $-4)$.

- True shortest path $A \to C \to B$ has total weight $3 + (-4) = -1$.
- Dijkstra's algorithm at iteration 1 examines neighbors of $A$: $d[B] = 2$, $d[C] = 3$. It extracts the minimum, which is $B$ with $d[B] = 2$, and **finalizes $B$ with $d[B] = 2$**.
- However, the true shortest distance $\delta(A, B) = -1$ (via $C$). Dijkstra has been "fooled" into locking in a non-optimal value because the negative edge $C \to B$ was not yet visible when $B$ was finalized.
- This is exactly why **Bellman-Ford algorithm** must be used for graphs with negative edge weights.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update the predecessor $\pi[v]$** during relaxation. Examiners check this for path reconstruction marks. *(−1 to 2 marks)*
> 2. **Confusing EXTRACT-MIN with the first unvisited vertex** — always pick the minimum tentative $d[\cdot]$, not the first one in the list.
> 3. **Stating "Dijkstra fails on negative cycles"** — it actually fails on *any* negative edge, even a single one. The more precise statement earns the full mark.
> 4. **Writing complexity without specifying the data structure** — "$O(V^2)$" alone loses a mark; write "$O(V^2)$ with array / $O((V+E)\log V)$ with binary heap".
> 5. **Not showing the predecessor chain** during path reconstruction. After the table, always backtrack from destination to source using $\pi$ pointers.
> 6. **Mixing up "tentative distance" $d[v]$ with "final distance" $\delta(s,v)$** in the proof section — this is a 2-mark penalty in the proof question.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dijkstra = Greedy + Relaxation + Priority Queue.** Memorize this trio; it's a KTU favorite.
- **Precondition:** All edge weights must be **non-negative** ($w(u,v) \geq 0$).
- **Core Invariant:** Once a vertex is extracted from the priority queue, its distance is **final and never changes**.
- **Relaxation Rule:** Update $d[v] \leftarrow d[u] + w(u,v)$ **only if** it strictly decreases $d[v]$; always update $\pi[v] \leftarrow u$ in this case.
- **Time Complexity Trio:**
  - Array → $O(V^2)$ — best for **dense** graphs.
  - Binary heap → $O\bigl((V+E)\log V\bigr)$ — best for **sparse** graphs.
  - Fibonacci heap → $O(E + V \log V)$ — asymptotically optimal.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(V + E)$ for the adjacency list plus distance, predecessor, and visited arrays.
- **Greedy Choice Proof = Cut-and-Paste Argument** — know the lemma: *"When $u$ is min-extracted, $d[u] = \delta(s,u)$."* Use the upper-bound property + optimal substructure.
- **Negative Weights** → Dijkstra fails; use **Bellman-Ford** (handles negatives, detects negative cycles in $O(VE)$).
- **All-Pairs** → run Dijkstra from every source, OR use **Floyd-Warshall** ($O(V^3)$, supports negatives, no cycle detection).
- **Unweighted Graph** → use **BFS** ($O(V + E)$) — Dijkstra degenerates to BFS.
- **Reconstructing the Path:** Follow the $\pi$ pointers backward from destination to source; reverse to get forward path.
- **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:** wrong relaxation direction, not resetting the visited set, off-by-one in iteration counts, missing $\pi$ updates, citing wrong complexity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Programming - Control Abstraction, Optimality Principle, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Programming - Control Abstraction, Optimality Principle, Matrix Chain Multiplication, Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Dynamic Programming (DP) — Formal Definition **Dynamic Programming (DP)** is an algorithmic strategy that systematically solves complex optimization problems by breaking them down into a collection of simpler overlapping subproblems, computing the solution to each subproblem **exactly once**, storing the result in a lookup table (memoization or tabulation), and reusing these stored results whenever the same subproblem re-occurs. It is applied to problems exhibiting two canonical properties: **Optimal Substructure** and **Overlapping Subproblems**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, DP is positioned as a generalisation of the divide-and-conquer paradigm, differing fundamentally in the *reuse* of solutions to subproblems rather than the *fresh* computation of independent subproblems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (verbatim tone):** > *Dynamic Programming is a method for solving optimization problems by combining the solutions to sub-problems. It is applicable when a problem has overlapping sub-problems and optimal substructure. A recursive solution with a table of stored solutions of sub-problems replaces recursion, thereby reducing the exponential complexity to polynomial complexity.* ## 1.2 The Three Engineering Ingredients of any DP Solution | # | Ingredient | Plain Meaning | |---|------------|---------------| | 1 | **Optimal Substructure** | An optimal solution to the problem contains within it optimal solutions to sub-problems. | | 2 | **Overlapping Subproblems** | The recursive algorithm revisits the *same* sub-problem many times — recursion is not a tree, it is a DAG. | | 3 | **Memoisation / Tabulation** | Store every solved sub-problem in a table and look it up instead of recomputing. | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The School Locker Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a student who is asked the *same* difficult math question by ten different classmates in a single day. The naive student solves it from scratch ten times (this is pure recursion, leading to exponential time). The *smart* student, after solving it the first time, **writes the answer on the inside of his locker door**. Every subsequent classmate is directed to the locker. This is **memoisation** — the locker is the table, the door is the constant-time lookup, and the class-mates are the overlapping recursive calls. ## 1.4 Control Abstraction of Dynamic Programming A **Control Abstraction** is a high-level, language-independent, declarative template that hides the mechanism of "store-and-lookup" so that any specific DP problem can be plugged in by supplying four parameters: the state description, the recurrence, the boundary conditions, and the goal. **Generic Pseudocode for the DP Control Abstraction:** ``` ALGORITHM DynamicProgramming(problem) // 1. Characterise the structure of an optimal solution // 2. Recursively define the value of an optimal solution // 3. Compute the value bottom-up (or top-down with memo) // 4. Construct an optimal solution from computed information FOR each subproblem in increasing order of size DO IF subproblem not yet solved THEN Solve subproblem using recurrence Store solution in Table[subproblem] END IF END FOR RETURN Table[goal_state] END ALGORITHM ``` The control abstraction exposes a clean separation between **(a) the *what* of the problem (recurrence relation)** and **(b) the *how* of the computation (table-filling order)**. This is the engineering abstraction that makes DP a *design technique* rather than a *single algorithm*. ## 1.5 The Principle of Optimality (Bellman's Optimality Principle) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bellman's Principle of Optimality (1957):** > *An optimal sequence of decisions has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal decision sequence with respect to the state resulting from the first decision.* In graph-theoretic language: **every sub-path of an optimal path is itself an optimal sub-path** between its endpoints. This principle is the *licence* to use DP — if it fails, DP cannot be applied. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Not every optimal-looking recurrence satisfies the optimality principle. The **Floyd–Warshall shortest path** problem *does* satisfy it, but the **longest simple path** problem *does not* — a sub-path of a longest simple path may not itself be the longest simple path between its endpoints. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Memory Models in DP | Model | Direction | Implementation | KTU Nomenclature | |-------|-----------|----------------|------------------| | **Top-Down (Memoisation)** | Recursion + cache | Recurse; on first entry store result; on re-entry return cached value. | *Recursive-with-memo* | | **Bottom-Up (Tabulation)** | Iterative | Fill a table in a topological order from smallest to largest sub-problem. | *Iterative tabulation* | Both are mathematically equivalent in cost; bottom-up is preferred in KTU answers for its deterministic space and time analysis. ## 2.2 The Four-Step Recipe for Designing any DP Algorithm 1. **Characterise** the structure of an optimal solution. 2. **Recursively define** the value of an optimal solution (write the recurrence). 3. **Compute** the value of an optimal solution (usually bottom-up). 4. **Construct** an optimal solution from the computed information (back-trace using a separate `s[][]` table). ## 2.3 Matrix-Chain Multiplication — Problem Statement > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** Given a chain of matrices $\langle A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n \rangle$ where matrix $A_i$ has dimensions $p_{i-1} \times p_i$, **fully parenthesise** the product $A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$ so that the total number of scalar multiplications is **minimised**. **Key observation:** Parenthesisation only affects the *order* of multiplications, not the *result* (matrix multiplication is associative). However, scalar multiplication cost is **not associative**. For two matrices $A$ ($p \times q$) and $B$ ($q \times r$), the scalar multiplication cost is: $$ \text{cost}(A \cdot B) = p \cdot q \cdot r $$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept | Recurrence / Equation | Boundary Condition | |---|---------|------------------------|--------------------| | 1 | DP memoised cost (top-down) | $T(n) = \sum T(\text{sub-problems}) + O(1)$ per *distinct* sub-problem | Depends on table size | | 2 | Matrix-Chain optimal substructure — split at $k$ | $m[i,j] = \displaystyle\min_{i \le k < j} \bigl\{\, m[i,k] + m[k+1,j] + p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j \,\bigr\}$ | $m[i,i] = 0$ | | 3 | Total scalar multiplications for one split | $\text{cost at split } k = p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j$ | — | | 4 | Number of sub-problems in MCM | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $i < j$ | | 5 | Time complexity of MCM | $O(n^{3})$ | — | | 6 | Space complexity of MCM | $\Theta(n^{2})$ for `m[][]`; $\Theta(n^{2})$ for `s[][]` | — | | 7 | Optimality Principle (Bellman) | $f(\text{state}) = \min / \max$ over choices, evaluated by induction on stage | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on the recurrence:** In KTU valuation, write the recurrence as $m[i,j]$ and **always** state that $m[i,i] = 0$ (a single matrix needs no multiplication). Writing $m[i,j] = 0$ for $i \ge j$ is the universally accepted boundary and is the single most-frequently-tested fact in this topic. ## 2.5 Why the Optimality Principle Holds for Matrix-Chain Multiplication Consider an optimal parenthesisation of $A_i A_{i+1} \cdots A_j$ that splits at some $k$. Then: - The left group $A_i \cdots A_k$ must be parenthesised **optimally** — otherwise substituting a cheaper parenthesisation would lower the total cost (contradiction). - The right group $A_{k+1} \cdots A_j$ must likewise be parenthesised **optimally**. This two-part argument is the *exact* invocation of Bellman's principle and the justification examiners expect in any 7-mark sub-question. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications of DP | Application Domain | Use Case | |--------------------|----------| | **Compiler design** | Optimal evaluation order of expressions / matrix-chain. | | **Bioinformatics** | Sequence alignment (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman). | | **Operations Research** | Inventory control, knapsack, resource allocation. | | **Networking & VLSI** | Optimal wire routing, network flow, shortest paths. | | **Machine Learning** | Viterbi algorithm for HMMs, optimal policy in MDPs. | | **Control systems** | Bellman equation in optimal control. | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Deriving the Matrix-Chain Recurrence **Step 1 — Set up the input.** We are given a chain $\langle A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n \rangle$ with dimensions $A_i$ being $p_{i-1} \times p_i$. The dimension vector is: $$ p = \langle p_0, p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n \rangle $$ **Step 2 — Define the sub-problem.** Let $m[i,j]$ be the **minimum number of scalar multiplications** needed to compute the product $A_i A_{i+1} \cdots A_j$, for $1 \le i \le j \le n$. **Step 3 — Identify the optimal substructure.** Suppose an optimal parenthesisation of $A_i \cdots A_j$ splits the chain between $A_k$ and $A_{k+1}$ for some $k$ with $i \le k < j$. The total cost equals: $$ \text{cost} = \underbrace{m[i,k]}_{\text{left sub-chain}} + \underbrace{m[k+1,j]}_{\text{right sub-chain}} + \underbrace{p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j}_{\text{merge cost of the two results}} $$ The reason the merge cost is $p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j$: the left result is $p_{i-1} \times p_k$, the right result is $p_k \times p_j$, so their product needs $p_{i-1} \cdot p_k \cdot p_j$ scalar multiplications. **Step 4 — Optimise over the split index.** Because the optimal $k$ is unknown, we take the minimum over all valid $k$: $$ m[i,j] \;=\; \min_{i \,\le\, k \,<\, j} \Bigl\{\, m[i,k] \;+\; m[k+1,j] \;+\; p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j \,\Bigr\} $$ **Step 5 — Boundary condition.** A chain of one matrix needs zero multiplications: $$ m[i,i] \;=\; 0 \quad \text{for all } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n $$ This completes the **recurrence specification**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — $n = 4$ Let the four matrices be $A_1, A_2, A_3, A_4$ with dimensions: $$ A_1 : 30 \times 1, \quad A_2 : 1 \times 40, \quad A_3 : 40 \times 10, \quad A_4 : 10 \times 25 $$ Hence the dimension vector is: $$ p = \langle p_0, p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4 \rangle = \langle 30, 1, 40, 10, 25 \rangle $$ We fill the table $m[i,j]$ bottom-up (by increasing chain length $\ell = j - i$). ### Chain length 1 (single matrices): $$ m[1,1] = m[2,2] = m[3,3] = m[4,4] = 0 $$ ### Chain length 2 (pairs): **Compute $m[1,2]$** (split at $k=1$): $$ m[1,2] \;=\; m[1,1] + m[2,2] + p_0\, p_1\, p_2 \;=\; 0 + 0 + 30 \cdot 1 \cdot 40 \;=\; 1200 $$ **Compute $m[2,3]$** (split at $k=2$): $$ m[2,3] \;=\; m[2,2] + m[3,3] + p_1\, p_2\, p_3 \;=\; 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 40 \cdot 10 \;=\; 400 $$ **Compute $m[3,4]$** (split at $k=3$): $$ m[3,4] \;=\; m[3,3] + m[4,4] + p_2\, p_3\, p_4 \;=\; 0 + 0 + 40 \cdot 10 \cdot 25 \;=\; 10000 $$ ### Chain length 3 (triples): **Compute $m[1,3]$** — try $k=1$ and $k=2$: For $k = 1$: $$ m[1,1] + m[2,3] + p_0\, p_1\, p_3 \;=\; 0 + 400 + 30 \cdot 1 \cdot 10 \;=\; 700 $$ For $k = 2$: $$ m[1,2] + m[3,3] + p_0\, p_2\, p_3 \;=\; 1200 + 0 + 30 \cdot 40 \cdot 10 \;=\; 13200 $$ Take the minimum: $$ m[1,3] \;=\; 700 \quad (\text{split at } k = 1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s[1,3] = 1 $$ **Compute $m[2,4]$** — try $k=2$ and $k=3$: For $k = 2$: $$ m[2,2] + m[3,4] + p_1\, p_2\, p_4 \;=\; 0 + 10000 + 1 \cdot 40 \cdot 25 \;=\; 11000 $$ For $k = 3$: $$ m[2,3] + m[4,4] + p_1\, p_3\, p_4 \;=\; 400 + 0 + 1 \cdot 10 \cdot 25 \;=\; 650 $$ Take the minimum: $$ m[2,4] \;=\; 650 \quad (\text{split at } k = 3) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s[2,4] = 3 $$ ### Chain length 4 (full chain $A_1 A_2 A_3 A_4$): **Compute $m[1,4]$** — try $k=1, 2, 3$: For $k = 1$: $$ m[1,1] + m[2,4] + p_0\, p_1\, p_4 \;=\; 0 + 650 + 30 \cdot 1 \cdot 25 \;=\; 1400 $$ For $k = 2$: $$ m[1,2] + m[3,4] + p_0\, p_2\, p_4 \;=\; 1200 + 10000 + 30 \cdot 40 \cdot 25 \;=\; 41200 $$ For $k = 3$: $$ m[1,3] + m[4,4] + p_0\, p_3\, p_4 \;=\; 700 + 0 + 30 \cdot 10 \cdot 25 \;=\; 8200 $$ Take the minimum: $$ m[1,4] \;=\; 1400 \quad (\text{split at } k = 1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s[1,4] = 1 $$ ### Final Result Tables The completed `m` table is: $$ m \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1200 & 700 & 1400 \\ - & 0 & 400 & 650 \\ - & - & 0 & 10000 \\ - & - & - & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ The completed `s` table (split indices) is: $$ s \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} - & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ - & - & 2 & 3 \\ - & - & - & 3 \\ - & - & - & - \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Optimal Parenthesisation (Back-Trace) Starting at $s[1,4] = 1$, the chain splits into $(A_1)$ and $(A_2 A_3 A_4)$. Then $s[2,4] = 3$ splits the right group into $(A_2 A_3)$ and $(A_4)$. Then $s[2,3] = 2$ splits the middle into $(A_2)(A_3)$. Therefore: $$ \text{Optimal parenthesisation} \;=\; \bigl(\, A_1 \, (A_2 \, A_3) \, A_4 \,\bigr) $$ with minimum scalar multiplication cost: $$ m[1,4] \;=\; 1400 $$ ## 3.3 Time & Space Complexity Derivation The `m` table has $\binom{n}{2} + n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$ entries. For each entry $m[i,j]$ with $j - i \ge 1$, we evaluate $j - i$ candidate splits $k$. The total work is: $$ T(n) \;=\; \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} (n - \ell) \cdot \ell $$ Expanding: $$ T(n) \;=\; \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} \bigl( n\ell - \ell^{2} \bigr) \;=\; n \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} \ell - \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} \ell^{2} $$ Using the standard summation identities: $$ \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} \ell \;=\; \frac{(n-1)n}{2}, \qquad \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} \ell^{2} \;=\; \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} $$ we obtain: $$ T(n) \;=\; n \cdot \frac{(n-1)n}{2} - \frac{(n-1)n(2n-1)}{6} $$ $$ T(n) \;=\; \frac{n^{2}(n-1)}{2} - \frac{n(n-1)(2n-1)}{6} $$ $$ T(n) \;=\; \frac{n(n-1)}{6} \bigl[\, 3n - (2n - 1) \,\bigr] \;=\; \frac{n(n-1)(n+1)}{6} $$ $$ T(n) \;=\; \frac{n^{3} - n}{6} \;\in\; \Theta(n^{3}) $$ The space is dominated by the two $\Theta(n^{2})$ tables `m[][]` and `s[][]`: $$ S(n) \;=\; \Theta(n^{2}) $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Hint:** The summation $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} (n-\ell)\ell = \dfrac{n^{3}-n}{6}$ is **the** standard derivation that KTU examiners expect in any 7-mark complexity sub-part. Memorise the three summation identities: $\sum \ell$, $\sum \ell^{2}$, and $\sum \ell^{3}$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 DP Control-Abstraction — Top-Down vs. Bottom-Up Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Problem P] --> B{Choose Paradigm} B -- Overlapping Subproblems? --> C[YES: Use DP] B -- NO --> D[Use Divide and Conquer] C --> E[Define State Description S] E --> F[Derive Recurrence R for sub-problems] F --> G[Identify Boundary Conditions] G --> H{Implementation Choice} H -- Top-Down --> I[Memoised Recursion
HashMap or Table lookup
on demand] H -- Bottom-Up --> J[Tabulation Loop
Fill Table in increasing
order of sub-problem size] I --> K[Combine Sub-solutions
using Recurrence R] J --> K K --> L[Back-trace using s-table
to recover structure] L --> M[Return Optimal Value and Parenthesisation] M --> N[END] ``` ## 4.2 Matrix-Chain Multiplication — Sub-problem Dependency DAG The directed acyclic graph (DAG) below shows the dependency of sub-problems $m[i,j]$ on smaller sub-problems. Arrows point *from* a parent sub-problem *to* its children. The `m[1,4]` entry is the goal. ```mermaid flowchart TD goal["m[1,4] - full chain"] a12["m[1,2]"] a13["m[1,3]"] a14["m[1,4]"] a23["m[2,3]"] a24["m[2,4]"] a34["m[3,4]"] a11["m[1,1] = 0"] a22["m[2,2] = 0"] a33["m[3,3] = 0"] a44["m[4,4] = 0"] goal --> a13 goal --> a24 goal --> a12 goal --> a34 goal --> a11 goal --> a22 goal --> a33 goal --> a44 a13 --> a12 a13 --> a23 a13 --> a11 a13 --> a22 a13 --> a33 a24 --> a23 a24 --> a34 a24 --> a22 a24 --> a33 a24 --> a44 a12 --> a11 a12 --> a22 a23 --> a22 a23 --> a33 a34 --> a33 a34 --> a44 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the DAG:** Each $m[i,j]$ node depends only on $m[i,k]$ and $m[k+1,j]$ for $i \le k < j$. This DAG has $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ nodes, and the longest chain in the DAG has length $n$ — justifying the bottom-up table-filling order from chain-length $1$ up to $n$. ## 4.3 Back-Trace Tree for the Worked Example The back-trace tree for $m[1,4]$ with $s[1,4] = 1$, $s[2,4] = 3$, $s[2,3] = 2$. ```mermaid flowchart TD root["A1 . A2 . A3 . A4"] n1["A1"] n2["A2 . A3 . A4"] n2a["A2 . A3"] n2b["A4"] n2a1["A2"] n2a2["A3"] root -- split at k=1 --> n1 root -- split at k=1 --> n2 n2 -- split at k=3 --> n2a n2 -- split at k=3 --> n2b n2a -- split at k=2 --> n2a1 n2a -- split at k=2 --> n2a2 ``` The leaves of the tree, read left-to-right, yield the optimal parenthesisation: $$ (A_1 \, (A_2 \, A_3) \, A_4) \quad \text{with cost } 1400 $$ ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MCM Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input Reader
p vector, n] --> B[Table Allocator
m n x n, s n x n] B --> C[Chain-Length Loop
for L = 2 to n] C --> D[Inner Split Loop
for k = i to j-1] D --> E[Cost Evaluator
q = m i k + m k+1 j + p i-1 p k p j] E --> F[Comparator
select min q, store k in s] F --> G{All entries filled?} G -- No --> D G -- Yes --> H[Back-Trace Module
recursive print using s] H --> I[Output
Minimum cost m 1 n
and Parenthesised Form] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State and explain the Principle of Optimality. Why is it essential for applying Dynamic Programming?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Principle of Optimality**, formulated by Richard Bellman, states: > *An optimal sequence of decisions has the property that, whatever the initial state and the first decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with respect to the state resulting from the first decision.* It is essential for Dynamic Programming because it is the *licence* that allows us to claim: *the optimal solution to a problem can be assembled from the optimal solutions of its independent sub-problems*. Without this principle, the recursive decomposition may not preserve optimality, and the table-filling strategy would yield a sub-optimal or incorrect answer. Examples that satisfy it: shortest path, matrix-chain multiplication, knapsack. Example that does **not**: longest simple path. ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Write the control abstraction of Dynamic Programming and differentiate it from Divide-and-Conquer in two points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** ``` DP(problem): for each sub-problem s in topological order: if Table[s] undefined: Table[s] = Recurrence( Table of children of s ) return Table[goal] ``` | Aspect | Divide and Conquer | Dynamic Programming | |--------|-------------------|---------------------| | Sub-problem structure | Independent / disjoint | Overlapping | | Re-computation | Each sub-problem solved fresh | Sub-problems stored in a table | | Cost benefit | Reduces $a^{n} \to n^{k}$ when $a > 1$ | Reduces $2^{n} \to$ polynomial | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a) Solve the Matrix-Chain Multiplication problem for the chain of 5 matrices with the following dimensions and find the minimum number of scalar multiplications along with the optimal parenthesisation.** $$ A_1: 5 \times 4, \quad A_2: 4 \times 6, \quad A_3: 6 \times 2, \quad A_4: 2 \times 7, \quad A_5: 7 \times 3 $$ Hence state the recurrence relation used. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution Dimension vector: $p = \langle 5, 4, 6, 2, 7, 3 \rangle$, $n = 5$. **Recurrence used:** $$ m[i,j] \;=\; \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } i = j \\[4pt] \min\limits_{i \le k < j}\bigl\{\, m[i,k] + m[k+1,j] + p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j \,\bigr\} & \text{if } i < j \end{cases} $$ **Length-1 entries:** all zeros. **Length-2 entries:** $$ m[1,2] = 5 \cdot 4 \cdot 6 = 120, \quad m[2,3] = 4 \cdot 6 \cdot 2 = 48 $$ $$ m[3,4] = 6 \cdot 2 \cdot 7 = 84, \quad m[4,5] = 2 \cdot 7 \cdot 3 = 42 $$ **Length-3 entries:** $m[1,3]$: try $k=1$: $0 + 48 + 5\cdot 4\cdot 2 = 88$; try $k=2$: $120 + 0 + 5\cdot 6\cdot 2 = 180$. So $m[1,3]=88$, $s[1,3]=1$. $m[2,4]$: $k=2$: $0 + 84 + 4\cdot 6\cdot 7 = 252$; $k=3$: $48 + 0 + 4\cdot 2\cdot 7 = 104$. So $m[2,4]=104$, $s[2,4]=3$. $m[3,5]$: $k=3$: $0 + 42 + 6\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 78$; $k=4$: $84 + 0 + 6\cdot 7\cdot 3 = 210$. So $m[3,5]=78$, $s[3,5]=3$. **Length-4 entries:** $m[1,4]$: $k=1$: $0 + 104 + 5\cdot 4\cdot 7 = 244$; $k=2$: $120 + 84 + 5\cdot 6\cdot 7 = 414$; $k=3$: $88 + 0 + 5\cdot 2\cdot 7 = 158$. So $m[1,4]=158$, $s[1,4]=3$. $m[2,5]$: $k=2$: $0 + 78 + 4\cdot 6\cdot 3 = 150$; $k=3$: $48 + 42 + 4\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 114$; $k=4$: $104 + 0 + 4\cdot 7\cdot 3 = 188$. So $m[2,5]=114$, $s[2,5]=3$. **Length-5 entry (final answer):** $m[1,5]$: $k=1$: $0 + 114 + 5\cdot 4\cdot 3 = 174$; $k=2$: $120 + 78 + 5\cdot 6\cdot 3 = 288$; $k=3$: $88 + 42 + 5\cdot 2\cdot 3 = 160$; $k=4$: $158 + 0 + 5\cdot 7\cdot 3 = 263$. So $m[1,5]=158$, $s[1,5]=3$. **Back-trace:** $s[1,5]=3 \Rightarrow (A_1 A_2 A_3)(A_4 A_5)$. $s[1,3]=1 \Rightarrow (A_1)(A_2 A_3)$. $s[4,5]=4 \Rightarrow (A_4)(A_5)$. **Optimal parenthesisation:** $$ \bigl(\, A_1 \, (A_2 A_3) \, (A_4 A_5) \,\bigr) $$ **Minimum scalar multiplications = 158.** --- **(b) Write the general algorithm (pseudocode) for the Matrix-Chain Multiplication problem using bottom-up dynamic programming. Analyse its time and space complexity in terms of $n$, the number of matrices. State and justify the principle of optimality for this problem. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Pseudocode (Bottom-Up DP):** ``` MATRIX-CHAIN-ORDER(p, n): // p[0..n] is the dimension vector for i = 1 to n: m[i,i] = 0 for L = 2 to n: // L = chain length for i = 1 to n - L + 1: j = i + L - 1 m[i,j] = +infinity for k = i to j - 1: q = m[i,k] + m[k+1,j] + p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j] if q < m[i,j]: m[i,j] = q s[i,j] = k return m and s ``` **PRINT-OPTIMAL-PARENS(s, i, j):** ``` if i == j: print "A" + i else: print "(" PRINT-OPTIMAL-PARENS(s, i, s[i,j]) PRINT-OPTIMAL-PARENS(s, s[i,j]+1, j) print ")" ``` **Complexity Analysis:** The triple-nested loop structure is: - Outer loop $L$: runs $n-1$ times. - Middle loop $i$: runs $n-L+1$ times. - Inner loop $k$: runs $L-1$ times. Total iterations: $$ \sum_{L=2}^{n} (n - L + 1)(L - 1) \;=\; \sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1} (n - \ell)\ell \;=\; \frac{n^{3} - n}{6} \;\in\; \Theta(n^{3}) $$ Hence **time complexity = $O(n^{3})$**. The tables $m$ and $s$ each have $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ entries, so **space complexity = $\Theta(n^{2})$**. **Optimality Principle Justification:** Let an optimal parenthesisation of $A_i \cdots A_j$ split at $k$. Then: - The product $A_i \cdots A_k$ must itself be parenthesised optimally — otherwise replacing it with a cheaper parenthesisation would strictly reduce the total cost, contradicting optimality of the full chain. - Symmetrically, $A_{k+1} \cdots A_j$ must be parenthesised optimally. - The merging cost of the two results is fixed at $p_{i-1}\,p_k\,p_j$, depending only on the split $k$ and the boundary dimensions. Therefore the global optimum is the minimum over all valid $k$ of the sum of two optimal sub-problems plus a fixed merge cost. This is **exactly Bellman's Principle of Optimality** applied to the chain of matrices. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a) Consider a chain of 6 matrices with dimensions:** $$ A_1: 10 \times 4, \; A_2: 4 \times 15, \; A_3: 15 \times 8, \; A_4: 8 \times 9, \; A_5: 9 \times 20, \; A_6: 20 \times 6 $$ **Find the minimum number of scalar multiplications and the corresponding optimal parenthesisation. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution Dimension vector: $p = \langle 10, 4, 15, 8, 9, 20, 6 \rangle$, $n = 6$. **Recurrence:** $m[i,j] = \displaystyle\min_{i \le k < j}\{ m[i,k] + m[k+1,j] + p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j \}$; $m[i,i] = 0$. **Length-1:** $m[i,i]=0$ for $i=1,\ldots,6$. **Length-2:** $$ m[1,2]=10\cdot4\cdot15=600, \quad m[2,3]=4\cdot15\cdot8=480 $$ $$ m[3,4]=15\cdot8\cdot9=1080, \quad m[4,5]=8\cdot9\cdot20=1440 $$ $$ m[5,6]=9\cdot20\cdot6=1080 $$ **Length-3:** $m[1,3]$: $k=1 \to 0+480+10\cdot4\cdot8 = 800$; $k=2 \to 600+0+10\cdot15\cdot8=1800$. So $m[1,3]=800$, $s[1,3]=1$. $m[2,4]$: $k=2 \to 0+1080+4\cdot15\cdot9=1620$; $k=3 \to 480+0+4\cdot8\cdot9=768$. So $m[2,4]=768$, $s[2,4]=3$. $m[3,5]$: $k=3 \to 0+1440+15\cdot8\cdot20=3840$; $k=4 \to 1080+0+15\cdot9\cdot20=3780$. So $m[3,5]=3780$, $s[3,5]=4$. $m[4,6]$: $k=4 \to 0+1080+8\cdot9\cdot6=1512$; $k=5 \to 1440+0+8\cdot20\cdot6=2400$. So $m[4,6]=1512$, $s[4,6]=4$. **Length-4:** $m[1,4]$: $k=1 \to 0+768+10\cdot4\cdot9=1128$; $k=2 \to 600+1080+10\cdot15\cdot9=3030$; $k=3 \to 800+0+10\cdot8\cdot9=1520$. So $m[1,4]=1128$, $s[1,4]=1$. $m[2,5]$: $k=2 \to 0+3780+4\cdot15\cdot20=4980$; $k=3 \to 480+1440+4\cdot8\cdot20=2560$; $k=4 \to 768+0+4\cdot9\cdot20=1488$. So $m[2,5]=1488$, $s[2,5]=4$. $m[3,6]$: $k=3 \to 0+1512+15\cdot8\cdot6=2232$; $k=4 \to 1080+1080+15\cdot9\cdot6=3975$; $k=5 \to 3780+0+15\cdot20\cdot6=5580$. So $m[3,6]=2232$, $s[3,6]=3$. **Length-5:** $m[1,5]$: $k=1 \to 0+1488+10\cdot4\cdot20=2288$; $k=2 \to 600+3780+10\cdot15\cdot20=7380$; $k=3 \to 800+1440+10\cdot8\cdot20=3840$; $k=4 \to 1128+0+10\cdot9\cdot20=2928$. So $m[1,5]=2288$, $s[1,5]=1$. $m[2,6]$: $k=2 \to 0+2232+4\cdot15\cdot6=2592$; $k=3 \to 480+1512+4\cdot8\cdot6=2688$; $k=4 \to 768+1080+4\cdot9\cdot6=2064$; $k=5 \to 1488+0+4\cdot20\cdot6=1968$. So $m[2,6]=1968$, $s[2,6]=5$. **Length-6 (final):** $m[1,6]$: $k=1 \to 0+1968+10\cdot4\cdot6=2208$; $k=2 \to 600+2232+10\cdot15\cdot6=3732$; $k=3 \to 800+1512+10\cdot8\cdot6=2792$; $k=4 \to 1128+1080+10\cdot9\cdot6=2748$; $k=5 \to 2288+0+10\cdot20\cdot6=3488$. So $m[1,6]=2208$, $s[1,6]=1$. **Back-trace:** $s[1,6]=1 \Rightarrow (A_1)(A_2 A_3 A_4 A_5 A_6)$. $s[2,6]=5 \Rightarrow (A_2 A_3 A_4 A_5)(A_6)$. $s[2,5]=4 \Rightarrow (A_2 A_3 A_4)(A_5)$. $s[2,4]=3 \Rightarrow (A_2 A_3)(A_4)$. $s[2,3]=2 \Rightarrow (A_2)(A_3)$. **Optimal parenthesisation:** $$ \bigl(\, A_1 \, ( (A_2 A_3) \, A_4 ) \, A_5 \, A_6 \,\bigr) $$ **Minimum scalar multiplications = 2208.** --- **(b) Explain the general strategy of Dynamic Programming. Discuss the control abstraction of DP. List and briefly explain any two applications of DP other than Matrix-Chain Multiplication. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **General Strategy of Dynamic Programming:** Dynamic Programming is a *bottom-up* (or memoised top-down) *algorithmic design technique* for optimisation problems that exhibit **overlapping sub-problems** and **optimal sub-structure**. The general strategy is: 1. **Characterise** the structure of an optimal solution. 2. **Recursively define** the value of an optimal solution. 3. **Compute** the value of an optimal solution, typically in a bottom-up table. 4. **Construct** an optimal solution from the computed information. **Control Abstraction of DP:** A control abstraction is a *generic* algorithmic template that hides the mechanism of "store-and-lookup" so that any DP problem can be instantiated by providing (a) the state, (b) the recurrence, and (c) the boundary. The general pseudocode is: ``` DYNAMIC-PROGRAMMING(P, n): for each sub-problem size s from 1 to n: for each sub-problem of size s: if Table[sub-problem] not yet defined: Table[sub-problem] = Recurrence(Table of children) return Table[goal-state] ``` **Two Applications of DP (other than MCM):** | # | Application | Recurrence Sketch | |---|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | **0/1 Knapsack** | $V[i,w] = \max\{\, V[i-1,w], \; V[i-1, w-w_i] + v_i \,\}$; $V[0,w]=0$, $V[i,0]=0$ | | 2 | **Longest Common Subsequence (LCS)** | $L[i,j] = L[i-1,j-1]+1$ if $X_i = Y_j$, else $L[i,j] = \max\{L[i-1,j], L[i,j-1]\}$ | | 3 | **Floyd–Warshall All-Pairs Shortest Path** | $D^{(k)}[i,j] = \min\{\, D^{(k-1)}[i,j], \; D^{(k-1)}[i,k] + D^{(k-1)}[k,j] \,\}$ | | 4 | **Optimal Binary Search Tree** | $e[i,j] = \min_{i \le r \le j}\{ e[i,r-1] + e[r+1,j] + w[i,j] \}$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Recurrence boundary forgotten:** Writing $m[i,j] = \ldots$ without stating $m[i,i] = 0$ costs 1 mark. Always state the boundary explicitly. > 2. **Off-by-one in indices:** KTU questions use 1-indexed matrices ($A_1, A_2, \ldots$). Using 0-indexed arrays without conversion loses marks. > 3. **Forgetting to back-trace:** When the question asks for *parenthesisation*, the candidate must show the back-trace from the `s` table. A correct `m[1,n]` value alone earns only 4–5 of 7 marks. > 4. **Confusing MCM with matrix multiplication:** The product order is associative; only the *cost* differs. Examiners penalise students who try to multiply the matrices to "verify". > 5. **Not writing the recurrence explicitly:** In 14-mark questions, a full 7-mark sub-part almost always requires stating the recurrence *before* solving. Skipping the recurrence costs 1–2 marks. > 6. **Confusing top-down memoisation with bottom-up tabulation:** Both give the same answer, but the time/space analysis differs in implementation detail. The complexity is $O(n^3)$ for both. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 DP Section:** - **Dynamic Programming** is a design technique for problems with **optimal substructure** + **overlapping sub-problems**. - **Bellman's Principle of Optimality:** every suffix of an optimal sequence is itself optimal with respect to the state it inherits. - **DP Control Abstraction** has 4 parameters: (i) state description, (ii) recurrence, (iii) boundary, (iv) goal state. It hides the store-and-lookup mechanism. - **Two memory models:** top-down (memoisation) and bottom-up (tabulation). Both are equivalent; bottom-up is the KTU default. - **Matrix-Chain Multiplication (MCM)** solves for the parenthesisation that minimises scalar multiplications. - **MCM Recurrence:** $m[i,j] = \displaystyle\min_{i \le k < j}\{ m[i,k] + m[k+1,j] + p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j \}$; $m[i,i]=0$. - **MCM Cost of one split** at $k$ is $p_{i-1}\, p_k\, p_j$ (dimensions of the two results being multiplied). - **Back-trace** uses a separate `s[i,j]` table that records the $k$ that achieved the minimum. - **Time complexity of MCM:** $O(n^{3})$ — derived from $\sum_{\ell=1}^{n-1}(n-\ell)\ell = \dfrac{n^{3}-n}{6}$. - **Space complexity of MCM:** $\Theta(n^{2})$ for both `m` and `s`. - **Optimality principle holds** for MCM because any non-optimal sub-parenthesisation could be swapped in to strictly reduce total cost — a contradiction. - **Optimality principle fails** for the **longest simple path** problem (sub-path of a longest simple path is not necessarily a longest simple sub-path). - **Number of sub-problems in MCM:** $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ strictly above the diagonal; total entries including diagonal = $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$. - **Real-world DP applications:** compiler optimisation, sequence alignment (bioinformatics), shortest paths (Floyd–Warshall), Viterbi decoding (HMMs), Bellman equation (control theory), resource allocation (knapsack). - **Common answer-writing pitfalls:** missing boundary, no back-trace, wrong indexing, conflating MCM with matrix multiplication itself. > [!NOTE] > **Module 3 Closing Note:** The MCM problem is the canonical example used in KTU to introduce DP because the recurrence is non-trivial, the back-trace is verifiable, and the $O(n^{3})$ analysis is derivable in a 14-mark answer. Master this problem first, then extend the same control abstraction to **Optimal Binary Search Tree**, **All-Pairs Shortest Path (Floyd–Warshall)**, and **Travelling Salesman Problem (Bit-Mask DP)** in subsequent modules.

topics.title: All Pairs Shortest Path Algorithm - Floyd-Warshall Algorithm, Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** All Pairs Shortest Path Algorithm - Floyd-Warshall Algorithm, Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **All-Pairs Shortest Path (APSP)** problem asks for the shortest path between **every pair of vertices** in a weighted, directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with weight function $w: E \to \mathbb{R}$. The **Floyd-Warshall Algorithm**, discovered by Robert Floyd in 1962 (with foundations from Bernard Roy and Stephen Warshall), is a **Dynamic Programming** algorithm that solves APSP in $\Theta(V^3)$ time using a simple, elegant 5-line triple-nested loop.

Unlike the repeated invocation of single-source algorithms (e.g., Dijkstra $|V|$ times), Floyd-Warshall works even when **negative edge weights are present**, provided **no negative-weight cycle is reachable** from any source vertex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):**
> The Floyd-Warshall algorithm computes shortest path lengths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted directed graph using a dynamic programming formulation where the shortest path between any two vertices is considered via a sequence of intermediate vertices, with the recurrence:
> $$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\bigl(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\ D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\bigr)$$
> where $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ represents the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using only vertices $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ as intermediates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kerala Road Trip Planner

Imagine you are the head of Kerala Tourism planning a road network across 6 major cities: **Trivandrum, Kochi, Thrissur, Kozhikode, Kannur, Kasaragod**. You want to know the **shortest driving distance between every pair of cities** to publish a tourist route map.

**Naive Approach**: Send 6 separate surveyors, each starting from one city, and use the fastest single-source method (Dijkstra). This is like running Dijkstra $|V| = 6$ times — works, but heavy.

**Smart Approach (Floyd-Warshall)**: You build a 6×6 table. For each potential **"stopover city" $k$"** (Trivandrum, Kochi, …, Kasaragod, in order), ask: *"For every pair of cities $(i, j)$, is it shorter to go $i \to k \to j$ than what I have recorded so far?"* If yes, update the table.

This is exactly Floyd-Warshall — it **progressively allows more cities to be used as intermediate stops**, refining shortest distances monotonically.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Vocabulary (Must Memorize for KTU Board):**
> - **APSP**: All-Pairs Shortest Path — find $\delta(i, j)$ for *all* $(i, j) \in V \times V$.
> - **Intermediate Vertex**: A vertex that lies on the path *strictly between* the source and destination (not the endpoints themselves).
> - **Distance Matrix $D^{(k)}$**: A $V \times V$ matrix where entry $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ is the shortest path from $i$ to $j$ using only vertices in the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ as intermediate stops.
> - **Negative Cycle**: A cycle whose total edge weight is negative. Floyd-Warshall **detects** but cannot compute correct distances if one exists.
> - **Transitive Closure**: A related application where $w[i][j] \in \{0, 1\}$ — finds reachability between all pairs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Intuition — The "Stopover City" Idea

The Floyd-Warshall algorithm is based on a simple observation about path structure:

> Any shortest path $p$ from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$ can be decomposed as:
> **$i \to \dots \to k \to \dots \to j$**, where every intermediate vertex on $p$ belongs to some *prefix* set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$.

We don't know $k$ in advance. So we **try every $k$ from $1$ to $|V|$ as the "highest-numbered" intermediate vertex** and update accordingly. This is the essence of **Dynamic Programming** — solving a problem by combining optimal solutions to overlapping subproblems.

## 2.2 The Recurrence Relation (THE Most Important Formula)

Let the vertices be labeled $V = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$. Define $D^{(k)}[i][j]$ as the shortest path weight from $i$ to $j$ using *only* intermediate vertices from the set $\{1, 2, \dots, k\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Floyd-Warshall Master Recurrence:**
> $$D^{(k)}[i][j] = \min\left(D^{(k-1)}[i][j],\ D^{(k-1)}[i][k] + D^{(k-1)}[k][j]\right)$$

**Why does this work?**
- For a shortest path $i \leadsto j$ using intermediates from $\{1, \dots, k\}$, there are **two cases**:
  - **Case 1**: Vertex $k$ is **NOT** used as an intermediate. Then the path is the same as one using $\{1, \dots, k-1\}$ intermediates: cost = $D^{(k-1)}[i][j]$.
  - **Case 2**: Vertex $k$ **IS** used as an intermediate.
