The construction of $E'$ can be done in $O(n^2)$ time by iterating over all unordered pairs of vertices and checking edge membership using an adjacency-matrix lookup (or hash-set lookup in $O(1)$ expected time per pair).

### Step 3 — Define the Size Parameter

$$
k' \;=\; n - k
$$

This computation is $O(1)$ on integer operands of size $O(\log n)$.

### Step 4 — Output

The reduction function $f$ outputs:

$$
f(G, k) \;=\; (\overline{G},\ n - k) \;=\; (G', k')
$$

The total running time of $f$ is therefore $O(n^2)$, which is polynomial in the input size $\langle G, k \rangle = n + m + \log k$.

## 3.2 Correctness of the Reduction (The Big Equivalence)

We must show the bidirectional equivalence:

$$
(G, k) \in \text{CLIQUE} \;\iff\; (\overline{G},\, n-k) \in \text{VERTEX\text{-}COVER}
$$

We prove each direction using Lemma A and Lemma B.

### Direction $\Rightarrow$ (Forward)

Assume $G$ has a clique $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert = k$.

1. By **Lemma A**, since $S$ is a clique in $G$, it is an **independent set** in $\overline{G}$.
2. By **Lemma B**, since $S$ is an independent set in $\overline{G}$, the set $V \setminus S$ is a **vertex cover** of $\overline{G}$.
3. The size of $V \setminus S$ is:
   $$\vert V \setminus S \vert \;=\; n - \vert S \vert \;=\; n - k \;=\; k'$$
4. Therefore, $\overline{G}$ has a vertex cover $V \setminus S$ of size exactly $k'$, so $(\overline{G}, k') \in \text{VERTEX\text{-}COVER}$. $\blacksquare$

### Direction $\Leftarrow$ (Reverse)

Assume $\overline{G}$ has a vertex cover $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert = k' = n - k$.

1. By **Lemma B**, since $C$ is a vertex cover of $\overline{G}$, the set $S = V \setminus C$ is an **independent set** in $\overline{G}$.
2. By **Lemma A**, since $S$ is an independent set in $\overline{G}$, the set $S$ is a **clique** in $G$.
3. The size of $S$ is:
   $$\vert S \vert \;=\; n - \vert C \vert \;=\; n - k' \;=\; n - (n - k) \;=\; k$$
4. Therefore, $G$ has a clique $S$ of size exactly $k$, so $(G, k) \in \text{CLIQUE}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Compositional View — A "Pipelined" Reduction Chain

Many KTU problems test the **chain of reductions**. Recall the classic Karp sequence:

$$
\text{3\text{-}SAT} \;\le_p\; \text{IS} \;\le_p\; \text{CLIQUE} \;\le_p\; \text{VERTEX\text{-}COVER} \;\le_p\; \text{SET\text{-}COVER}
$$

The CLIQUE $\to$ VERTEX-COVER reduction is the **middle link**, often paired with the INDEPENDENT-SET reduction:

$$
\text{CLIQUE} \;\le_p\; \text{IS} \;\le_p\; \text{VERTEX\text{-}COVER}
$$

where IS $\to$ VC uses the identity reduction $(G, k) \mapsto (G, n-k)$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (for Examination Practice)

Let $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and:

$$
E \;=\; \bigl\{\{1,2\},\{1,3\},\{2,3\},\{3,4\},\{4,5\}\bigr\}
$$

Let $k = 3$. The subgraph induced by $S = \{1, 2, 3\}$ is a triangle $K_3$, so $(G, 3)$ is a YES-instance of CLIQUE.

**Construct $\overline{G}$:** The missing edges are:
$$
\overline{E} \;=\; \bigl\{\{1,4\},\{1,5\},\{2,4\},\{2,5\},\{3,5\}\bigr\}
$$

So $\overline{G} = (V, \overline{E})$.

**New size parameter:** $k' = 5 - 3 = 2$.

**Verify:** In $\overline{G}$, the set $C = V \setminus S = \{4, 5\}$ has size 2. Check it is a vertex cover of $\overline{G}$:
- Edge $\{1,4\}$: $4 \in C$ ✓
- Edge $\{1,5\}$: $5 \in C$ ✓
- Edge $\{2,4\}$: $4 \in C$ ✓
- Edge $\{2,5\}$: $5 \in C$ ✓
- Edge $\{3,5\}$: $5 \in C$ ✓

All edges are covered, so $(\overline{G}, 2)$ is a YES-instance of VERTEX COVER, confirming the reduction. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note (KTU):** When you write the proof, **always state the reduction function $f$ explicitly first**, then prove both directions of equivalence using the lemmas. A common student mistake is to write only the forward direction and forget the reverse — losing 4–5 marks out of 14.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of the Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["CLIQUE Instance: Graph G of n vertices, parameter k"] --> buildComp["Step 1: Build Complement Graph G-bar"]
    buildComp --> adjMatrix["Step 2: Iterate over all O-n-squared pairs of V, flip edge membership using adjacency matrix"]
    adjMatrix --> setKprime["Step 3: Set k-prime equal to n minus k"]
    setKprime --> outputInst["Output: G-bar and k-prime form the VERTEX COVER Instance"]
    outputInst --> solver["Polynomial-time VERTEX COVER Solver hypothetical or oracle"]
    solver --> invert["Invert the answer: YES becomes NO and NO becomes YES via complement duality"]
    invert --> finalAns["Final Answer for original CLIQUE Instance"]
```

## 4.2 Logical Flow of the Equivalence Proof

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    direction TB
    A["ASSUME: G has clique S of size k"] --> B["Lemma A: S is independent set in G-bar"]
    B --> C["Lemma B: V minus S is vertex cover in G-bar"]
    C --> D["Size: V minus S has n minus k elements equal to k-prime"]
    D --> E["CONCLUSION FORWARD: G-bar has vertex cover of size k-prime"]

    F["ASSUME: G-bar has vertex cover C of size k-prime"] --> G["Lemma B: V minus C is independent set in G-bar"]
    G --> H["Lemma A: V minus C is clique in G"]
    H --> I["Size: V minus C has n minus k-prime elements equal to k"]
    I --> J["CONCLUSION REVERSE: G has clique of size k"]

    E -.-> K["BIDIRECTIONAL EQUIVALENCE HOLDS"]
    J -.-> K
    K --> L["REDUCTION IS CORRECT and POLYNOMIAL TIME"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Three Key Set Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph G_Universe["Graph G: Vertices V equals 1 to n"]
        direction TB
        Clique_S["CLIQUE S in G, size k"]:::cliqueStyle
        IndSet_Gbar["INDEPENDENT SET in G-bar of same size k"]:::indStyle
        VC_Gbar["VERTEX COVER C in G-bar, size n minus k"]:::vcStyle
    end

    Clique_S -- "Lemma A: complement graph" --> IndSet_Gbar
    IndSet_Gbar -- "Lemma B: complement set V minus S" --> VC_Gbar
    VC_Gbar -- "Inverse of Lemmas A and B" --> Clique_S

    classDef cliqueStyle fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
    classDef indStyle fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#003399,stroke-width:2px,color:#001a66
    classDef vcStyle fill:#d6ffd6,stroke:#006622,stroke-width:2px,color:#003311
```

## 4.4 Karp's 21 NP-Complete Problems — Clique and VC in Context

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SAT["SAT and 3-SAT"]:::root --> VC1["VERTEX COVER"]
    SAT --> Clique1["CLIQUE"]
    SAT --> IS1["INDEPENDENT SET"]
    IS1 -- "Identity map, k to n minus k" --> VC1
    Clique1 -- "Complement graph G to G-bar, k to n minus k" --> VC1
    VC1 --> SetCover["SET COVER"]
    Clique1 --> SetCover
    IS1 --> SetCover

    classDef root fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#aa8800,stroke-width:3px,color:#554400
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows depict the direction of polynomial-time many-one reductions. Every arrow $A \to B$ means $A \le_p B$. The transitive closure of these arrows is what makes Karp's 1972 result a foundational milestone in theoretical computer science.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, 3 marks]**
>
> **Q1.** Define the **CLIQUE** decision problem. Why is it in NP?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CLIQUE decision problem takes as input an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, and asks whether $G$ contains a clique of size at least $k$ — i.e., a subset $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \ge k$ such that every pair of vertices in $S$ is joined by an edge. It belongs to NP because, given a candidate set $S$, a deterministic verifier can check in polynomial time $O(k^2)$ that all $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs in $S$ are edges of $G$. **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 marks]**
>
> **Q2.** Define the **VERTEX COVER** decision problem. State its relationship with the **Independent Set** problem.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The VERTEX COVER decision problem takes an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, and asks whether $G$ has a vertex cover of size at most $k$ — i.e., a subset $C \subseteq V$ with $\vert C \vert \le k$ such that every edge in $E$ has at least one endpoint in $C$. **Relationship with Independent Set:** A set $I \subseteq V$ is an independent set in $G$ if and only if $V \setminus I$ is a vertex cover of $G$. Hence, $I$ is an independent set of size $k$ iff $V \setminus I$ is a vertex cover of size $n - k$. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / 14 marks, KTU Pattern]**
>
> **Q.A (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the formal definition of NP-Completeness. State and prove that CLIQUE is NP-Complete by showing that it is in NP and by Karp's reduction from a known NP-Complete problem.
>
> **Q.A (b) [7 marks]:** Prove that the **VERTEX COVER** problem is NP-Complete by giving a polynomial-time many-one reduction from CLIQUE. State the lemmas used and prove both directions of the equivalence.

### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 marks

**Definition of NP-Completeness (2 marks):**
A decision problem $\Pi$ is NP-Complete iff:
1. $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$ — any "yes" instance has a polynomial-time verifiable certificate.
2. $\Pi$ is NP-Hard — every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to $\Pi$. Karp's theorem (1972) lists 21 such problems, including SAT, 3-SAT, CLIQUE, VERTEX COVER, INDEPENDENT SET, and SET COVER.

**CLIQUE is in NP (2 marks):** Given a candidate subset $S$ of size $k$, the verifier checks all $\binom{k}{2}$ pairs and confirms each is an edge of $G$. Time: $O(k^2) \le O(n^2)$, polynomial.

**CLIQUE is NP-Hard (3 marks):** By Karp's reduction from SAT (or 3-SAT), one can construct a polynomial-time mapping from any Boolean formula $\phi$ to a graph $G_\phi$ and an integer $k_\phi$ such that $\phi$ is satisfiable iff $G_\phi$ has a $k_\phi$-clique. Since SAT is in NP and 3-SAT is NP-Complete, CLIQUE inherits NP-Hardness. (Full chain: 3-SAT $\le_p$ INDEPENDENT SET $\le_p$ CLIQUE via complement graph.)

### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Statement of Reduction (2 marks):** We exhibit a polynomial-time mapping $f: \text{CLIQUE} \to \text{VERTEX\text{-}COVER}$ defined by:

$$
f(G, k) \;=\; (\overline{G}, n - k)
$$

where $\overline{G}$ is the complement graph on the same vertex set $V$ with edges exactly where $G$ does **not** have them.

**Forward direction (2 marks):** Suppose $G$ has a $k$-clique $S$. By Lemma A, $S$ is an independent set in $\overline{G}$. By Lemma B, $V \setminus S$ is a vertex cover of $\overline{G}$ of size $n - k$. Hence $(\overline{G}, n-k)$ is a YES-instance.

**Reverse direction (2 marks):** Suppose $\overline{G}$ has a vertex cover $C$ of size $\le n - k$. By Lemma B, $V \setminus C$ is an independent set in $\overline{G}$ of size $\ge k$. By Lemma A, $V \setminus C$ is a clique in $G$ of size $\ge k$. Hence $(G, k)$ is a YES-instance.

**Polynomiality (1 mark):** Computing $\overline{G}$ takes $O(n^2)$ time; computing $n - k$ takes $O(1)$. Total polynomial in input size.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state both directions of the equivalence** (loses 4 marks).
> 2. **Confusing independent set and clique** in the complement graph. **Mnemonic:** *complement flips both "edges ↔ non-edges" and "clique ↔ independent set"*.
> 3. **Writing $k' = k$** instead of $k' = n - k$ — instant 0 in the size parameter sub-step.
> 4. **Not verifying that the reduction runs in polynomial time** — mandatory final step.
> 5. **Writing the reduction as $\le_p^{T}$ (Turing) instead of $\le_p$ (many-one)** — KTU expects the standard Karp many-one reduction.

---

## Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 14 marks, KTU Pattern]**
>
> **Q.B (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the CLIQUE problem with an example graph. State the **Complement-Clique Duality Lemma** and prove it formally.
>
> **Q.B (b) [7 marks]:** Using the **Complement-Clique Duality Lemma** and the **Independent Set–Vertex Cover Duality**, prove that CLIQUE $\le_p$ VERTEX COVER. Illustrate the reduction on a 5-vertex example.

### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 marks

**CLIQUE definition with example (3 marks):** A clique of size $k$ in $G = (V, E)$ is a subset $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert = k$ such that for every distinct $u, v \in S$, $\{u, v\} \in E$. Example: In the path graph $P_5$ with edges $\{1,2\}, \{2,3\}, \{3,4\}, \{4,5\}$, the maximum clique has size 2 (e.g., $\{2,3\}$); the empty graph trivially has clique of size 1.

**Statement of Lemma A (2 marks):** A subset $S \subseteq V$ is a clique in $G$ if and only if $S$ is an independent set in $\overline{G}$.

**Proof of Lemma A (2 marks):**
$(\Rightarrow)$ Suppose $S$ is a clique in $G$. Then for every distinct $u, v \in S$, $\{u,v\} \in E$. By definition of $\overline{G}$, $\{u, v\} \notin \overline{E}$. Hence no edge of $\overline{G}$ has both endpoints in $S$, so $S$ is independent in $\overline{G}$.
$(\Leftarrow)$ Suppose $S$ is independent in $\overline{G}$. Then for every distinct $u, v \in S$, $\{u, v\} \notin \overline{E}$, so $\{u, v\} \in E$. Hence $S$ is a clique in $G$. $\blacksquare$

### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Reduction function (2 marks):** Given $(G, k)$ for CLIQUE, output $(\overline{G}, n - k)$ for VERTEX COVER.

**Equivalence proof (3 marks):** The composition of Lemma A and Lemma B gives:

$$
S \text{ is a } k\text{-clique in } G \;\xLeftrightarrow{\text{Lemma A}}\; S \text{ is a } k\text{-independent set in } \overline{G} \;\xLeftrightarrow{\text{Lemma B}}\; V \setminus S \text{ is a vertex cover of } \overline{G} \text{ of size } n - k.
$$

**Illustrative 5-vertex example (2 marks):** As worked in §3.4, with $V = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$, $E = \{\{1,2\},\{1,3\},\{2,3\},\{3,4\},\{4,5\}\}$, the triangle $\{1,2,3\}$ is a 3-clique in $G$. The complement $\overline{G}$ has edges $\overline{E} = \{\{1,4\},\{1,5\},\{2,4\},\{2,5\},\{3,5\}\}$, and $C = \{4, 5\}$ is a 2-vertex cover of $\overline{G}$, confirming the reduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Clique & Vertex Cover NP-Completeness**

- ✅ **CLIQUE Instance:** $(G, k)$ — does $G$ contain a complete subgraph on $k$ vertices?
- ✅ **VERTEX COVER Instance:** $(G, k)$ — does $G$ contain $k$ vertices touching every edge?
- ✅ **Reduction Map (Master Equation):** $f(G, k) = (\overline{G}, n - k)$ where $n = \vert V \vert$.
- ✅ **Two Pillars of the Proof:** **Lemma A** (complement-clique duality) and **Lemma B** (IS-VC duality).
- ✅ **Complement Construction Cost:** $O(n^2)$ — polynomial.
- ✅ **Both Problems ∈ NP:** certificate verification is polynomial in $n, m, k$.
- ✅ **CLIQUE is in Karp's Original 21 NP-Complete List (1972).**
- ✅ **VERTEX COVER is in Karp's Original 21 NP-Complete List (1972).**
- ✅ **Reduction Chain (very high-yield):** $3\text{-SAT} \le_p \text{INDEPENDENT SET} \le_p \text{CLIQUE} \le_p \text{VERTEX COVER} \le_p \text{SET COVER}$.
- ✅ **Common Examiner Traps:** Forgetting reverse direction, writing $k' = k$, confusing IS and Clique in the complement, not stating polynomiality.
- ✅ **Mnemonic to Remember:** *"Flip the graph, flip the question, flip the size."* — Complement $G$ to get $\overline{G}$, transform Clique question to VC question, set $k' = n - k$.
- ✅ **Practical Implications:** No known polynomial-time algorithm; all known exact algorithms are exponential ($O(2^{n/3})$ for VC, $O(n^{\,k \cdot \omega/3})$ for Clique). The **PCP theorem** implies that even approximating VC within factor $2 - \epsilon$ is NP-Hard.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approximation Algorithms - Bin Packing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approximation Algorithms - Bin Packing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Bin Packing Problem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BIN PACKING PROBLEM (Decision Form):**
> Given a finite set of $n$ items $I = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_n\}$ with associated sizes $s(i_k) \in (0, 1]$ for each item, and a collection of identical **bins of unit capacity (1.0)**, determine the **minimum number of bins** required to pack all items such that the total size of items in any bin does not exceed 1.

Let us formalize this using optimization notation. Let the **optimal** number of bins be denoted as $OPT(I)$, and the number of bins used by an approximation algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ on input $I$ be $\mathcal{A}(I)$. Then:

$$\mathcal{A}(I) \leq \rho \cdot OPT(I)$$

where $\rho$ is the **approximation ratio** of algorithm $\mathcal{A}$. The Bin Packing Problem is a classical **NP-hard** combinatorial optimization problem, so polynomial-time exact algorithms are not believed to exist (unless $P = NP$). This makes it a prime candidate for the design and analysis of **approximation algorithms**.

### 1.2 Intuition: The Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **REAL-WORLD ANALOGY — Moving Boxes:**
> Imagine you are shifting houses. You have a pile of differently weighted items (books, clothes, utensils) and a stack of identical cardboard boxes. Each box can hold at most 1.0 unit of weight. The naive question is: *What is the smallest number of boxes you will need?*

A human, without computer assistance, would instinctively sort heavy items first and then try to **fit** lighter items into whatever space remains. This natural human heuristic is precisely what the **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** algorithm does. The Bin Packing Problem captures exactly this real-world logistics challenge.

### 1.3 Why Approximation?

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Problem Class | NP-Hard |
| Decision Version | NP-Complete |
| Standard Metric | Number of bins used |
| Key Lower Bound | $\lceil \sum s(i_k) \rvert$ |

- **Constant metrics**: $s(i_k) \le 1$ for every item, bin capacity $= 1$.
- **Total size lower bound**: Any packing must use at least $\sum_{k=1}^{n} s(i_k)$ bins, hence $OPT(I) \ge \lceil S \rceil$ where $S = \sum s(i_k)$.

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test whether you know that Bin Packing is **NP-Hard** (not just NP-Complete), because the decision version "Can we pack in $k$ bins?" is NP-Complete, but the optimization version (find minimum $k$) is NP-Hard.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Bin Packing Algorithms

The bin packing algorithms are broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Online Algorithms** (process items in given order, no foresight)
   - Next Fit (NF)
   - First Fit (FF)
   - Best Fit (BF)

2. **Offline Algorithms** (sort items first by size, decreasing)
   - First Fit Decreasing (FFD)
   - Best Fit Decreasing (BFD)

### 2.2 Next Fit (NF) Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEXT FIT (NF) — Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. Start with an empty bin $B_1$ (the *current bin*).
> 2. For each item $x$ in the given order:
>    - If $x$ fits in the current bin, place $x$ in it.
>    - Otherwise, **close** the current bin, open a new bin $B_{k+1}$, and place $x$ in this new bin.
> 3. Return the number of bins used.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — only one bin is "open" at a time.

**Approximation Ratio:** NF is a **2-approximation** algorithm, i.e., $NF(I) \le 2 \cdot OPT(I)$.

### 2.3 First Fit (FF) Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRST FIT (FF) — Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. Maintain all currently opened bins: $B_1, B_2, \ldots$
> 2. For each item $x$ in the given order:
>    - Place $x$ into the **first bin** $B_k$ (smallest $k$) that has enough remaining capacity.
>    - If no existing bin can hold $x$, open a new bin $B_{k+1}$ and place $x$ in it.
> 3. Return the total number of bins used.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — for each item, we may scan up to $O(n)$ bins.

**Approximation Ratio:** FF is a **1.7-approximation** algorithm.

### 2.4 Best Fit (BF) Algorithm

**Best Fit (BF) — Algorithm Steps:**
1. For each item $x$, place it into the bin that has the **smallest remaining capacity** that can still accommodate $x$ (i.e., the *tightest* fit).
2. If no bin can hold $x$, open a new bin.

This is also a **1.7-approximation** with time complexity $O(n \log n)$ if implemented with balanced BSTs, or $O(n^2)$ naively.

### 2.5 First Fit Decreasing (FFD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIRST FIT DECREASING (FFD) — Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. **Sort** all items in **non-increasing order** of size: $s(i_1) \ge s(i_2) \ge \cdots \ge s(i_n)$.
> 2. Apply the standard **First Fit** algorithm on this sorted list.
> 3. Return the number of bins used.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n + n^2) = O(n^2)$.

**Approximation Ratio:** FFD is a $\frac{11}{9}$-approximation algorithm.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Memorize These!**

| Algorithm | Type | Approximation Ratio | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Next Fit (NF) | Online | 2 | $O(n)$ |
| First Fit (FF) | Online | $\frac{17}{10} = 1.7$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Best Fit (BF) | Online | $\frac{17}{10} = 1.7$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| First Fit Decreasing (FFD) | Offline | $\frac{11}{9} \approx 1.222$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Best Fit Decreasing (BFD) | Offline | $\frac{11}{9}$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Lower Bound | — | $OPT(I) \ge \max \left(\lceil S \rceil, \; \max_i s(i_i)\right)$ | — |
| Total Size | — | $S = \sum_{k=1}^{n} s(i_k)$ | — |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Bin Packing models appear in:
- **Logistics**: Truck loading, container packing, warehouse management.
- **Cloud Computing**: Virtual machine allocation to physical servers.
- **VLSI Design**: Placing circuit components on chips of limited width.
- **Network Routing**: Packing data packets into transmission frames.
- **Operating Systems**: Memory allocation in fixed-size pages/frames.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Next Fit (NF)

**Input:** Item sizes $= \{0.5, 0.7, 0.3, 0.6, 0.4, 0.5, 0.2, 0.8\}$

| Step | Item | Current Bin | Fits? | Action | Bins Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | $B_1$ (cap left: 1.0) | Yes | Place in $B_1$ | 1 |
| 2 | 0.7 | $B_1$ (cap left: 0.5) | No (0.7 > 0.5) | Close $B_1$, open $B_2$ | 2 |
| 3 | 0.3 | $B_2$ (cap left: 1.0) | Yes | Place in $B_2$ | 2 |
| 4 | 0.6 | $B_2$ (cap left: 0.7) | Yes | Place in $B_2$ | 2 |
| 5 | 0.4 | $B_2$ (cap left: 0.1) | No | Close $B_2$, open $B_3$ | 3 |
| 6 | 0.5 | $B_3$ (cap left: 1.0) | Yes | Place in $B_3$ | 3 |
| 7 | 0.2 | $B_3$ (cap left: 0.5) | Yes | Place in $B_3$ | 3 |
| 8 | 0.8 | $B_3$ (cap left: 0.3) | No | Close $B_3$, open $B_4$ | 4 |

**Total bins used by NF:** $NF(I) = 4$.

**Optimal solution** (lower bound): $\sum s(i) = 0.5+0.7+0.3+0.6+0.4+0.5+0.2+0.8 = 4.0$, so $OPT(I) = 4$. Here $NF = OPT$ (best case).

### 3.2 Proof that NF is a 2-Approximation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THEOREM:** For any input $I$, $NF(I) \le 2 \cdot OPT(I)$.

**Proof (Direct Construction):**

Consider two consecutive bins produced by Next Fit: $B_k$ and $B_{k+1}$. The crucial observation is:

- $B_{k+1}$ was opened **only because** the next item $x$ did not fit in $B_k$, i.e., $s(x) > (\text{remaining capacity of } B_k)$.
- The total size in $B_k$ plus the size of $x$ is greater than 1.0.

Therefore, for every pair of consecutive bins, the sum of items in those two bins exceeds **1.0**.

$$\text{Size}(B_k) + \text{Size}(B_{k+1}) > 1.0$$

Now sum this inequality over all pairs of consecutive bins. If $NF$ uses $m$ bins, we have $\lfloor m/2 \rfloor$ pairs:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} s(i_j) = S \ge \frac{NF(I)}{2}$$

This follows because the total size of all items $S$ is bounded by the sum over pairs, and each pair has combined size > 1.0. Formally:

$$S > \left\lfloor \frac{m}{2} \right\rfloor \implies 2S \ge m = NF(I)$$

Since $OPT(I) \ge S$ (trivially, as the total size must be packed), we obtain:

$$NF(I) \le 2 \cdot S \le 2 \cdot OPT(I)$$

**Conclusion:** NF achieves an approximation ratio of $\rho = 2$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — First Fit Decreasing (FFD)

**Input:** Item sizes $= \{0.8, 0.5, 0.4, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1\}$

**Step 1 — Sort in non-increasing order:**
$$\text{Sorted} = \{0.8, 0.5, 0.4, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply First Fit:**

| Step | Item | Try $B_1$ (cap) | Try $B_2$ (cap) | Try $B_3$ (cap) | Action | Bins |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8 | 1.0 → 0.2 | — | — | $B_1 \gets 0.8$ | 1 |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.2 (no) | 1.0 → 0.5 | — | $B_2 \gets 0.5$ | 2 |
| 3 | 0.4 | 0.2 (no) | 0.5 → 0.1 | — | $B_2 \gets +0.4$ | 2 |
| 4 | 0.4 | 0.2 (no) | 0.1 (no) | 1.0 → 0.6 | $B_3 \gets 0.4$ | 3 |
| 5 | 0.3 | 0.2 (no) | 0.1 (no) | 0.6 → 0.3 | $B_3 \gets +0.3$ | 3 |
| 6 | 0.2 | 0.2 → 0.0 | 0.1 (no) | 0.3 → 0.1 | $B_1 \gets +0.2$ | 3 |
| 7 | 0.2 | 0.0 (no) | 0.1 (no) | 0.1 (no) | Open $B_4 \gets 0.2$ | 4 |
| 8 | 0.1 | 0.0 (no) | 0.1 → 0.0 | 0.1 (no) | $B_2 \gets +0.1$ | 4 |

**Final Bins:**
- $B_1 = \{0.8, 0.2\}$ → total 1.0
- $B_2 = \{0.5, 0.4, 0.1\}$ → total 1.0
- $B_3 = \{0.4, 0.3, 0.2\}$ (wait, 0.4+0.3+0.2 = 0.9... let me redo)

Let me redo Step 5-6 more carefully:

| Step | Item | $B_1$ (rem) | $B_2$ (rem) | $B_3$ (rem) | Action | Bins |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 0.4 | 0.2 no | 0.1 no | open 1.0 | $B_3 \gets 0.4$ | 3 |
| 5 | 0.3 | 0.2 no | 0.1 no | 0.6 yes | $B_3 \gets +0.3$, rem 0.3 | 3 |
| 6 | 0.2 | 0.2 yes | 0.1 no | 0.3 yes | $B_1 \gets +0.2$, rem 0.0 | 3 |
| 7 | 0.2 | 0.0 no | 0.1 no | 0.3 yes | $B_3 \gets +0.2$, rem 0.1 | 3 |
| 8 | 0.1 | 0.0 no | 0.1 yes | 0.1 no | $B_2 \gets +0.1$, rem 0.0 | 3 |

**Corrected Final Bins:**
- $B_1 = \{0.8, 0.2\}$ → total $1.0$ ✓
- $B_2 = \{0.5, 0.4, 0.1\}$ → total $1.0$ ✓
- $B_3 = \{0.4, 0.3, 0.2\}$ → total $0.9$

**Total bins used by FFD:** $FFD(I) = 3$.

**Optimal:** $S = 0.8+0.5+0.4+0.4+0.3+0.2+0.2+0.1 = 2.9$, so $OPT(I) = 3$. **FFD = OPT** in this case.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When Does NF Fail Most?

The **worst-case** for Next Fit is when items alternate between just over 0.5 and just under 0.5:

$$\text{Input} = \{0.5+\epsilon, \; 0.5-\epsilon, \; 0.5+\epsilon, \; 0.5-\epsilon, \ldots\}$$

For $n$ items in this alternating sequence, NF uses $n$ bins, but the **optimal** solution packs pairs together: $OPT = \lceil n/2 \rceil$. The ratio tends to 2 as $n \to \infty$.

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{NF(I)}{OPT(I)} = 2$$

This confirms that the **approximation bound of 2 is tight** for NF.

### 3.5 Derivation: FFD Approximation Bound $\frac{11}{9}$

The bound $FFD(I) \le \frac{11}{9} \cdot OPT(I) + c$ (where $c$ is a small constant) was proven by Dósa & Sgall in 2013, improving earlier results. The proof relies on intricate **weighting functions** that assign "weights" to bins based on their content and analyzes cases based on the structure of items. The proof is non-trivial and is typically tested only at the conceptual level in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Inequality for KTU:**
> $$FFD(I) \le \frac{11}{9} \cdot OPT(I) + 6/9$$

The full proof involves analyzing 5–6 distinct cases based on the size of the largest item and the structure of the final few bins in the FFD packing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    stepI -- NO --> stepJ
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Bin Packing Problem. Why is it considered NP-hard? What is the role of approximation algorithms in solving it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bin Packing Problem** is defined as: Given a set of $n$ items with sizes $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \in (0, 1]$ and a collection of bins each of unit capacity, find the **minimum number of bins** required to pack all items such that the sum of sizes in any bin $\le 1$.

It is **NP-hard** because:
- Its **decision version** (Can all items be packed into $k$ bins?) is in the class **NP-Complete**.
- The optimization version is at least as hard as the NP-Complete decision version.

Since no polynomial-time exact algorithm is known (unless P = NP), **approximation algorithms** are designed that produce near-optimal solutions in polynomial time, with a provable worst-case approximation ratio.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for NP-hard justification, 1 for role of approximation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Next Fit and First Fit algorithms for Bin Packing. Which one gives a better approximation ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Next Fit (NF) | First Fit (FF) |
|---|---|---|
| Bin Consideration | Only the **current** bin | **All open** bins so far |
| Memory | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ bins to track |
| Time Complexity | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Approximation Ratio | 2 (worse) | 1.7 (better) |

**First Fit (FF)** gives a better approximation ratio ($1.7 < 2$) because it considers all available bins for placement, leading to tighter packing. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Next Fit** algorithm for the Bin Packing problem with a suitable example. Prove that it is a 2-approximation algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following set of item sizes: $\{0.5, 0.4, 0.7, 0.3, 0.6, 0.2, 0.5, 0.4\}$. Apply the **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** algorithm and determine the number of bins used. Verify if your answer is optimal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm Explanation (3 marks):**

The Next Fit algorithm processes items one by one in the given order. It maintains a single *current bin*. For each item:
- If the item fits in the current bin, it is placed there.
- If it does not fit, the current bin is sealed and a new bin is opened.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**

Take items: $\{0.5, 0.4, 0.8, 0.3, 0.7\}$

- Start $B_1 = \{0.5\}$, remaining = 0.5.
- Item 0.4: fits, $B_1 = \{0.5, 0.4\}$, remaining = 0.1.
- Item 0.8: does not fit (0.8 > 0.1). Close $B_1$, open $B_2 = \{0.8\}$, remaining = 0.2.
- Item 0.3: does not fit (0.3 > 0.2). Close $B_2$, open $B_3 = \{0.3\}$.
- Item 0.7: does not fit (0.7 > 0.7? No — 0.7 fits! $B_3 = \{0.3, 0.7\}$, remaining = 0.0).

**Total bins = 3.**

**Proof of 2-Approximation (2 marks):**

Consider any two consecutive bins $B_k$ and $B_{k+1}$ in the NF packing. Bin $B_{k+1}$ was opened because the first item placed in it did not fit in $B_k$. This means:

$$\text{Size}(B_k) + s(\text{first item of } B_{k+1}) > 1 \implies \text{Size}(B_k) + \text{Size}(B_{k+1}) > 1$$

Summing over all $\lfloor NF/2 \rfloor$ pairs:

$$S = \sum_{i} s_i > \frac{NF(I)}{2} \implies NF(I) < 2S \le 2 \cdot OPT(I)$$

Since $S \le OPT(I)$ (the total size lower bound), the result follows. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Sort in non-increasing order (1 mark)**

Original: $\{0.5, 0.4, 0.7, 0.3, 0.6, 0.2, 0.5, 0.4\}$
Sorted:   $\{0.7, 0.6, 0.5, 0.5, 0.4, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2\}$

**Step 2: Apply First Fit (4 marks)**

| Item | $B_1$ (rem) | $B_2$ (rem) | $B_3$ (rem) | Action | Bins |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.7 | 0.3 (used) | — | — | Place in $B_1$ | 1 |
| 0.6 | 0.3 (no) | 0.4 (used) | — | Place in $B_2$ | 2 |
| 0.5 | 0.3 (no) | 0.4 (no) | 0.5 (used) | Place in $B_3$ | 3 |
| 0.5 | 0.3 (no) | 0.4 (no) | 0.5 (yes) | $B_3 \gets +0.5$ | 3 |
| 0.4 | 0.3 (no) | 0.4 (yes) | 0.0 (no) | $B_2 \gets +0.4$ | 3 |
| 0.4 | 0.3 (no) | 0.0 (no) | 0.0 (no) | Open $B_4 \gets 0.4$ | 4 |
| 0.3 | 0.3 (yes) | 0.0 (no) | 0.0 (no) | $B_1 \gets +0.3$ | 4 |
| 0.2 | 0.0 (no) | 0.0 (no) | 0.0 (no) | Open $B_5 \gets 0.2$ | 5 |

**Final bins:** $B_1=\{0.7,0.3\}$, $B_2=\{0.6,0.4\}$, $B_3=\{0.5,0.5\}$, $B_4=\{0.4\}$, $B_5=\{0.2\}$

**FFD Result = 5 bins** (1 mark)

**Step 3: Verify Optimality (1 mark)**

$$S = 0.7+0.6+0.5+0.5+0.4+0.4+0.3+0.2 = 3.6 \implies OPT \ge 4$$

But $FFD = 5 > 4$, so this packing is **not optimal**. A better packing exists: $OPT(I) = 4$ (achievable, e.g., $\{0.7,0.3\}, \{0.6,0.4\}, \{0.5,0.4,0.2\}, \{0.5\}$ wait this is 4 bins). Let me recheck — actually with proper packing: $\{0.7,0.3\}, \{0.6,0.4\}, \{0.5,0.5\}, \{0.4,0.2\}$ = 4 bins. Yes, OPT = 4.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **First Fit Decreasing (FFD)** algorithm. Illustrate with an example and state its approximation ratio with time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **Next Fit** algorithm on the input items: $\{0.6, 0.3, 0.5, 0.4, 0.7, 0.2, 0.5\}$. Show all intermediate steps and compute the **approximation ratio achieved** with respect to the optimal solution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

First Fit Decreasing is a two-phase algorithm:
- **Phase 1 (Preprocessing):** Sort all items in non-increasing order of size.
- **Phase 2 (Packing):** Apply the standard First Fit algorithm on the sorted list.

For each item, scan bins in order and place the item in the **first bin** that can accommodate it. If none can, open a new bin.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**

Input: $\{0.4, 0.8, 0.3, 0.5, 0.6, 0.2\}$

- **Sorted:** $\{0.8, 0.6, 0.5, 0.4, 0.3, 0.2\}$
- $B_1 \gets 0.8$, $B_2 \gets 0.6$ (0.8+0.6 > 1), $B_2 \gets +0.4$ (0.6+0.4=1.0 ✓), $B_3 \gets 0.5$ (no fit in $B_1$ or $B_2$), $B_3 \gets +0.3$ (0.5+0.3=0.8), $B_1 \gets +0.2$ (0.8+0.2=1.0 ✓).
- **Result: 3 bins** — $B_1=\{0.8,0.2\}$, $B_2=\{0.6,0.4\}$, $B_3=\{0.5,0.3\}$.

**Properties (2 marks):**

- **Approximation Ratio:** $\frac{11}{9} \approx 1.222$
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ for sorting $+ O(n^2)$ for First Fit $= O(n^2)$.
- **Optimal:** $S = 0.4+0.8+0.3+0.5+0.6+0.2 = 2.8$, so $OPT \ge 3$. **FFD = OPT** here.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Apply Next Fit on** $\{0.6, 0.3, 0.5, 0.4, 0.7, 0.2, 0.5\}$ **(5 marks):**

| Step | Item | Current Bin Remaining | Fits? | Action | Bins |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.6 | $B_1$: 1.0 | Yes | $B_1 \gets \{0.6\}$, rem 0.4 | 1 |
| 2 | 0.3 | $B_1$: 0.4 | Yes | $B_1 \gets \{0.6, 0.3\}$, rem 0.1 | 1 |
| 3 | 0.5 | $B_1$: 0.1 | No | Close $B_1$, open $B_2 \gets \{0.5\}$, rem 0.5 | 2 |
| 4 | 0.4 | $B_2$: 0.5 | Yes | $B_2 \gets \{0.5, 0.4\}$, rem 0.1 | 2 |
| 5 | 0.7 | $B_2$: 0.1 | No | Close $B_2$, open $B_3 \gets \{0.7\}$, rem 0.3 | 3 |
| 6 | 0.2 | $B_3$: 0.3 | Yes | $B_3 \gets \{0.7, 0.2\}$, rem 0.1 | 3 |
| 7 | 0.5 | $B_3$: 0.1 | No | Close $B_3$, open $B_4 \gets \{0.5\}$, rem 0.5 | 4 |

**NF Result = 4 bins** (1 mark)

**Compute Optimal and Ratio (1 mark):**

$$S = 0.6+0.3+0.5+0.4+0.7+0.2+0.5 = 3.2 \implies OPT \ge 4$$

Optimal packing: $B_1 = \{0.6, 0.4\}, B_2 = \{0.3, 0.7\}, B_3 = \{0.5, 0.5\}, B_4 = \{0.2\}$. So $OPT = 4$.

$$\text{Approximation Ratio} = \frac{NF(I)}{OPT(I)} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Confusing NF with FF** in proofs: NF only checks the *current* bin, while FF checks *all open bins*. The 2-approximation proof relies on the property that **two consecutive bins together exceed 1.0**, which is **only true for NF**, not for FF or FFD.
> 2. **Forgetting the sorting step** in FFD: Many students apply FF directly without sorting first, which makes it just regular FF, not FFD. The decreasing sort is what gives the better $11/9$ bound.
> 3. **Miscalculating $OPT$**: Always compute the lower bound $\lceil S \rceil$ as a starting point. $OPT$ may be strictly greater than $\lceil S \rceil$ if items cannot be perfectly packed.
> 4. **State the time complexity in the correct form**: $O(n^2)$ for FFD, not $O(n \log n)$. Sorting is $O(n \log n)$ but the dominant term is the FF scan.
> 5. **Mark allocation**: In 14-mark questions, a clear table of bin contents fetches **at least 2 marks**. Drawing a tabular representation is mandatory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST:**

- **Bin Packing Definition:** Pack $n$ items of sizes in $(0,1]$ into unit-capacity bins. Minimize the number of bins used. **NP-Hard**.
- **Lower Bound on OPT:** $OPT(I) \ge \lceil \sum s_i \rceil$.
- **Next Fit (NF):** Online, $O(n)$, ratio $= 2$. Proof uses "two consecutive bins sum > 1".
- **First Fit (FF):** Online, $O(n^2)$, ratio $\le 1.7$.
- **Best Fit (BF):** Online, $O(n \log n)$ with BST, ratio $\le 1.7$.
- **FFD:** Offline, sort decreasing then FF, $O(n^2)$, ratio $= 11/9$.
- **BFD:** Offline, sort decreasing then BF, ratio $= 11/9$.
- **Worst-case NF input:** Alternating $(0.5+\epsilon, 0.5-\epsilon)$ pattern gives ratio approaching 2.
- **FFD worse-case items:** The asymptotic bound $11/9$ is **tight** — there exist instances where $FFD/OPT$ approaches $11/9$.
- **State the assumption:** All item sizes $\le 1$ (otherwise they can never be packed — instance is infeasible).
- **Mention polynomial-time solvability:** All these algorithms run in polynomial time, even though the problem is NP-hard — this is the entire purpose of approximation.
- **Formula to memorize cold:**
  $$\text{NF} \le 2 \cdot OPT, \quad \text{FF} \le 1.7 \cdot OPT, \quad \text{FFD} \le \frac{11}{9} \cdot OPT + \frac{6}{9}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Randomized Algorithms - Definitions of Monte Carlo and Las Vegas Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST502 - DESIGN AND ANALYSIS OF ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Randomized Algorithms - Definitions of Monte Carlo and Las Vegas Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Randomized Algorithm?

A **Randomized Algorithm** is an algorithm that incorporates randomness (typically through pseudo-random number generators) as a fundamental part of its logic. Instead of following a single, deterministic sequence of steps, a randomized algorithm makes random choices during execution, which influences the outcome and/or the running time. By repeating the algorithm or by careful structural design, the probability of producing a correct (or sufficiently accurate) result can be made arbitrarily close to **1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A randomized algorithm is a computational procedure that, in addition to the input, takes a stream of random bits $r \in \{0,1\}^*$ and uses them to make random choices that affect its output or running time. We say an algorithm $A(x, r)$ *randomized solves* a problem if for every valid input $x$, the probability (over the choice of $r$) that $A$ produces the correct answer is at least $1 - \epsilon$, where $\epsilon$ is the **error probability** and $0 \le \epsilon < \tfrac{1}{2}$.

The two principal classifications of randomized algorithms are:
1. **Monte Carlo Algorithms** — bounded running time, probabilistic correctness.
2. **Las Vegas Algorithms** — guaranteed correctness, expected bounded running time.

## 1.2 The Two Foundational Families

> [!NOTE]
> **Monte Carlo Algorithm (Definition):**
> A Monte Carlo algorithm is a randomized algorithm that is **guaranteed to terminate** in a deterministic (worst-case) running time bound, but whose output has a small probability of being **incorrect**. The running time is fixed; the answer may be wrong.

> [!NOTE]
> **Las Vegas Algorithm (Definition):**
> A Las Vegas algorithm is a randomized algorithm that is **guaranteed to produce a correct output**, but whose running time is a **random variable**. The answer is always right; the time taken varies probabilistically.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Sorts of Lucky Student"

Imagine two students taking a competitive exam:

- **Monte Carlo Student:** Always finishes the paper in **exactly 90 minutes** (fixed time). However, 1 out of 10 times, they mis-bubble a sheet and mark a wrong answer. The result is sometimes incorrect, but the duration is predictable.
- **Las Vegas Student:** Always answers **every question correctly** (correct output). But on some days they are slow, on other days fast — the finish time is unpredictable. They never fail the answer, only the clock.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU exams:** If the *time is fixed but the answer may be wrong* → **Monte Carlo**. If the *answer is always right but the time varies* → **Las Vegas**.

## 1.4 Why Study Randomized Algorithms?

| Motivation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Simplicity** | Many randomized algorithms are dramatically simpler than their deterministic counterparts. |
| **Speed** | Probabilistic decisions often yield lower worst-case expected complexity (e.g., Randomized Quicksort: $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ expected vs deterministic adversarial $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$). |
| **Breaking Adversaries** | Randomness defeats pathological worst-case inputs engineered by an opponent. |
| **Cryptographic Necessity** | Public-key cryptography (e.g., RSA, Rabin) fundamentally requires randomized primality tests. |
| **Distributed Coordination** | Randomized protocols (e.g., Randomized Consensus) outperform deterministic ones in asynchronous systems. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Monte Carlo Algorithm — Anatomy

A Monte Carlo algorithm $M$ for a decision problem with answer set $\{0, 1\}$ satisfies:

1. **Deterministic Time:** For every input $x$ and every random string $r$, $M(x, r)$ halts within a fixed number of steps $T(n)$, where $n = \vert x \vert$.
2. **Probabilistic Correctness:** For every input $x$,
$$
\Pr_r[M(x, r) = \text{correct answer}] \ge 1 - \epsilon, \quad 0 \le \epsilon < \tfrac{1}{2}
$$
3. **Two Error Variants:**
   - **One-Sided Error (one-sided Monte Carlo):** Algorithm may only produce *false positives* (or only *false negatives*), never both. Example: **Miller–Rabin Primality Test** — declares a composite prime with probability $\le 2^{-k}$ after $k$ rounds.
   - **Two-Sided Error (two-sided Monte Carlo):** Algorithm may produce either kind of error, but $\Pr[\text{error}] \le \epsilon < \tfrac{1}{2}$. Example: **Freivalds' Matrix Multiplication Verification**.

### 2.1.2 Las Vegas Algorithm — Anatomy

A Las Vegas algorithm $L$ for any problem satisfies:

1. **Correctness:** For every input $x$ and every random string $r$, $L(x, r)$ outputs the **correct** answer. No exceptions.
2. **Randomized Time:** The running time $T(x, r)$ is a random variable. The expected running time is bounded:
$$
\mathbb{E}_r[T(x, r)] \le f(n), \quad \text{where } n = \vert x \vert
$$
3. **Zero-Error Guarantee:** The probability of an incorrect answer is **exactly 0** for every $r$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Insight (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - A **one-sided error** Monte Carlo algorithm with error $\epsilon$ can be converted into a Las Vegas algorithm by running it *until* it reports success (or with amplification by repetition). The expected number of repetitions to succeed with probability $1 - \delta$ is $\mathcal{O}(\log(1/\delta))$.
> - A **two-sided error** Monte Carlo algorithm can be amplified to *any* desired confidence by repeating it $k$ times and taking the **majority vote**, yielding error $\le 2^{-c k}$ (Chernoff/Hoeffding bound).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Form / Bound |
|---|---|---|
| $\Pr[\text{success}]$ | Probability of correct answer in one run | $p \ge \tfrac{1}{2} + \delta$ |
| $\epsilon$ | One-run error probability | $\epsilon \le \tfrac{1}{2} - \delta$ |
| $k$ | Number of independent repetitions | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| $\delta$ | Amplified confidence parameter | $\delta > 0$ |
| $T(n)$ | Worst-case running time of Monte Carlo | Deterministic bound |
| $\mathbb{E}[T(n)]$ | Expected running time of Las Vegas | $\sum_{t} t \cdot \Pr[T=t]$ |
| $n$ | Input size | $n = \vert x \vert$ |

**Chernoff Bound (Amplification Tool):** For $k$ independent trials each with success probability $p \ge \tfrac{1}{2} + \delta$, the probability that the majority vote is wrong is at most:

$$
\Pr[\text{majority fails}] \le e^{-2 k \delta^2}
$$

**Las Vegas → Monte Carlo Conversion Cost:** A Las Vegas algorithm with expected time $T$ can be transformed into a Monte Carlo algorithm by terminating after $c \cdot T$ steps. The probability of obtaining *no* answer within $c \cdot T$ steps decays exponentially:

$$
\Pr[T > c \cdot \mathbb{E}[T]] \le \tfrac{1}{c}
$$

(Markov's inequality applied to non-negative $T$.)

## 2.3 Canonical Examples for KTU 2024

| Algorithm | Type | One/Two-Sided Error | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Randomized Quicksort | **Las Vegas** | N/A (always correct) | Sorting |
| Randomized Selection (R. QuickSelect) | **Las Vegas** | N/A (always correct) | Order statistics |
| Miller–Rabin Primality Test | **Monte Carlo** (one-sided) | Only false positives (composite declared prime) | Number theory |
| Freivalds' Algorithm | **Monte Carlo** (two-sided) | Either direction | Matrix verification |
| Karger's Min-Cut | **Monte Carlo** (one-sided) | Only false positives (cut larger than min returned) | Graph algorithms |
| Randomized Cache Replacement | **Las Vegas** | N/A | Systems |
| Randomized Hashing (Universal Hashing) | **Monte Carlo** | Two-sided (collision) | Data structures |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Monte Carlo** methods are pervasive in **computational physics** (nuclear reactor simulation), **finance** (option pricing via Monte Carlo integration), **computational biology** (phylogenetic tree sampling), and **rendering** (path tracing for global illumination in computer graphics).
- **Las Vegas** methods dominate **algorithmic infrastructure**: Randomized Quicksort is the default in `qsort` of most C standard libraries, and randomized pivoting is used in production database systems like PostgreSQL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amplifying a One-Sided Monte Carlo Algorithm

**Problem Setup:** Suppose $M$ is a one-sided Monte Carlo algorithm that always answers *No* correctly but may answer *Yes* erroneously with probability $\Pr[M = \text{Yes} \mid x \in Y] \le \epsilon$, where $Y$ is the set of legitimate "Yes" instances.

**Goal:** Reduce the error to at most $\delta$ by repetition.

**Step 1 — Run $M$ independently $k$ times** on input $x$. Each run uses a fresh random string $r_i$.

**Step 2 — Define the output of the amplified algorithm $M_k$:**
- If *any* of the $k$ runs returns *Yes*, then $M_k$ outputs *Yes*.
- Otherwise, $M_k$ outputs *No*.

> **Why this logic for one-sided error:** A *Yes* instance can only be missed (wrongly output as *No*) if *all* $k$ runs are wrong, which is exponentially unlikely.

**Step 3 — Bound the amplified error probability:**
$$
\Pr[M_k \text{ says No} \mid x \in Y] \;=\; \Pr[\text{all } k \text{ runs are wrong}] \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} \Pr[M \text{ wrong on } r_i]
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the bound $\Pr[M \text{ wrong}] \le \epsilon$:**
$$
\Pr[M_k \text{ says No} \mid x \in Y] \;\le\; \prod_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon \;=\; \epsilon^{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k$ given the desired confidence $1 - \delta$:**
$$
\epsilon^{k} \;\le\; \delta \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; k \cdot \ln \epsilon \;\le\; \ln \delta
$$

Since $0 < \epsilon < 1$, $\ln \epsilon < 0$, hence we divide and flip:
$$
k \;\ge\; \dfrac{\ln(1/\delta)}{\ln(1/\epsilon)}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Closed Form:**
$$
\boxed{\,k \;\ge\; \dfrac{\ln \delta^{-1}}{\ln \epsilon^{-1}}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** To achieve error $\le 10^{-6}$ starting from $\epsilon = \tfrac{1}{2}$, we need $k \ge \dfrac{\ln 10^{6}}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{6 \ln 10}{\ln 2} \approx 19.93$, so $k = 20$ repetitions suffice.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Running Time of a Las Vegas Algorithm

**Setup:** Suppose algorithm $L$ on input $x$ succeeds (returns a correct answer) on a single independent attempt with probability $p$, and each attempt costs at most $T$ steps.

**Step 1 — Model the number of attempts as a geometric random variable** $X \sim \text{Geom}(p)$, with $\Pr[X = k] = (1-p)^{k-1} p$ for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$

**Step 2 — Expected number of attempts:**
$$
\mathbb{E}[X] \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} p
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the sum** using the standard geometric identity $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k q^{k-1} = \tfrac{1}{(1-q)^2}$ for $\vert q \vert < 1$:
$$
\mathbb{E}[X] \;=\; p \cdot \dfrac{1}{\bigl(1 - (1-p)\bigr)^{2}} \;=\; p \cdot \dfrac{1}{p^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{p}
$$

**Step 4 — Total expected running time:**
$$
\boxed{\,\mathbb{E}[T_{LV}(x)] \;=\; \dfrac{T}{p}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If the per-attempt success probability is $p = \tfrac{1}{2}$, the expected cost **doubles**; if $p = \tfrac{1}{n}$, the expected cost grows by a factor of $n$. This is why the per-iteration success probability $p$ must be carefully engineered in real Las Vegas designs (e.g., randomized quicksort pivot selection: $p \ge 1 - \tfrac{2}{n^2}$ on choosing a good pivot).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Monte Carlo | Las Vegas |
|---|---|---|
| **Output correctness** | Probabilistic (error $\le \epsilon$) | **Always correct** |
| **Running time** | Deterministic worst-case $T(n)$ | Expected $\mathbb{E}[T(n)]$, worst-case may be unbounded |
| **Resource use** | Predictable | Variable |
| **Convertibility** | Can be made Las Vegas by repetition (one-sided) | Can be made Monte Carlo by time-cutoff |
| **Lower-bound relevance** | Useful for hard problems where no fast deterministic algorithm exists | Useful when correctness is non-negotiable |
| **Amplification** | Repetition + majority vote reduces error exponentially | Repetition reduces expected time marginally |
| **Examples** | Miller–Rabin, Freivalds, Karger | Randomized Quicksort, Randomized Quickselect |
| **Failure surface** | "Wrong answer" | "Takes too long" |
| **Best application** | Decision problems, verifications, approximations | Optimization, sorting, search |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Karger's Min-Cut Error Amplification)

**Statement:** Karger's randomized min-cut algorithm on a graph with $n$ vertices succeeds (finds the true min-cut) with probability $p \ge \tfrac{2}{n(n-1)}$.

**Step 1 — Single-run success probability:**
$$
p \;\ge\; \dfrac{2}{n(n-1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Repeat $k$ times independently and keep the minimum cut found.** Probability of *at least one* success:
$$
\Pr[\text{at least one success in } k \text{ runs}] \;=\; 1 - (1 - p)^{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Bound via $1 - q \le e^{-q}$:**
$$
(1 - p)^{k} \;\le\; e^{-k p}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose $k$ to make the failure probability $\le \tfrac{1}{n}$:**
$$
e^{-k p} \;\le\; \tfrac{1}{n} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; k \;\ge\; \dfrac{\ln n}{p} \;\ge\; \dfrac{\ln n \cdot n(n-1)}{2} \;=\; \mathcal{O}(n^{2} \ln n)
$$

**Step 5 — Total running time of amplified version:** $T_{\text{amp}}(n) = k \cdot T_{\text{single}}(n) = \mathcal{O}(n^2 \ln n) \cdot \mathcal{O}(n^2) = \mathcal{O}(n^4 \ln n)$ — a Las Vegas algorithm with high probability of finding the true min-cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the algorithm is "one-sided error" Monte Carlo:** The cut it returns is *always a valid cut*, but it may not be the *minimum* cut. So the only error is returning a non-minimal cut — analogous to a "false positive" for the predicate "this is the minimum cut".

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow: Choosing Between Monte Carlo and Las Vegas

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Design a Randomized Algorithm]) --> q1{Is the answer\nrequired to be 100% correct?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Can we bound the\nexpected running time?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Is a small bounded\nerror acceptable?}
    q2 -- Yes --> lv([Use Las Vegas Algorithm\nE T n bounded\nanswer always correct])
    q2 -- No --> rev([Reformulate the problem\nor use a different approach])
    q3 -- Yes --> mc1{Does the algorithm\nonly fail in one direction?}
    q3 -- No --> mc2([Use Two-Sided Error Monte Carlo\nwith repetition + majority vote])
    mc1 -- Yes --> mc3([Use One-Sided Error Monte Carlo\namplify by repeating until success])
    mc1 -- No --> mc2
```

## 4.2 Operational Schematic: Monte Carlo vs Las Vegas Execution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MCRun[Monte Carlo Execution]
        M1[Input x] --> M2[Generate random r]
        M2 --> M3[Deterministic computation\nin fixed T n steps]
        M3 --> M4[Output: may be wrong\nwith probability eps]
    end

    subgraph LVRun[Las Vegas Execution]
        L1[Input x] --> L2[Generate random r sub 1]
        L2 --> L3{Computation\nsucceeds?}
        L3 -- No --> L4[Try again with new r sub 2]
        L4 --> L3
        L3 -- Yes --> L5[Output: always correct]
    end
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map of Randomized Algorithm Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Randomized Algorithms] --> mc[Monte Carlo\nFixed time, may err]
    root --> lv[Las Vegas\nAlways correct, variable time]

    mc --> mc1side[One-Sided Error\nMiller Rabin, Karger]
    mc --> mc2side[Two-Sided Error\nFreivalds]

    lv --> lvex1[Randomized Quicksort]
    lv --> lvex2[Randomized Quickselect]
    lv --> lvex3[Randomized Hashing Schemes]

    mc1side -.Amplify via repetition.-> mcStrong[Amplified Monte Carlo\nerror less than 2 sup c k]
    mc2side -.Majority vote.-> mcStrong
    lvex3 -.Cut off after c times E T.-> lv2mc[Las Vegas becomes Monte Carlo\nwith time cutoff]

    style root fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style mc fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style lv fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style mcStrong fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style lv2mc fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Amplification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Base Algorithm]
        B1[Input x of size n] --> B2[Random Source R]
        B2 --> B3[Algorithm A: x and r]
        B3 --> B4[Single output y]
    end

    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Amplification Layer]
        A1[Repetition Controller\nruns A independently k times] --> A2[Output Aggregator]
        A2 --> A3{Vote / Threshold}
        A3 -- majority yes --> A4[Return YES]
        A3 -- majority no --> A5[Return NO]
    end

    end

    B4 --> A1
    A4 --> E1
    A5 --> E1
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define a randomized algorithm. Distinguish between Monte Carlo and Las Vegas algorithms with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **randomized algorithm** is an algorithm whose behavior is determined not only by its input but also by values produced by a random number generator. The randomness is a *structural* part of the algorithm, not a substitute for correctness.

**Distinction:**

| Feature | Monte Carlo | Las Vegas |
|---|---|---|
| Running time | Deterministic (bounded) | Random variable (expected bounded) |
| Output correctness | May be wrong (bounded error $\epsilon$) | Always correct |
| Example | Miller–Rabin primality test (one-sided) | Randomized Quicksort |

*(Award 1 mark for randomized algorithm definition, 1 mark for table-style distinction, 1 mark for correct examples.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**Q: A Monte Carlo algorithm has one-sided error probability $\epsilon = \tfrac{1}{4}$. How many independent repetitions are needed to bring the error below $10^{-3}$? Show the derivation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For one-sided error, the amplified algorithm $M_k$ outputs the *correct Yes* only if *at least one* of the $k$ runs is correct. The probability that *all* $k$ runs fail is $\epsilon^k$.

**Step 1:** We require $\epsilon^k \le 10^{-3}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $\epsilon = \tfrac{1}{4}$:
$$
\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^k \le 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3:** Take logarithms (using $\log_{10}$ for clarity):
$$
-k \log_{10} 4 \le -3 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k \ge \dfrac{3}{\log_{10} 4} \approx \dfrac{3}{0.602} \approx 4.98
$$

**Step 4:** Round up:
$$
\boxed{k = 5 \text{ repetitions}}
$$

*(Award 1 mark for setting up $\epsilon^k \le 10^{-3}$, 1 mark for solving the inequality, 1 mark for correct integer answer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 4 carries a 14-mark question. You will be offered two full-alternative questions; you attempt **ONE**. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Define Monte Carlo and Las Vegas algorithms. Describe the structure of a **one-sided error** Monte Carlo algorithm with an example. Explain how the error probability is reduced through amplification by repeated independent trials. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider the Miller–Rabin primality test. Suppose a single round declares a composite number to be prime with probability at most $\tfrac{1}{4}$. For a confidence requirement of $1 - 10^{-12}$, determine the minimum number of independent rounds $k$ required. Justify the formula used. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Monte Carlo definition** (1 mark): A Monte Carlo algorithm has a *fixed* running time for every input; its *output* may be incorrect with bounded probability $\epsilon < \tfrac{1}{2}$.

2. **Las Vegas definition** (1 mark): A Las Vegas algorithm *always* returns the correct answer; its *running time* is a random variable with bounded expectation.

3. **One-sided error structure** (2 marks): A one-sided error Monte Carlo algorithm can err in only one direction (e.g., declare composite numbers as prime, but never declare a prime as composite). This asymmetry is exploited for amplification: run the algorithm $k$ times and accept *only* if **all** runs agree on the "definitive" answer; if any run says "definitely wrong", the algorithm is certain.

4. **Amplification derivation** (2 marks): For one-sided error, repeating $k$ times reduces the error to at most $\epsilon^k$. For two-sided error, repeating $k$ times and taking the **majority vote** reduces the error exponentially (by Chernoff bound $\le e^{-2k\delta^2}$). Example: **Karger's Min-Cut**, **Miller–Rabin Primality Test**.

5. **Example** (1 mark): Mention Miller–Rabin or Karger's min-cut explicitly with context.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — State the problem (1 mark):** One round of Miller–Rabin has error probability $\epsilon = \tfrac{1}{4}$ (i.e., it may falsely call a composite "prime"). We need the probability of *at least one* false prime declaration in $k$ rounds to be at most $10^{-12}$.

**Step 2 — Set up the inequality (2 marks):**
$$
\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^{k} \le 10^{-12}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve using logarithms (2 marks):**
$$
k \cdot \log_{10} \!\left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right) \le -12
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
k \cdot (-\log_{10} 4) \le -12
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
k \ge \dfrac{12}{\log_{10} 4} \approx \dfrac{12}{0.60206} \approx 19.93
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude and justify (2 marks):**
$$
\boxed{k_{\min} = 20 \text{ rounds}}
$$

**Justification:** The amplification principle for one-sided error states $\Pr[\text{amplified error}] \le \epsilon^k$. Since the function $\epsilon^k$ is monotonically decreasing in $k$ for $\epsilon \in (0,1)$, the smallest integer $k$ satisfying the inequality achieves the desired confidence $1 - 10^{-12}$ with the minimum computational overhead.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** Define a Las Vegas algorithm formally. Explain with a derivation why the expected running time of a Las Vegas algorithm that succeeds on each independent attempt with probability $p$ and costs $T$ steps per attempt is $T/p$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a randomized search algorithm that probes a sorted array of size $n$ at uniformly random positions, and it stops as soon as it finds the target. Suppose each probe costs $\mathcal{O}(1)$ and the success probability per probe is $p = \tfrac{1}{n}$. Compute the expected number of probes. State the asymptotic running time. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Formal definition (2 marks):** A Las Vegas algorithm $L$ on input $x$ satisfies: (i) for every random string $r$, $L(x, r)$ produces a correct answer; (ii) the running time $T(x, r)$ is a random variable with $\mathbb{E}_r[T(x, r)] \le f(\vert x \vert)$ for some function $f$.

2. **Geometric distribution model (2 marks):** The number of attempts $X$ until the first success is a geometric random variable: $\Pr[X = k] = (1-p)^{k-1} p$ for $k \ge 1$. This model applies because each attempt is independent and identically distributed.

3. **Derivation of $\mathbb{E}[X] = 1/p$ (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} p = p \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k q^{k-1}, \quad q = 1 - p
$$
Using the standard identity $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k q^{k-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-q)^2} = \dfrac{1}{p^2}$:
$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = p \cdot \dfrac{1}{p^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{p}
$$

4. **Total expected time (1 mark):** $\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{total}}] = \mathbb{E}[X] \cdot T = \dfrac{T}{p}$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters (2 marks):**
- Per-probe cost: $T = \mathcal{O}(1)$
- Per-probe success probability: $p = \tfrac{1}{n}$
- Number of probes follows Geometric($p$).

**Step 2 — Apply the expected-value formula (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbb{E}[X] = \dfrac{1}{p} = \dfrac{1}{1/n} = n
$$

**Step 3 — Total expected running time (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{\text{total}}] = \mathbb{E}[X] \cdot T = n \cdot \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic conclusion (1 mark):**
$$
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[T(n)] = \mathcal{O}(n)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although any *particular* probe may fail, the Las Vegas formulation guarantees that *eventually* the target is found. The expected cost is linear in $n$, which is asymptotically optimal for searching (no deterministic algorithm can do better than $\Omega(n)$ in the worst case without extra structure like hashing or binary search).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "randomized" with "approximation":** Approximation algorithms return a *guaranteed-close* answer in polynomial time; randomized algorithms return the *exact* (or decision) answer with a *probabilistic guarantee*. These are *different* algorithmic paradigms and marks will be deducted if conflated.
> 2. **Mixing up error directions:** When asked about one-sided vs two-sided Monte Carlo, students often forget to specify *which direction* the error lies in. Always state explicitly: "false positive only" or "false negative only".
> 3. **Forgetting the $\ln/\log$ base:** In amplification derivations, base mismatch in $\log$ produces wrong integer $k$. Show the step where you switch between $\ln$ and $\log_{10}$ explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong rounding direction for $k$:** Since we need $\epsilon^k \le \delta$, $k$ must be the **ceiling** (round *up*) of the computed value, not the floor.
> 5. **Saying "Monte Carlo is incorrect":** Monte Carlo is *probabilistically correct* with bounded error — it is *not* "incorrect" or "unreliable". State the error bound $\epsilon$ explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting the conversion direction:** "Las Vegas → Monte Carlo" uses time cutoff; "Monte Carlo → Las Vegas" uses repetition. Mixing these up costs a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print-This-and-Revise Checklist for KTU Module 4:**

- [ ] **Randomized algorithm:** Behavior depends on both input and random bits; correctness is probabilistic, not absolute.
- [ ] **Monte Carlo = fixed time, may err.** Running time is a deterministic function of input size.
- [ ] **Las Vegas = always correct, may take long.** Output is guaranteed correct for every random string.
- [ ] **One-sided error Monte Carlo** errs in only *one* direction; amplifiable by *repetition-until-acceptance*.
- [ ] **Two-sided error Monte Carlo** may err in *both* directions; amplified by *majority vote over $k$ repetitions*.
- [ ] **Chernoff bound** for majority-vote amplification: $\Pr[\text{error after } k \text{ runs}] \le e^{-2k\delta^2}$ where $\delta$ is the per-run advantage over $\tfrac{1}{2}$.
- [ ] **Las Vegas expected time formula:** $\mathbb{E}[T] = T_{\text{per-attempt}} / p$ where $p$ is the per-attempt success probability.
- [ ] **One-sided amplification formula:** $k \ge \ln(\delta^{-1}) / \ln(\epsilon^{-1})$.
- [ ] **Las Vegas → Monte Carlo cutoff:** After $c \cdot \mathbb{E}[T]$ steps, terminate; failure probability $\le 1/c$ by Markov.
- [ ] **Canonical Las Vegas examples:** Randomized Quicksort, Randomized Quickselect, randomized hashing schemes.
- [ ] **Canonical Monte Carlo examples:** Miller–Rabin primality (one-sided), Freivalds' matrix verification (two-sided), Karger's min-cut (one-sided).
- [ ] **Key insight:** Karger's min-cut has per-run success probability $p \ge \tfrac{2}{n(n-1)}$; repeating $\mathcal{O}(n^2 \log n)$ times turns it into a Las Vegas algorithm with high probability.
- [ ] **Adversary model:** Randomness defeats worst-case adversarial inputs; this is the *core practical reason* randomized algorithms are faster in practice.
- [ ] **Always specify error direction** when writing Monte Carlo answers in KTU exams; "bounded-error" is a generic term covering both one- and two-sided.
- [ ] **Never confuse** "randomized" with "approximation" or "heuristic". Randomized algorithms can have *provable* probabilistic guarantees.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Randomized Quicksort** is a variant of the deterministic Quicksort algorithm in which the pivot element for partitioning is selected **uniformly at random** from the subarray being sorted, rather than following a fixed rule (such as always picking the first, last, or middle element). The goal of this randomization is to make the algorithm's running time **independent of the input distribution**, ensuring that no particular input can systematically force the algorithm into its worst-case behavior.

Formally, the procedure RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r) swaps A[r] with an element chosen uniformly at random from A[p..r], then calls the standard PARTITION routine. The driver procedure RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r) recurses on the resulting two subarrays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Randomization here is a *probabilistic guarantee*, not a heuristic. The expected running time is O(n log n) for **every** input, regardless of whether the input is sorted, reverse-sorted, or adversarially chosen.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lottery of Pivots

Imagine a teacher who has to seat 50 students in height order. A **deterministic** strategy would always pick the *first* student as a reference and split the class by height relative to that one person. If the class is already sorted by name (an adversary case), this student is the shortest, leaving 49 students to be compared serially — a disaster.

A **randomized** strategy is smarter: the teacher closes their eyes, points to a *random* student, and uses them as the pivot. No matter how the students are arranged, a "bad" choice is now *unlikely* in a deep, structural sense — the probability that the random pivot lands near an extreme position in a large subarray decays rapidly. This is the entire philosophical core of randomized quicksort: **break the chain of bad decisions by making every decision independent and random**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU Board Examinations

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pivot** | The element used to partition the array into elements less than, equal to, and greater than itself. |
| **Partition** | The linear-time routine that rearranges the subarray around the pivot. |
| **Indicator Random Variable (IRV)** | A Bernoulli random variable X ∈ {0,1} used to count events; its expectation equals the event's probability. |
| **Expected Running Time** | E[T(n)] — the average over the internal coin flips of the algorithm, **not** the average over inputs. |
| **Rank of pivot** | The position (k) the pivot element would occupy in the sorted order of the current subarray. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle but Exam-Critical Distinction:** Randomized quicksort's expected time is taken over the *random choices of the algorithm itself*, not over the input distribution. This is what makes the guarantee unconditional.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The randomized quicksort operates through the following carefully ordered sequence:

1. **Base Case Check:** If `p < r`, proceed; otherwise the subarray has 0 or 1 element and is trivially sorted.
2. **Random Pivot Selection:** Generate a random integer `i` uniformly in the range `[p, r]`, then swap `A[i]` with `A[r]`. This brings the randomly chosen pivot to the end so the deterministic PARTITION routine can be reused.
3. **Partitioning:** Invoke PARTITION(A, p, r), which performs an in-place rearrangement. It returns an index `q` such that:
   - `A[p..q-1]` contains elements ≤ A[q]
   - `A[q+1..r]` contains elements ≥ A[q]
4. **Recursive Calls:** Recurse on the left subarray `(p, q-1)` and the right subarray `(q+1, r)`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the swap-and-reuse trick?** It preserves the elegance of the classical PARTITION routine while injecting randomness. Many students in the KTU board exam lose marks by writing a *new* partition function from scratch — a costly and error-prone detour.

## 2.2 Pseudocode (Standard CLRS Form — Required for Board Answers)

```
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
1  if p < r
2      q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
3      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
4      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)

RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
1  i = RANDOM(p, r)
2  exchange A[r] <-> A[i]
3  return PARTITION(A, p, r)

PARTITION(A, p, r)
1  x = A[r]            // pivot
2  i = p - 1
3  for j = p to r - 1
4      if A[j] ≤ x
5          i = i + 1
6          exchange A[i] <-> A[j]
7  exchange A[i + 1] <-> A[r]
8  return i + 1
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is used below as a delimiter for "such that" or for set-builder notation. In **markdown table cells**, this symbol is rendered as `\mid` or `\vert` to prevent breaking the table syntax. Below, the table uses inline LaTeX delimiters.

| Symbol / Expression | Definition | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $T(n)$ | Total running time on input of size n | Recurrence |
| $\Theta(n)$ | Time for PARTITION on n elements | Recurrence base cost |
| $X_k$ | Indicator RV: 1 if pivot is the k-th smallest | Expectation derivation |
| $\Pr(X_k = 1)$ | Probability the pivot has rank k | $= 1/n$ (uniform) |
| $E[T(n)]$ | Expected running time | Indicator sum method |
| $\sum_{k=1}^{n} X_k \cdot (T(k-1) + T(n-k))$ | Decomposition of T(n) by pivot rank | Key step |
| $T(n) \le a n \ln n + b n$ | Closed-form solution of the recurrence | Final result |
| $H_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{i}$ | n-th harmonic number; $H_n \approx \ln n + \gamma$ | Bounding the sum |

## 2.4 Complexity Summary Table (Board-Ready)

| Case | Time Complexity | Occurs When |
|---|---|---|
| **Best** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Pivot splits array exactly in half each time |
| **Average (Expected)** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | For **any** input — this is the strength of randomization |
| **Worst** | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Vanishingly small probability ($1/n!$ factor) — never deterministically triggered by input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Space Complexity:** $\Theta(\log n)$ expected (recursion stack), $\Theta(n)$ worst case on adversarial recursion pattern.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Randomized quicksort (in its *introsort* hybrid form) is the **default sorting algorithm** in many production systems, including the C++ Standard Library (`std::sort` uses introsort, which falls back to heapsort if recursion depth exceeds $2 \log_2 n$, and starts with randomized quicksort). It is also used in:
- Database query engines for in-memory sorting
- Statistical packages (R, NumPy) for general-purpose sorting
- Graphics engines for depth-sorting primitives
- Distributed sorting frameworks (TeraSort family) where pivot selection is randomized across nodes

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setting Up the Recurrence

Let $T(n)$ be the running time of RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT on a subarray of $n$ elements. PARTITION costs $\Theta(n)$. If the pivot ends up at position $q$ (where $1 \le q \le n$), the two recursive calls have sizes $q-1$ and $n-q$. We obtain the recurrence:

$$T(n) = T(q-1) + T(n-q) + \Theta(n)$$

> **Step Logic:** $T(q-1)$ sorts the left part, $T(n-q)$ sorts the right part, and $\Theta(n)$ is the cost of the partition pass that scans every element once.

## 3.2 The Key Trick — Summing Over Pivot Ranks

Because $q$ is a *random variable* uniform over $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, we rewrite the total work by introducing **indicator random variables** $X_k$:

$$X_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if the pivot chosen is the } k\text{-th smallest element} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Because the pivot is chosen uniformly at random from the $n$ elements:

$$\Pr(X_k = 1) = \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{for every } k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

We can now express the total recursive cost as a sum over all possible pivot ranks. The pivot with rank $k$ leaves a left subproblem of size $k-1$ and a right subproblem of size $n-k$:

$$T(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} X_k \cdot \bigl(T(k-1) + T(n-k)\bigr) + \Theta(n)$$

> **Step Logic:** Exactly one $X_k$ equals 1, so the sum selects *one* term corresponding to the realized pivot rank. This converts the random recurrence into a deterministic sum.

## 3.3 Taking the Expectation

Linearity of expectation lets us pass E[] inside the sum, and the independence of $X_k$ from the recursive subproblem sizes gives:

$$E[T(n)] = E\left[ \sum_{k=1}^{n} X_k \cdot \bigl(T(k-1) + T(n-k)\bigr) \right] + \Theta(n)$$

$$E[T(n)] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} E[X_k] \cdot E\bigl[T(k-1) + T(n-k)\bigr] + \Theta(n)$$

> **Step Logic:** The pivot choice in the *current* recursive call is independent of the random choices made in deeper recursive calls — a critical independence property of randomized quicksort.

Substituting $E[X_k] = 1/n$ and expanding:

$$E[T(n)] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl(E[T(k-1)] + E[T(n-k)]\bigr) + \Theta(n)$$

By symmetry, the two halves of the sum are equal, so we can write a single sum from $0$ to $n-1$ and multiply by 2:

$$E[T(n)] = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} E[T(k)] + \Theta(n)$$

> **Step Logic:** Substituting $i = k-1$ in the first sum gives $\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} E[T(i)]$; the second sum is the same by the reflection $k \to n-k$.

## 3.4 Solving the Recurrence by Substitution

We **guess** the solution $E[T(n)] \le a n \ln n + b n$ for constants $a, b > 0$ to be determined, with base case $E[T(0)] = E[T(1)] = b$. Substituting into the recurrence:

$$E[T(n)] = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (a k \ln k + b k) + \Theta(n)$$

> **Step Logic:** This is the inductive hypothesis applied term-by-term. We will bound the harmonic-sum component separately.

To bound the sum, we use the integral approximation $\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k \le \int_0^{n} x \, dx = n^2 / 2$ and the integral bound for the harmonic series $\sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \ln k \le \int_1^{n} \ln x \, dx = n \ln n - n + 1$. Substituting:

$$\frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} (a k \ln k + b k) \le \frac{2}{n} \left( a \int_1^{n} x \ln x \, dx + b \cdot \frac{n^2}{2} \right)$$

Evaluating $\int_1^n x \ln x \, dx$ via integration by parts:

$$\int_1^{n} x \ln x \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \ln x - \frac{x^2}{4} \right]_1^{n} = \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4} + \frac{1}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$E[T(n)] \le \frac{2}{n} \left( a \left( \frac{n^2}{2} \ln n - \frac{n^2}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \right) + \frac{b n^2}{2} \right) + \Theta(n)$$

$$E[T(n)] \le a n \ln n - \frac{a n}{2} + \frac{a}{2n} + b n + \Theta(n)$$

$$E[T(n)] \le a n \ln n + b n - \frac{a n}{2} + \Theta(n)$$

> **Step Logic:** We have isolated the $a n \ln n + b n$ form we wanted, but a negative correction term $-\frac{a n}{2}$ remains. We absorb it by choosing the leading constant $a$ large enough to dominate the $\Theta(n)$ term: choose $a$ so that $-\frac{a n}{2} + c n \le 0$, i.e., $a \ge 2c$.

For sufficiently large $n$, the $\Theta(n)$ term is bounded above by $c n$. Picking $a \ge 2c$ completes the induction, and we conclude:

$$\boxed{E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)}$$

## 3.5 The Analytical Matrix (Step-By-Step Summary)

| Step | Transformation | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Write $T(n) = T(q-1) + T(n-q) + \Theta(n)$ | Captures recursion + partition cost |
| 2 | Introduce $X_k = 1$ if pivot has rank $k$ | Discretizes the random pivot |
| 3 | Sum over ranks: $T(n) = \sum X_k(T(k-1) + T(n-k)) + \Theta(n)$ | One $X_k$ is 1, rest are 0 |
| 4 | Take expectations; use $E[X_k] = 1/n$ | Uniform pivot selection |
| 5 | Combine symmetric sums: $E[T(n)] = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} E[T(k)] + \Theta(n)$ | Mirror symmetry of the two sums |
| 6 | Guess $E[T(n)] \le a n \ln n + b n$; verify by substitution | Bounded by integral approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\Theta(n \log n)$ and not $O(n \log n)$ in practice?** Because a matching lower bound $\Omega(n \log n)$ holds for any comparison-based sorting algorithm on $n$ elements (information-theoretic argument). Thus randomized quicksort is **asymptotically optimal** among comparison sorts.
