# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Control Flow of Randomized Quicksort

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: A p r] --> B{p less than r}
    B -- No --> C[Return: Subarray Sorted]
    B -- Yes --> D[Randomized Partition A p r]
    D --> E[Receive pivot index q]
    E --> F[Recursive Call Left A p q-1]
    E --> G[Recursive Call Right A q+1 r]
    F --> H[Merge Implicit: In Place Sort]
    G --> H
    H --> I[End: Return to Caller]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of the Randomized Partition Routine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Input: Subarray A p r] --> R2[Generate Random Index i between p and r]
    R2 --> R3[Swap A r with A i]
    R3 --> R4[Set x to A r as pivot]
    R4 --> R5[Initialize i to p minus 1]
    R5 --> R6[Loop j from p to r minus 1]
    R6 --> R7{Is A j less than or equal to x}
    R7 -- Yes --> R8[Increment i then Swap A i with A j]
    R7 -- No --> R6
    R8 --> R6
    R6 -- Loop Done --> R9[Final Swap A i+1 with A r]
    R9 --> R10[Return i plus 1 as pivot position q]
```

## 4.3 Recursive Decomposition Tree (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Root Call on n elements] --> S1[Left Subproblem Size q-1]
    S0 --> S2[Right Subproblem Size n-q]
    S1 --> S3[Left-Left Size q1-1]
    S1 --> S4[Left-Right Size q-1-q1]
    S2 --> S5[Right-Left Size q2-1]
    S2 --> S6[Right-Right Size n-q-q2]
    S3 --> S7[Continued Recursion until base case]
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Base Case: size 0 or 1 returned unchanged]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the tree:** The root node pays $\Theta(n)$ for partitioning. Each level of the recursion pays $\Theta(n)$ *in aggregate* provided the pivot splits the array into subproblems whose sizes shrink geometrically on average. The expected depth is $\Theta(\log n)$, yielding total expected work $\Theta(n \log n)$.

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: Deterministic vs Randomized Quicksort

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Deterministic QS] --> A1[Pivot Rule Fixed: First Last or Median of Three]
    A1 --> A2[Adversarial Input Can Force O n squared]
    A2 --> A3[No Probabilistic Guarantee]
    B[Randomized QS] --> B1[Pivot Chosen Uniformly at Random]
    B1 --> B2[Expected O n log n for Any Input]
    B2 --> B3[No Input Can Systematically Cause Worst Case]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the expected depth is $\Theta(\log n)$:** A randomly chosen pivot, in expectation, splits the subarray into two parts each of size at most $3n/4$ (because a 25-75 split or better happens with probability $1/2$). Iterated logarithmically, this gives an expected recursion depth of $O(\log n)$.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: What is randomized quicksort? How does it differ from the standard quicksort algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Randomized quicksort is a variant in which the pivot element is selected uniformly at random from the subarray being partitioned, ensuring the expected running time is $O(n \log n)$ for any input.
- **(1 mark) Difference — Pivot Selection:** Standard quicksort uses a fixed rule (e.g., first or last element) for pivot selection, whereas randomized quicksort picks a random index in $[p, r]$.
- **(1 mark) Difference — Performance Guarantee:** Deterministic quicksort can degrade to $O(n^2)$ on already-sorted or adversarially-chosen inputs, while randomized quicksort's expected $O(n \log n)$ holds for every input.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Q: Define the indicator random variable $X_k$ used in the analysis of randomized quicksort and state its expected value.**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** $X_k$ is an indicator random variable that takes the value 1 if the pivot element selected in a particular call of PARTITION is the $k$-th smallest element of the current subarray, and 0 otherwise.
- **(1 mark) Probability:** Because the pivot is chosen uniformly at random from $n$ elements, $\Pr(X_k = 1) = 1/n$.
- **(1 mark) Expectation:** By the fundamental property of indicator random variables, $E[X_k] = \Pr(X_k = 1) = 1/n$.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT algorithm in detail. Provide the pseudocode for both RANDOMIZED-PARTITION and PARTITION, and describe the role of the random pivot.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Description (2 marks):** Randomized quicksort works on the divide-and-conquer paradigm. It first checks the base case ($p < r$), then selects a pivot uniformly at random from the subarray $A[p..r]$, partitions the array around this pivot, and recurses on the two resulting subarrays.

**Pseudocode for RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT (1.5 marks):**
```
RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, r)
1  if p < r
2      q = RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
3      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, p, q - 1)
4      RANDOMIZED-QUICKSORT(A, q + 1, r)
```

**Pseudocode for RANDOMIZED-PARTITION (1.5 marks):**
```
RANDOMIZED-PARTITION(A, p, r)
1  i = RANDOM(p, r)
2  exchange A[r] <-> A[i]
3  return PARTITION(A, p, r)
```

**Role of the Random Pivot (2 marks):** The random pivot decouples the algorithm's performance from any specific input ordering. The probability that the random pivot is the $k$-th smallest is exactly $1/n$, which prevents an adversary from forcing unbalanced partitions. This randomization is what guarantees expected $O(n \log n)$ time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expected running time of randomized quicksort using indicator random variables. Clearly state the final asymptotic result.

**Model Solution:**

**Setting up the Recurrence (1.5 marks):**
$$T(n) = T(q-1) + T(n-q) + \Theta(n)$$

where $q$ is the random rank of the pivot.

**Indicator Variable Setup (1.5 marks):** Define $X_k = 1$ if pivot has rank $k$, with $E[X_k] = 1/n$. Rewrite:
$$T(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} X_k \bigl(T(k-1) + T(n-k)\bigr) + \Theta(n)$$

**Taking the Expectation (2 marks):** Using linearity of expectation and independence:
$$E[T(n)] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \bigl(E[T(k-1)] + E[T(n-k)]\bigr) + \Theta(n) = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} E[T(k)] + \Theta(n)$$

**Solving by Substitution (2 marks):** Guess $E[T(n)] \le a n \ln n + b n$. Substituting and using integral bounds gives:
$$E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write `T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/2) + Θ(n)` as if the pivot *always* splits in half. This is the *best case*, not the expected case. The correct expected-case recurrence is $\frac{2}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} E[T(k)] + \Theta(n)$, which sums over *all* possible pivot ranks. Writing the balanced-split recurrence costs you **at least 3 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare deterministic quicksort and randomized quicksort with respect to (i) pivot selection, (ii) time complexity in the best, average, and worst cases, and (iii) dependence on input distribution.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Pivot Selection (2 marks):**
- *Deterministic:* Uses a fixed rule — first element, last element, or median-of-three.
- *Randomized:* Picks a random index uniformly from $A[p..r]$ and swaps it with $A[r]$ before partitioning.

**(ii) Time Complexity Comparison (3 marks):**

| Case | Deterministic QS | Randomized QS |
|---|---|---|
| Best | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ (in expectation) |
| Average | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ (in expectation) |
| Worst | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ (with probability $\le 2/n!$) |

**(iii) Input Distribution Dependence (2 marks):**
- *Deterministic:* Heavily dependent. A sorted, reverse-sorted, or median-of-three-breaking input triggers the worst case.
- *Randomized:* Input-independent. The expected $O(n \log n)$ holds for *any* input, because the expectation is over the algorithm's own random choices, not over inputs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Show that the expected number of comparisons made by randomized quicksort on an input of size $n$ is at most $2n \ln n$ asymptotically. Use the indicator random variable method.

**Model Solution:**

**Counting Comparisons via Pivot Ranks (2 marks):** When the pivot has rank $k$, it is compared with every other element in the current subarray *exactly once* (during PARTITION) — except elements that have already been chosen as pivots in earlier recursive calls. Equivalently, the element of rank $k$ is compared with an element of rank $j$ in a given call *only if* one of them is the pivot of that call.

**Defining Comparisons (1.5 marks):** Let $X_{ij} = 1$ if the element of rank $i$ is compared with the element of rank $j$ at some point during sorting, and 0 otherwise. The total number of comparisons is $X = \sum_{i < j} X_{ij}$.

**Probability of a Comparison (1.5 marks):** Two elements are compared if and only if one of them is the pivot of the subarray that *first* contains both. The pivot must be either the $i$-th or $j$-th smallest among the elements with ranks in $[i, j]$. Probability:
$$\Pr(X_{ij} = 1) = \frac{2}{j - i + 1}$$

**Summing Up (2 marks):**
$$E[X] = \sum_{i < j} \frac{2}{j - i + 1} = \sum_{k=2}^{n} (n - k + 1) \cdot \frac{2}{k} \le 2n \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k} \le 2n \ln n + O(n)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A common error is to bound $\Pr(X_{ij} = 1)$ as $1/n$ instead of $2/(j-i+1)$. The correct probability depends on the *relative* ranks of the two elements, not on the absolute subarray size. Using the wrong probability leads to an overcounted bound of $O(n^2)$ — losing **4 marks** in a typical valuation.

> **Use this as the last 60 seconds of revision before walking into the exam hall.**

- [x] **Randomized quicksort** = standard quicksort + uniform random pivot selection before each partition.
- [x] The **only** modification is the swap of $A[r]$ with a random $A[i]$ in $[p, r]$; the PARTITION routine is reused unchanged.
- [x] **Best case:** $O(n \log n)$ — pivot always splits the array in half.
- [x] **Expected case:** $O(n \log n)$ — guaranteed for *any* input, since the expectation is over the algorithm's coin flips, not over inputs.
- [x] **Worst case:** $O(n^2)$ — possible but with probability vanishingly small (bounded by $2/n!$).
- [x] **Indicator random variable** $X_k = 1$ if the pivot is the $k$-th smallest; $\Pr(X_k = 1) = 1/n$.
- [x] **Master recurrence:** $E[T(n)] = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} E[T(k)] + \Theta(n)$.
- [x] **Substitution solution:** $E[T(n)] = O(n \log n)$; equivalently bounded by $2n \ln n + O(n)$ comparisons.
- [x] **Space complexity:** $\Theta(\log n)$ expected recursion stack; $\Theta(n)$ worst case.
- [x] **Engineering use:** `std::sort` in C++, Java's primitive-type sorts (Dual-Pivot Quicksort variant), and database query engines all rely on randomized quicksort.
- [x] **Key difference from deterministic QS:** Randomized QS's $O(n \log n)$ guarantee is *unconditional* on input distribution; deterministic QS's is *conditional* on the input not being adversarial.
- [x] **Independent randomness property:** The pivot choice at the current recursive level is statistically independent of the random choices made at deeper levels — this is what makes the linearity-of-expectation step in the analysis legal.
- [x] **Comparison bound formula:** $E[\text{comparisons}] \le 2n \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k} \le 2n \ln n + 2n$.
